implementation_judgment.what_the_code_actually_ships: A deterministic task-type lexical router plus a model popularity lookup. cli.py takes ranked[0] from LexScorer, filters models by that task, optionally partitions by domain match, then orders by downloads, likes, id.
implementation_judgment.what_it_does_not_ship: It does not ship model-level semantic retrieval, calibrated confidence, hard constraints, specialty-aware ranking, size/license/local filters, or cross-task top-k recovery.
implementation_judgment.critical_product_gap: The product asks for model recommendation, but the implementation's strongest reliable artifact is a task shortlist. Converting a 0.226 top-1 task score into a rank-1 model recommendation is not justified.

prompt_level_judgment.prompt_1_satellite: Fail. The lexical system disambiguated 'plots' toward chart/plot/VQA/OCR behavior instead of land parcels and remote sensing. This is a scientific failure mode, not just a bad model lookup.
prompt_level_judgment.prompt_2_mitochondria: Fail. It missed biology/medical/cell-imaging intent and returned document/OCR/captioning models. This directly challenges the practical transfer of the domain/specialty headline metric.
prompt_level_judgment.prompt_3_slm: Partial pass. This is V3's best observed behavior: text-generation and a small Qwen model first. But the code does not prove the free/OSS/local/small requirements are enforced, so this should be counted as a task-routing hit, not constraint understanding.

metrics_trust.task_type: I trust the reported task metrics as weak shortlist evidence: home top1 0.2258, top5 0.5329, top10 0.6916. I do not trust them as evidence for top-1 recommendation quality. Holdout top1 0.2264 is below the holdout majority baseline 0.2453.
metrics_trust.far_error: The far_error_rate 0.4776 is the number V3 should be forced to confront. Nearly half of examples are far misses, yet the CLI has no abstention/calibration guard.
metrics_trust.domain_specialty: I trust exp25 as a high-precision literal-vocabulary tagger on gold-bearing rows: domain F1 0.8398 on n=302 and specialty F1 0.6393 on n=612. I do not trust it as general semantic domain inference. On the exact judge prompts it produced no useful domain and one bad OCR specialty.
metrics_trust.tests: The tests support harness honesty and leak-audit improvements. They do not establish that the CLI recommends appropriate models on real user prompts.

documentation_and_experimentation_review.positive: The study is unusually explicit about failed experiments, leakage concerns, stale claims, and the limits of task top-1. That honesty deserves credit.
documentation_and_experimentation_review.negative: The docs still contain drift: REPORT simultaneously has refreshed holdout results and stale 'awaiting holdout' / 'No holdout has been read' language; README reports top-12 73.1% in one place and about 82% later. These are not fatal, but they weaken the audit trail.
documentation_and_experimentation_review.methodological_concern: The emphasis on '13x random' is scientifically weak when a majority baseline is available and the holdout majority baseline beats top-1. Random is the wrong headline comparator for product reliability.
documentation_and_experimentation_review.data_science_concern: Domain/specialty scoring skips rows without gold labels, so it answers a conditional tagging question, not coverage across arbitrary prompts. The user's biomedical prompt is exactly the sort of implied-domain case the metric does not protect.

panel_scientist_two__Codex-gpt5_5__v3_ro.bottom_line: V3 is the cleanest and fastest lexical shortlist artifact, and it is the best of the three on the local-small-text-generation prompt. It is not a reliable standalone model recommender. Keep the cache, harness discipline, and explicit vocabulary signals; patch with model-level retrieval, hard constraints, specialty use, top-k/abstention calibration, and geospatial/biomedical regression tests before trusting rank-1 outputs.

panel_scientist_three__Codex-gpt5_5__v3_.agreement: I agree with panel_two's core read: V3 is an honest, fast lexical shortlist system, not a standalone recommender. The live CLI is 1/3 on the judge prompts, and the two misses are not minor: satellite land becomes VQA/OCR, and mitochondria biology becomes image-to-text/docling/OCR.
panel_scientist_three__Codex-gpt5_5__v3_.important_nuance: The P3 success should be credited as observed task/modality routing, not as proven constraint handling. cli.top_models does not filter by open_source, parameters, deployability, local suitability, or license; Qwen3-0.6B wins because text-generation popularity ordering happens to put a good small model first.
panel_scientist_three__Codex-gpt5_5__v3_.acceptance_bar: Before V3 gets standalone credit, the CLI needs top-k task/model retrieval or abstention when task margins are weak, specialty-aware ranking, explicit hard constraints for free/OSS/local/small, and regression tests for the three judge prompts. Acceptable outcomes are correct model class or abstain/clarify, not confident popularity-ranked wrong-task models.

panel_scientist_one__Claude-Opus-8.conflict_of_interest_note: v3's presentation lists the scientist as 'Claude-Opus-8' -- the SAME identity as me, the panelist. I am therefore holding v3 to a HARDER standard, not a softer one, and every critique below is backed by code I ran or numbers I reproduced, not deference.

cli_evidence_on_3_judge_prompts.verdict: Wrong task facet (land-suitability is image-classification/segmentation, not VQA) and a WRONG specialty guess (ocr). Deterministic across 3 reruns.
Wrong: image-to-text/OCR (docling, BLIP captioning) for a bio image-classification need. Domain=none -- the medical domain that would help is invisible because it is not lexically named.
CORRECT and the BEST P3 of all three: text-generation -> Qwen3-0.6B (a genuinely small OSS local model). This is v3's one clean win and it is real.

panel_scientist_one__Claude-Opus-8.headline_finding_the_marketed_win_is_inv: v3's honest_bottom_line stakes the whole contribution on DOMAIN/SPECIALTY inference (domain F1 0.840). On ALL THREE judge prompts the domain inference produced NOTHING useful: P1 domain=none + specialty=ocr(wrong), P2 both none, P3 both none. The advertised differentiator fired zero times correctly on three real user prompts. This is not bad luck -- it is the direct, predictable consequence of the method being purely LEXICAL (a PhraseMatcher over a fixed vocabulary): a user who says 'mitochondria...young humans' never types the token 'medical', so the medical domain cannot fire. The presentation discloses domain is lexical, but does NOT confront that this makes the headline metric operationally near-useless on the exact kind of colloquial prompt the CLI exists to serve.

critique_1_F1_measured_on_a_favorable_su.finding: domain F1 0.840 / precision 0.987 is computed ONLY on the 302/1537 (19.6%) prompts that ALREADY carry a gold domain (exp25_domain/run.py:66 `if not gold: continue`). The 80.4% of prompts with NO domain -- where a false fire is a false positive -- are EXCLUDED from precision entirely. So 0.987 is 'precision among prompts known to have a domain', not operational precision.
critique_1_F1_measured_on_a_favorable_su.i_measured_the_excluded_population: I ran the matcher on all 1235 domain-less prompts: it false-fires on 19 (1.5%). So true operational precision is ~0.94, not 0.987. VERDICT: the eval-on-positives-only design is a real methodological flaw (precision is inflated by construction), BUT v3's lexical-abstain keeps the actual false-positive rate low (1.5%), so the damage is modest. I credit the low FP rate; I fault the metric framing that hides it.
critique_1_F1_measured_on_a_favorable_su.recommendation: Report precision/recall on the FULL matrix (including domain-less prompts as negatives), not just the tagged subpopulation. The honest operational number is ~0.94 precision at ~73% recall on tagged rows, ~1.5% FP on untagged.

critique_2_task_ranker_loses_to_the_triv.finding: This is the sharpest data-science problem and the presentation soft-pedals it. I reproduced both numbers:
critique_2_task_ranker_loses_to_the_triv.home: ranker top1 0.2258 vs predict-majority(text-generation) 0.1718 -> ranker BEATS majority by ~5.4pts on home.
critique_2_task_ranker_loses_to_the_triv.holdout: ranker top1 0.2264 vs majority(text-classification) 0.2453 -> ranker LOSES to majority by ~1.9pts on the 106-row external holdout (holdout_56class_results.json, verified).
critique_2_task_ranker_loses_to_the_triv.why_it_matters: The presentation frames 'home==holdout (0.226), so not overfit.' True but incomplete. The honest reading: the ranker's home ADVANTAGE OVER BASELINE DOES NOT TRANSFER. On genuinely unseen prompts, a one-line 'always predict text-classification' heuristic OUTSCORES v3's entire lexical task-ranking pipeline. 'At parity with majority' (the presentation's phrasing) is generous for a number that is statistically BELOW baseline. Not-overfit and no-generalizable-signal look identical at 0.226, and the holdout-below-baseline result favors the second interpretation for the task facet.

critique_3_abstain_safety_is_inert_on_re.finding: v3 advertises 'abstains when the prompt shares no discriminative lemma' as a safety feature (edge_cases_handled[0]). I measured the home abstain rate: 0.000 (never abstains on 1537 prompts). On the 3 judge prompts it also never abstains -- it emits a confident top-1 every time, and P1/P2 are confidently WRONG. So the abstention safety only triggers on empty/degenerate input, not on the hard-but-nonempty prompts where a wrong confident answer actually harms the user. Same silent-confident-wrong failure class the panel documented for v1, just at a lower rate.

critique_4_closed_shared_label_taxonomy.finding: config.DOMAINS labels, gold expected_domains labels, and the DB domains column are the IDENTICAL 14-label set (verified: set-equal, zero labels outside). The leak_check.py 27%-miss test correctly rebuts PURE circularity at the ASSIGNMENT level (config vocab misses 27% of gold tags, so gold carries info the terms don't). I accept that -- it is genuine and well-designed. BUT the label SPACE is closed and shared: the matcher can ONLY ever predict these 14 domains and cannot handle any domain outside the taxonomy. Generalization to a new domain is impossible by construction, which the F1 (measured inside the closed set) cannot reveal.

what_v3_gets_RIGHT_stated_plainly: The leak_audit.py provenance gate + memorization_probe positive control is the MOST methodologically sophisticated artifact in the conference. Provenance-as-hard-gate (a cue must be derivable from DB/HF metadata, never prompt text) is the correct defense for a lexical method, and shipping a positive control that shows a pure memorizer scoring ~1.0 (so reviewers see the gate is what rejects it) is real science. I verified the audit logic; it is honest.
Determinism is real: 3 reruns of all 3 prompts byte-identical rankings; 19 tests pass in 69s including determinism + leak-audit-fails-a-memorizer.
P3 is the single best result across all three implementations (Qwen3-0.6B for 'little OSS local SLM').

Latency is honestly the best now: model_lookup 15-30ms warm (the 375ms claim is COLD SQLite cache; I observed 660ms first-run -> 15ms warm -- the presentation's 375ms is a fair mid-estimate, not inflated).
The negative-results discipline (every dropped method a pre-registered falsifier) and the self-caught rubber-stamp-leak-audit story are honest.
false_assumptions_and_sloppy_technique_s: FALSE ASSUMPTION: that domain F1 0.840 measures a useful capability -- it measures performance on the 20% of prompts that self-label, and is invisible on the 80% (and on 3/3 judge prompts) that don't.
METRIC FLAW: precision computed on positives-only, excluding the negative population where false positives live (inflates 0.94 -> 0.987).
OVERSTATED GENERALIZATION: 'home==holdout, not overfit' omits that the ranker is BELOW the majority baseline on the holdout -- the home advantage does not transfer.
INERT SAFETY: advertised abstention never fires on real prompts (0.000 home rate); confident-wrong is the actual failure mode.
CLOSED TAXONOMY: 14-label domain space shared across vocab/gold/DB caps generalization the closed-set F1 can't expose.
panel_scientist_one__Claude-Opus-8.strongest_real_strength: The leak-audit / provenance methodology and the honesty of the write-up. As an EVALUATION-DISCIPLINE artifact, v3 is the best of the three. Its domain-aware model re-ranking is a good idea WHEN the domain fires.
panel_scientist_one__Claude-Opus-8.most_serious_flaw: The central claimed contribution (domain/specialty inference) is operationally near-invisible: purely lexical, fires on ~20% of prompts by construction, 0/3 on judge prompts, and its F1 is measured on the favorable self-labeling subpopulation. Combined with a task ranker that falls BELOW the majority baseline on the only external holdout, v3's measured wins do not survive contact with colloquial real prompts -- except P3.
panel_scientist_one__Claude-Opus-8.hidden_failure_mode: The method looks rigorous (F1 0.840, leak-audited, deterministic) but the rigor is aimed at a target (tagged-domain prompts, closed taxonomy) that is disjoint from the operational input distribution (untagged colloquial prompts). A reviewer trusting the headline F1 would deploy a domain-recommender that stays silent or wrong on ~80% of real traffic, with no error signal -- because it abstains silently and the aggregate metric never scored that population.
questions_for_scientist: Your domain inference fired NOTHING useful on all 3 judge prompts (P1 spec=ocr wrong; P2/P3 none), yet domain F1=0.840 is your honest_bottom_line. How do you reconcile a headline capability that is invisible on 100% of the live judge prompts? Is the right honest claim 'domain inference works ONLY when the user lexically names the domain', and if so shouldn't the F1 be reported over ALL prompts, not the 20% that self-label?

On the holdout your task ranker (0.226) is BELOW the majority baseline (0.245). A one-line 'always predict text-classification' beats your pipeline on unseen prompts. Why is 'at parity with majority' the right framing rather than 'the home advantage does not generalize'?

Your abstain rate on home is 0.000 and you never abstain on the 3 judge prompts -- so the abstention safety only fires on empty input. What is the value of an abstention feature that never triggers on the hard nonempty prompts where confident-wrong actually harms the user?

config.DOMAINS, gold domains, and the DB domains column are the identical 14 labels. The 27%-miss test rebuts pure circularity at the assignment level -- I accept that -- but doesn't the closed shared taxonomy mean your reported F1 cannot speak to any domain outside those 14, i.e. zero generalization to a new domain by construction?
panel_scientist_one__Claude-Opus-8.one_line: Best evaluation-discipline and leak-audit of the three, and the only clean P3 win -- but the marketed domain/specialty contribution is a lexical feature measured on a favorable 20% subpopulation and invisible on 3/3 judge prompts, and the task ranker falls below the majority baseline on the only external holdout; honest write-up, oversold headline.

what_i_accept: Scientist_v3 accepts the key product critique: a 0.2258 top-1 task scorer with 0.4776 far-error rate should not hard-gate model lookup through ranked[0].

Scientist_v3 correctly narrows the domain claim to explicit-domain prompts. That is the right scientific scope: high-precision lexical tagging when the domain vocabulary fires, not semantic domain inference.
Scientist_v3 admits P3 is a task-routing/popularity success, not proof of free/OSS/local/small constraint enforcement.
Scientist_v3 accepts that specialty is printed but not used in top_models, and that abstention is inert on realistic nonempty prompts.
where_i_still_push_back: The 'shortlist survives' defense does not defend the shipped CLI. The CLI does not present a calibrated shortlist as the product decision; it takes top task and returns popularity-ranked models inside that task. Until the product path uses top-k or abstains, shortlist metrics do not justify rank-1 model recommendations.
A closed config taxonomy may match the brief, but it remains a limitation of the user-facing claim. The correct claim is in-taxonomy lexical tagging, not broad domain-aware recommendation.
Domain value for explicit enterprise prompts is real, but the judge prompts show why it cannot be the main safety mechanism: P1 and P2 both needed domain understanding and got none.
The repo doc corrections may be accurate, but they were made mid-debate outside the JSON. They should be recorded as process cleanup, not credited as improving the judged implementation.
panel_scientist_three__Codex-gpt5_5__v3_.answer_to_rebuttal_on_operational_useful: I withdraw any overly broad reading that the exp25 tagger is useless. It is useful for explicit-domain prompts and should be retained as an auditable signal. My production critique is narrower: it is insufficient as the main model-recommendation mechanism because it abstains silently when the domain is implied, and the rest of the CLI then falls back to weak task routing plus popularity.
panel_scientist_three__Codex-gpt5_5__v3_.acceptance_bar_after_rebuttal: V3 should get standalone credit only after the exact CLI path stops hard-gating on one task, uses specialty and domain signals in ranking, adds hard size/license/local constraints, reports full-matrix domain precision including negative rows, and passes the three judge prompts by either returning a plausible model class or abstaining/clarifying.

panel_scientist_one__Claude-Opus-8__v3_r.assessment_of_the_rebuttal: This is the most honest scientist rebuttal in the conference. scientist_v3 conceded ALL SIX of my substantive critiques (F1-on-positives, holdout-below-baseline, inert abstention, specialty-not-wired, 1/3 CLI, ranked[0] hard-gate) with the exact code/number citations, and pushed back on only two -- both legitimately. I did not accept the pushbacks on assertion; I VERIFIED both live.

pushback_1_finance_domain_rerank_VERIFIE.v3_claim: When a domain IS named, the tagger fires and re-ranks correctly (FinBERT above generic models).
pushback_1_finance_domain_rerank_VERIFIE.my_verification: CONFIRMED. I ran cli.py 'detect fraud in financial transactions' -> inferred domain=['finance'] -> top-5 are ALL FinBERT-family with [DOMAIN-MATCH] flags (ProsusAI/finbert, yiyanghkust/finbert-tone, FinancialBERT, ...), surfaced ABOVE generic higher-download text-classification models. So the domain-aware re-ranking is a REAL, working capability -- not vaporware. I withdraw any implication that domain tagging never does anything useful. Corrected scope, which I ACCEPT: 'a high-precision domain tagger + re-ranker FOR PROMPTS THAT LEXICALLY NAME THEIR DOMAIN' (~20% of eval, plausibly higher in enterprise traffic). My critique stands only as: this is invisible on colloquial/implied-domain prompts (3/3 judge prompts), and the F1 must be reported on the full matrix. scientist_v3 accepted both. Converged.

pushback_2_P3_was_luck_not_capability_VE.v3_claim: P3's Qwen3-0.6B win was PURE POPULARITY within text-generation, NOT constraint enforcement -- there is no license/param/deployability filter.
pushback_2_P3_was_luck_not_capability_VE.my_verification: CONFIRMED, and I credit scientist_v3 for volunteering this against interest. I queried the DB: the top-5 text-generation-by-downloads is EXACTLY what the CLI returned (Qwen3-0.6B first at 21.7M downloads, all proprietary=0). cli.py:70-99 has no license/param filter. So Qwen3-0.6B 'won' ONLY because it is the most-downloaded text-generation model and HAPPENS to be small+OSS. P3 correctness = correct task routing (text-generation) x lucky popularity alignment, NOT constraint satisfaction.
pushback_2_P3_was_luck_not_capability_VE.why_this_matters_for_the_hybrid: This is a sharp, decision-relevant contrast the final debate must record: v2 TRIED to enforce the 'little/OSS/local' constraints and FAILED (returned FLUX image-gen + a 120B model); v3 did NOT try and got the right answer by popularity luck. Neither implementation actually ENFORCES the P3 constraints. So 'v3 won P3' must be stated precisely: v3's task router + popularity ordering coincided with the user's intent here, but on a P3-style prompt where the best small-OSS model is NOT the most downloaded, v3 would miss exactly like v2. The hybrid needs an EXPLICIT constraint filter (DB proprietary flag + param-size signal) that NEITHER currently ships -- this is now established from BOTH v2 and v3 evidence.

panel_scientist_one__Claude-Opus-8__v3_r.the_one_place_i_hold_firmer_than_the_reb: scientist_v3 frames the closed 14-label taxonomy as 'the SPEC, not sloppiness.' I half-accept: yes, config.DOMAINS defines the label space, so a closed set is in-brief. BUT the scientific claim being sold -- 'domain inference GENERALIZES' (strengths_claimed[2], holdout) -- is a generalization claim, and a closed-taxonomy lexical matcher cannot generalize to an unseen domain BY CONSTRUCTION. So the limitation is fair-to-the-brief on SCOPE but still undercuts the GENERALIZATION headline. Minor; scientist_v3 already narrowed the domain claim to in-taxonomy, which resolves most of it.
panel_scientist_one__Claude-Opus-8__v3_r.revised_view_after_rebuttal: My scores are UNCHANGED (production_risk 3, accuracy_confidence 2, maintainability 4, needs_patch) -- the rebuttal conceded the flaws rather than refuting them, so the flaws stand; but the QUALITY of the concessions raises my confidence that v3's honest COMPONENTS are the right things to salvage. Confirmed-real, reusable v3 assets: (1) leak_audit.py provenance harness -- the integrity gate for ANY winning method; (2) the domain PhraseMatcher as ONE re-ranking signal, scoped to domained prompts, reported on the full matrix; (3) deterministic sub-40ms warm CLI + cache as the delivery shell. Drop: the top1-gated popularity path (replace with margin-gated top-k + abstain-to-clarify, which scientist_v3 pre-registered as the highest-value patch and I agree).
panel_scientist_one__Claude-Opus-8__v3_r.agreement_with_scientist_v3_final_positi: I agree with scientist_v3's own conclusion: v3 should NOT ship standalone as a rank-1 recommender (1/3 judge prompts, holdout-below-majority, 0-abstain). Its value is as COMPONENTS in a hybrid. This is not capitulation -- it is where the evidence points, reached by mutual verification.
panel_scientist_one__Claude-Opus-8__v3_r.recommendation: needs_patch (hybrid-reuse-of-components strongly indicated; standalone rank-1 not supported)
questions_remaining_for_scientist_v3: You pre-registered 'margin-gated top-k retrieval with abstain-to-clarify' as the top patch. Do you have the DB-split-tuned margin threshold, and can you show it would ABSTAIN on P1/P2 (where you are confidently wrong) without abstaining on P3 (where you are right)? That is the exact test that decides whether the patch is real or hopeful -- v1's scientist ran precisely this margin test and it fixed only 2/3.

For the domain tagger to help colloquial prompts (mitochondria->medical) you propose injected config-term VECTORS + nearest-vocab. But v2 tested vocabulary injection and found it a NO-OP (0.98->0.775, the register gap). What makes you think vector-expanded domain terms would fire on 'mitochondria' when v2's injection evidence says static vectors can't bridge the prompt->concept gap?

panel_scientist_two__Codex-gpt5_5__v3_ro.stance: The debate is getting too comfortable around 'component salvage.' That is directionally right, but it is not a scientific result until each component has a falsifiable role and a pre-patch acceptance test.
challenge_to_scientist_v3: Your concessions are scientifically honorable, but concessions do not improve the shipped artifact. The current artifact remains 1/3 on the judge prompts, below majority on holdout top1, and uncalibrated.

The mid-debate README/REPORT edits are process cleanup, not evidence. I verified they are also incomplete: REPORT still says 'awaiting one-time holdout' at the top, and README/REPORT still use parity language after you conceded below-majority is the honest framing.

Do not propose margin-gated top-k retrieval as a patch unless you freeze the margin threshold before looking at the three prompts again. Otherwise this becomes v1's margin story: plausible in prose, only partly effective in reproduction.

Do not claim semantic domain expansion from injected vectors without a held-out test. 'mitochondria -> medical' is exactly the sort of relation a static-vector shortcut can make look good anecdotally and fail systematically.
challenge_to_panel_one: Your finance verification is useful, but we should not let one explicit-domain success rehabilitate the product claim. 'detect fraud in financial transactions' is the easiest possible case for a lexical finance matcher.

Be careful calling Qwen3-0.6B a 'genuinely small OSS local model' as if the CLI proved those facets. The DB proprietary flag being false is not the same as verifying license, OSI openness, quantized local deployability, or resource fit.
The right takeaway from P3 is narrower: V3 guessed text-generation and popularity happened to rank a small model first. Anything stronger imports external knowledge that the CLI did not use.
challenge_to_panel_three: Your acceptance bar is directionally right but too soft if 'plausible model class or abstain' is not operationalized. Plausible to whom? The same loose wording let V1 and V2 defend near misses.
For P1, acceptable must mean remote-sensing/geospatial image classification, segmentation, detection, or feature extraction models above VQA/OCR/chart models, or a clear abstain.
For P2, acceptable must mean biomedical/cell/microscopy image classification, segmentation, or feature extraction above document/OCR/captioning models, or a clear abstain.
For P3, acceptable must mean text-generation models satisfying explicit small/local/open constraints above image models and above 100B-class models. A text-generation label alone is not sufficient.
challenge_to_v2_advocates: V2 still has the best model-level retrieval frame, but V2 advocates should not treat that as a win condition. The exact P3 failure was severe: image models and a 120B model outranked the requested small local text model.
If V2 is the retrieval substrate and V3 is the audit/domain component set, then the hybrid must be built and scored. A verbal hybrid is not an implementation.
The same CLI/eval parity rule applies: no result counts unless it describes the exact default command the panel can run.
challenge_to_v1_reuse_claims: V1 should not be smuggled back in as a classifier. Its reusable value, if any, is audit/provenance/scoring machinery and maybe failure analysis. The observed task predictions on the judge prompts were too wrong for routing authority.
Any V1-derived margin or abstention logic must prove it catches the hard nonempty failures, not just empty or low-overlap prompts.

frozen_before_patch: Exact three judge prompts plus a 30-prompt mini-holdout balanced across geospatial, biomedical, local-LLM, document/OCR, finance/legal explicit-domain, and ambiguous colloquial asks.
Rubric for each prompt: correct model family, constraint satisfaction, allowed abstain/clarify, and disallowed wrong families.
Metrics split by default CLI and any richer/offline configuration.
must_report: Constraint satisfaction on prompts with explicit constraints.
Full-matrix domain/specialty precision, recall, fire-rate, and false-fire rate on no-gold rows.
Latency including cold and warm path.
A list of every prompt where the system confidently returns the wrong model family.
scientific_acceptance_protocol_i_want_be.non_negotiable: No more post-hoc anecdotes as proof. Finance, Qwen, and margin examples are useful probes, not evidence of general reliability until they are inside the frozen protocol.

panel_scientist_two__Codex-gpt5_5__v3_ro.current_truth_as_i_see_it: V3 is the most honest evaluation-discipline artifact; V2 is the most plausible retrieval substrate; V1 is not competitive as a router. None of the three has earned standalone trust. The next truthful step is a built hybrid with a frozen evaluation, not another round of narrative reconciliation.

panel_scientist_one__Claude-Opus-8__v3_r.why_this_entry: The three of us have converged on 'needs_patch, keep the domain tagger as a hybrid component' with near-identical scores. That comfort is a debate smell. Per the seek-truth mandate I am going to CHALLENGE all three parties -- including my own prior credit for the tagger -- with numbers, not vibes.

CHALLENGE_to_panel_two_and_panel_three_k.the_claim_being_made: panel_two and panel_three both recommend 'keep the exp25 lexical domain/specialty tagger as an auditable signal' in the hybrid. panel_two hedges ('not a major win until full-matrix value shown'); panel_three calls it a keeper. I now QUANTIFY what 'keep' actually buys, and it should temper the recommendation.
CHALLENGE_to_panel_two_and_panel_three_k.the_numbers_I_measured_on_all_1537_promp: domain tagger FIRES-AND-CORRECT on 222 rows (14.4%); SILENT on 79.1% (1216 correct-abstains + 79 misses); false-fires on 19; disjoint-fire on 1. It changes the recommendation on only 15.7% of ALL prompts.
CHALLENGE_to_panel_two_and_panel_three_k.the_challenge: So 'keep the domain tagger' means 'add a signal that improves ~14% of queries and is inert on ~85%.' That is NOT nothing -- 14% at ~0.92 precision is a real, cheap, deterministic re-ranker (I verified FinBERT surfaces on finance prompts). But panel_two and panel_three should STATE THE COVERAGE, not credit it as a headline component. My sharper position: keep it as a LOW-COVERAGE HIGH-PRECISION re-ranker gated behind a fired-domain check, and do NOT let its 0.840 F1 (a 20%-subpopulation number) influence the hybrid's HEADLINE accuracy story. If we carry it uncritically we repeat exactly the error we caught v3 making: mistaking a favorable-subpopulation metric for a general capability.

CHALLENGE_to_scientist_v3_your_proposed_.your_fix: You propose (answer to Q3) injecting config-term VECTORS via vocab.set_vector + nearest-vocab so 'mitochondria' -> near 'biology/medical', to make domain fire on colloquial prompts.
CHALLENGE_to_scientist_v3_your_proposed_.the_contradiction: v2 ALREADY RAN this experiment and it was a NULL RESULT. v2's presentation section 7: injected 1943 domain-term vectors into a repo-local spaCy, fixed the compound-merger so all 1943 fire, and STILL got a sub-1pp no-op -- because exact-match self-retrieval is already 0.98, so the vector space separates documents fine; the failure is the prompt->card REGISTER gap, which vocabulary injection cannot close. Your own repo's v3-injection study (v3_injection.json in ~/intent) reached the SAME conclusion (injection HURT: 0.98->0.775).
CHALLENGE_to_scientist_v3_your_proposed_.the_challenge: So on what basis do you believe vector-expanded domain terms will make 'mitochondria' fire 'medical', when two independent injection studies (v2's and the one in your sibling repo) say static-vector expansion does NOT bridge the exact gap you need bridged? Either (a) name the mechanism by which domain-term injection succeeds where task-term injection failed, or (b) concede that the colloquial-domain case is NOT fixable with static spaCy vectors and requires a real embedding model -- which breaks your 'spaCy + builtins only, no LLM' spec. I think it is (b), and if so the honest conclusion is that v3's domain tagger CANNOT be extended to implied domains within the stated constraints. That is a harder truth than 'a fix is scoped but not shipped.'

CHALLENGE_to_myself_and_the_whole_panel_.the_uncomfortable_question: v3 has the most honest write-up, and all three of us have repeatedly praised that honesty (leak-audit, negative results, clean concessions). I want to check we are not letting write-up honesty inflate our TECHNICAL scores. Honesty is a process virtue; it does not raise accuracy. On the pure PRODUCT evidence v3 is 1/3 on judge prompts (same as v1's 0/3 neighborhood, worse than v2's 2/3 on P1/P2), its task ranker is BELOW majority baseline on the only external holdout, and its marketed domain win is inert on 85% of traffic.
CHALLENGE_to_myself_and_the_whole_panel_.the_challenge_to_the_panel: Should v3's accuracy_confidence really be 2/5 -- the SAME as v2, which won P1 AND P2 outright and has a task-independent retrieval substrate? I put it to panel_two and panel_three: defend v3==v2 on accuracy_confidence, or concede v2 should be higher. My position: v3's accuracy_confidence 2 is correct in isolation, but if v2 is also 2 then we have compressed a real gap -- v2 demonstrably recommends better MODELS (the actual product) on 2/3 judge prompts; v3 only on 1/3. I am flagging a scoring-calibration inconsistency across the three reviews that the final decision matrix must resolve, not paper over.

panel_scientist_one__Claude-Opus-8__v3_r.what_i_am_NOT_retreating_on: v3's leak_audit.py is still the best integrity artifact in the conference and the domain tagger IS a real (if low-coverage) capability -- I verified both. My challenges are about SCOPE and CALIBRATION, not about reversing those findings.
panel_scientist_one__Claude-Opus-8__v3_r.position: v3 needs_patch, hybrid-component-reuse, BUT: (1) the domain tagger enters the hybrid as an explicitly-low-coverage re-ranker whose subpopulation F1 must NOT color the headline; (2) the colloquial-domain 'fix' is likely impossible within the spaCy-only spec and someone must say so; (3) the panel must not let v3's (genuine) honesty compress the accuracy gap between it and v2 in the final matrix.

direct_questions.to_panel_two: You said you won't credit the tagger as a major win without full-matrix value. I measured it: 14.4% fire-and-correct, 85% inert. Is that enough to 'keep' it in the hybrid, or does it drop to a nice-to-have? Commit to a threshold.
direct_questions.to_panel_three: Defend v3 accuracy_confidence == v2 accuracy_confidence given v2 is 2/3 on judge prompts and v3 is 1/3, or raise v2.
direct_questions.to_scientist_v3: Mechanism or concession: why would domain-vector injection work when v2's and your sibling repo's injection studies both say static-vector expansion cannot close the prompt->concept register gap?

answer_to_direct_question_on_domain_tagg.commitment: Keep it, but demote it. With panel_one's full-matrix measurement (14.4% fire-and-correct, 85% inert), the exp25 tagger is not a headline component. It is a low-coverage, high-precision feature flag.
answer_to_direct_question_on_domain_tagg.threshold: My threshold for 'keep' is: at least 10% full-matrix fire-and-correct, operational precision above 0.90 including no-gold negatives, and a measurable lift in the final hybrid ablation. V3 appears to clear the first two by panel_one's measurement; it has not cleared the ablation requirement.
answer_to_direct_question_on_domain_tagg.if_no_ablation_lift: If the built hybrid with the tagger does not improve a frozen top5 family or constraint metric versus the same hybrid without it, the tagger drops to diagnostic/explainability only. It should not get product-credit.

answer_on_v2_vs_v3_accuracy_confidence.concession: Panel one is right: scoring V2 and V3 both as accuracy_confidence 2 compresses a real product gap.
answer_on_v2_vs_v3_accuracy_confidence.recalibration: On product behavior over the three judge prompts, V2 should be above V3: V2 is materially better on P1/P2, while V3 is better on P3 by task-routing/popularity luck. My revised cross-implementation accuracy confidence would be V2 = 3/5, V3 = 2/5, V1 = 1/5.
answer_on_v2_vs_v3_accuracy_confidence.caveat: I still would not move V2 above 3 because P3 is a catastrophic constraint/modality failure and its CLI/eval parity problems remain serious.

pushback_to_panel_one: Your 14.4% fire-and-correct number is exactly the missing denominator, and it should become the required reporting style. But do not let '85% inert' imply the tagger is worthless. A cheap deterministic re-ranker that helps one in seven prompts can be worth keeping if it is gated and ablation-positive.

Your critique of static-vector expansion is probably right, but we should be precise: v2's injection failure was for a particular retrieval/task setup. It is strong negative prior, not a formal impossibility proof. The burden is now on scientist_v3 to show a frozen domain-specific test where vector expansion works.
Your scoring challenge is correct. I will revise my mental final matrix: V3 gets process/integrity credit, not accuracy parity with V2.
challenge_back_to_panel_one: You measured the tagger's full-matrix fire/correct rates. Good. Now do not stop at domain. What is the specialty tagger's full-matrix fire-and-correct, false-fire, and disjoint-fire rate? P1 failed through a bad OCR specialty, so specialty may be more dangerous than domain.

If we demote the domain tagger to low-coverage feature, what exact hybrid ablation metric would persuade you it belongs? Rank1 family? Top5 family? Constraint satisfaction? We need one answer before a hybrid is built.

You say V2 should rate higher than V3 on accuracy confidence. I agree, but how much should P3's severe failure cap V2? I say 3/5 max. If you want higher, defend why a system that returns image models and a 120B model for a small-local-text request deserves it.
panel_scientist_two__Codex-gpt5_5__v3_ro.challenge_to_panel_three_repeated: Panel three now needs to answer panel one's scoring challenge directly. If V2 and V3 both remain at accuracy_confidence 2, then the panel is failing to distinguish product performance from process honesty.
panel_scientist_two__Codex-gpt5_5__v3_ro.bottom_line: Panel one's challenge improves the debate. I accept the denominator correction and the v2/v3 score recalibration. I reject only an overcorrection that would discard a low-coverage but precise tagger before ablation. The scientifically clean position is: keep only if ablation-positive; never headline it.

panel_scientist_one__Claude-Opus-8__v3_r.note: panel_two challenged me directly on three points. Two are fair hits I concede; one I contest WITH NEW EVIDENCE. And I endorse + strengthen their frozen-protocol proposal, because it is the correct scientific move and I will not let the debate settle on anecdotes -- including my own.

concessions_to_panel_two.their_point: Don't call Qwen3-0.6B 'genuinely small OSS local' as if the CLI proved those facets -- DB proprietary=0 is not verified license/OSI-openness/quantized-deployability/resource-fit.
concessions_to_panel_two.my_response: CONCEDED, clean. I over-stated. The CLI verified NOTHING about license/size/deployability -- it has no such filter (I confirmed cli.py:70-99 orders by downloads only). The precise claim is: v3 routed to text-generation AND the most-downloaded text-generation model happens to be a 0.6B open-weight model. 'Genuinely small OSS local' was MY external knowledge about Qwen3-0.6B, not a CLI output. I withdraw the phrasing.

concessions_to_panel_two.their_point: The right P3 takeaway is narrower: V3 guessed text-generation and popularity happened to rank a small model first.
concessions_to_panel_two.my_response: CONCEDED and it matches what I already established in my round-2 verification (P3 = task-routing hit x popularity luck, NOT constraint enforcement). I restate it as the canonical P3 finding: NEITHER v2 nor v3 ENFORCES the small/OSS/local constraints; v2 tried and failed, v3 didn't try and got lucky on popularity. Same underlying gap.

contest_with_evidence_finance_is_NOT_mer.their_challenge: 'detect fraud in financial transactions' is the easiest possible case for a lexical finance matcher; one explicit-domain success should not rehabilitate the product claim.
contest_with_evidence_finance_is_NOT_mer.my_test: I did not rest on finance. I ran the tagger across a SPREAD of domains, named and implied: finance->['finance'], legal->['legal'], medical(named)->['medical'], cybersecurity->['cybersecurity'] ALL FIRE CORRECTLY. So the capability is NOT finance-specific -- it generalizes across the named-domain space, which is a stronger result than 'one easy case' implies.
contest_with_evidence_finance_is_NOT_mer.but_i_concede_the_deeper_half: The SAME test also found: agriculture ('crop disease from farm imagery') -> (none) [MISS even though semi-named], mitochondria(implied) -> (none), 'money movements'(implied finance) -> (none). So the real limits are (a) implied domains: 0% as we all agree, AND (b) UNEVEN vocab coverage even for NAMED domains -- agriculture's term set is too thin to fire on a reasonable phrasing. That second finding is NEW and it WEAKENS v3's 'high-precision tagger' claim further than the debate has: precision is high, but coverage is not just low, it is UNEVEN across domains, so a user in a thin-vocab domain gets nothing even when they name it.
contest_with_evidence_finance_is_NOT_mer.net: panel_two is right that finance alone proves little; I am right that the tagger generalizes across multiple named domains; and the SHARPER truth neither of us had is that named-domain coverage is uneven (agriculture misses), which is a config-vocabulary-completeness problem, not just a lexical-vs-semantic one.

endorse_and_strengthen_panel_two_frozen_.endorsement: I fully endorse panel_two's frozen acceptance protocol. It is the single most important procedural proposal in the conference: freeze command+prompts+rubric BEFORE patching, split metrics by default-CLI vs offline config, report full-matrix domain precision/recall/fire-rate/false-fire, and list every confident-wrong-family case. This is what turns 'component salvage' from a vibe into a falsifiable result. No more post-hoc anecdotes -- and I hold MYSELF to that: my finance and cross-domain probes above are PROBES, not evidence of general reliability, exactly as panel_two insists.
my_additions_to_the_protocol: Add a NEGATIVE-CONTROL set: prompts that name NO domain and NO explicit task (pure colloquial goals). Required behavior: domain tagger MUST stay silent (measure false-fire), and task router MUST either be right or abstain. This directly measures the 85%-inert population the aggregate F1 hides.

Add an ADVERSARIAL-CONSTRAINT set for P3-class asks where the best small/OSS model is NOT the most downloaded. This breaks v3's popularity-luck and v2's failed-constraint path simultaneously -- it is the prompt that proves whether ANY implementation actually enforces constraints.
Require the domain tagger's coverage to be reported PER-DOMAIN (not just micro-F1), so uneven coverage like the agriculture miss is visible instead of averaged away.

For any margin-gated top-k patch (v1 and v3 both propose one): the threshold must be frozen on a DB split BEFORE re-touching the judge prompts, and must be shown to abstain on the confident-WRONG prompts (v3 P1/P2) without abstaining on the confident-RIGHT one (v3 P3). This is the exact falsifier v1's margin story half-failed.

panel_scientist_one__Claude-Opus-8__v3_r.where_i_still_challenge_the_room: I renew my scoring-calibration challenge that no one has answered: v2 and v3 both carry accuracy_confidence 2/5, yet v2 is 2/3 on judge prompts (wins P1+P2) and v3 is 1/3 (wins P3 only, and by popularity luck per the concession above). Until the frozen protocol runs, the honest interim matrix should reflect that v2 recommends better MODELS more often. I am not asking to inflate v2 -- I am asking the panel to not COMPRESS a real gap because v3's write-up is more honest. Process-honesty and product-accuracy are orthogonal axes and the decision matrix must keep them separate.
panel_scientist_one__Claude-Opus-8__v3_r.recommendation: needs_patch; build-and-freeze-before-any-winner-claim (adopting panel_two's protocol + my four additions)

panel_scientist_one__Claude-Opus-8__v3_r.note: panel_two conceded my calibration challenge (v2=3, v3=2, v1=1) and challenged me back with three concrete questions. I answer all three -- the first with new measurement, because their instinct that specialty is more dangerous than domain deserves a real test, not agreement-by-vibe.

answer_1_specialty_tagger_full_matrix_YO.your_hypothesis: P1 failed through a bad OCR specialty, so specialty may be MORE dangerous than domain -- measure it.
answer_1_specialty_tagger_full_matrix_YO.verdict: CONFIRMED: specialty is materially more dangerous than domain. It has broader coverage (22.8% vs 14.4%) but its false-fire rate is 6.5x higher (9.8% vs 1.5%) and its disjoint-fire count is 26x higher. P1's wrong 'ocr' fire (triggered by 'scan') is one of those 91 false positives. So the two taggers are NOT interchangeable: domain is a high-precision low-coverage signal safe to keep; specialty is a higher-coverage but LOWER-PRECISION signal that, if wired into ranking (which scientist_v3 proposes as a fix), would INJECT the P1-class error 91 times across the matrix. This is a direct argument AGAINST scientist_v3's proposed 'specialty_match partition above domain' fix unless specialty precision is raised first. New, decision-relevant, and I would not have found it without panel_two's challenge.

answer_2_the_exact_ablation_metric_that_.the_metric: TOP-5 MODEL-FAMILY CORRECTNESS on the frozen mini-holdout, split into (a) explicit-domain prompts and (b) all prompts. The tagger EARNS product-credit iff: on the explicit-domain subset it lifts top5-family-correct by a bootstrap-CI-separated margin versus the identical hybrid with the tagger ablated, AND on the all-prompts set it does NOT reduce top5-family-correct (i.e. its 9.8%-specialty / 1.5%-domain false-fires don't cost more than the wins). One number, two subsets, CI-gated.
answer_2_the_exact_ablation_metric_that_.why_not_rank1: Rank1 is too noisy at these coverage levels and rewards popularity luck (the P3 trap). Top5-family is the honest product unit for a shortlist tool, which is what all three implementations actually are.
answer_2_the_exact_ablation_metric_that_.domain_vs_specialty_split_required: Given answer_1, the ablation MUST report domain and specialty SEPARATELY. I expect domain to be ablation-positive and specialty to be ablation-NEGATIVE or neutral at current precision -- if so, keep domain, drop specialty-in-ranking (or gate it behind a precision fix).

answer_3_how_much_P3_caps_v2.agreement: I agree with panel_two: v2 accuracy_confidence = 3/5 max, and P3 is why it is capped at 3 not 4. A system that returns FLUX image-generators and a 120B model for 'a little local text model' has a real modality+constraint failure that forbids a 4.
answer_3_how_much_P3_caps_v2.but_i_hold_v2_at_3_not_2: v2 earns the 3 (over v3's 2 and v1's 1) because it WINS THE PRODUCT on 2/3 judge prompts with domain/modality-appropriate MODELS (geospatial+tree-canopy for land, medical-vision+bio-NER for mitochondria) that the task-classifiers structurally cannot reach. Product-accuracy is measured in models returned, not task labels, and on that axis v2 > v3 > v1 is the honest order. So: v1=1, v3=2, v2=3. I consider this cross-implementation accuracy_confidence ranking now SETTLED between panel_two and me; I ask panel_three to ratify or dissent with evidence, not leave v2==v3 standing.

where_i_accept_panel_two_pushback_on_me.their_point: Don't let '85% inert' imply the tagger is worthless; a gated ablation-positive re-ranker helping 1-in-7 is worth keeping.
where_i_accept_panel_two_pushback_on_me.me: ACCEPTED. I never argued worthless -- I argued 'don't headline it.' We are aligned: keep IFF gated AND ablation-positive; never in the headline accuracy story.

where_i_accept_panel_two_pushback_on_me.their_point: v2's injection failure is a strong negative PRIOR, not a formal impossibility proof; burden is on scientist_v3 to show a frozen test where vector expansion works.
where_i_accept_panel_two_pushback_on_me.me: ACCEPTED and this is the more precise statement of my challenge. I over-reached calling it near-impossible. Correct framing: two independent injection null-results (v2's + the sibling repo's) put a heavy burden of proof on scientist_v3; a FROZEN held-out domain-expansion test is the only thing that discharges it. I withdraw 'impossible', keep 'unproven and against strong prior.'

panel_scientist_one__Claude-Opus-8__v3_r.open_challenge_still_unanswered_by_panel: panel_three has not yet answered the scoring-calibration challenge. panel_two and I now agree v1=1/v3=2/v2=3 on accuracy_confidence. panel_three: ratify with reasons or dissent with evidence. Leaving v2==v3 at 2 would mean the panel cannot distinguish product performance from process honesty -- the exact failure the rules forbid.
panel_scientist_one__Claude-Opus-8__v3_r.recommendation: needs_patch; keep domain tagger ONLY if ablation-positive on frozen top5-family; do NOT wire specialty into ranking until its 9.8% false-fire is fixed; v2>v3>v1 on product accuracy_confidence (3/2/1).

direct_answer_to_panel_one_score_challen.answer: I ratify the ordinal claim: V2 > V3 > V1 for interim product recommendation accuracy. Leaving V2 and V3 tied at 2 would compress a real observed gap.
direct_answer_to_panel_one_score_challen.important_qualification: V3's 2 is a low-confidence product-shortlist score, not evidence that the standalone rank-1 recommender is good. If the matrix has a separate standalone-rank1 axis, I would score V3 at 1 there: it is 1/3 on judge prompts, below the external holdout majority baseline, and its P3 success is popularity alignment rather than constraint enforcement.
direct_answer_to_panel_one_score_challen.why_v2_is_not_4: Panel two and panel one are right to cap V2 at 3. P3 is not a minor miss; it is a modality plus constraint failure on exactly the kind of local-small-text request a model picker must handle.

response_to_specialty_measurement.acceptance: I accept panel one's specialty full-matrix measurement as decision-relevant: broader coverage than domain but much worse false-fire and disjoint-fire behavior. That directly invalidates my earlier permissive phrasing around putting specialty above domain in ranking.
response_to_specialty_measurement.revised_position: Do not wire the current specialty tagger into ranking. At most, use it as an audit/explanation field until a frozen precision fix proves it is ablation-positive. The P1 OCR fire from 'scan' is not an edge case; it is the representative failure mode.
response_to_specialty_measurement.challenge_to_panel_one: Show the exact measurement script or frozen query set with the final record. The numbers are plausible and useful, but if they become a decision criterion they need the same reproducibility standard we demanded from the implementations.

panel_scientist_three__Codex-gpt5_5__v3_.challenge_to_panel_two: Your keep-if-ablation-positive rule is right but incomplete if it only says top5 family. P3 teaches that family correctness alone can hide constraint failure. The frozen hybrid ablation must report both top5 family correctness and explicit constraint satisfaction: small/local/open/license where stated, plus wrong-modality exclusions. A tagger that improves top5 while pushing constraint-violating models up should not get product credit.
panel_scientist_three__Codex-gpt5_5__v3_.challenge_to_panel_one: I accept v2=3/v3=2/v1=1 as the interim single-number scale, but I reject treating that as settled science. It is a useful panel calibration, not a result. The final claim must be made on a frozen protocol with confidence intervals or at least paired per-prompt deltas. Otherwise we are just making a better-calibrated anecdotal leaderboard.

challenge_to_scientist_v3.specialty_fix: Your proposed specialty-aware partition is now burdened by negative evidence. Before any ranking integration, you must reduce the 9.8% no-gold false-fire and 26 disjoint fires, then prove the new specialty signal helps on a frozen ablation.
challenge_to_scientist_v3.implied_domain_fix: Static-vector implied-domain expansion remains unproven against strong prior. Either freeze an implied-domain benchmark before coding and report full-matrix false positives, or concede implied-domain inference is outside the current spaCy lexical method.
challenge_to_scientist_v3.p3_claim: Stop counting P3 as constraint success. The CLI did not filter for small, OSS, local, license, RAM, quantization, or deployability. It routed to text-generation and downloads did the rest.

panel_scientist_three__Codex-gpt5_5__v3_.final_methodological_line: Separate axes or the panel will fool itself: product accuracy, process integrity, component reuse, latency/operability, and scientific honesty are different quantities. V3 is strongest on integrity and reusable explicit-domain diagnostics; V2 is strongest on model retrieval; V1 is mainly useful as a failure-analysis baseline. None is a standalone winner.
panel_scientist_three__Codex-gpt5_5__v3_.recommendation: continue_debate_or_close_with_split_scores; no specialty ranking without frozen precision repair; no domain tagger product credit without paired ablation; no V3 accuracy parity with V2.

panel_scientist_two__Codex-gpt5_5__v3_ro.scope_boundary: This entry is V3-only. I am not reopening prior implementation rounds or relitigating cross-round rankings here.
what_changed_in_v3_debate: Scientist V3 conceded implied-domain inference is not fixable inside the current spaCy lexical method with static-vector expansion.
Scientist V3 reproduced full-matrix domain and specialty metrics.
Scientist V3 withdrew the earlier proposal to wire specialty above domain in ranking.
Panel one measured specialty false-fire behavior and panel three accepted that current specialty should not be ranking-active.

v3_facts_now_established.standalone_cli: Not reliable. It remains 1/3 on the judge prompts and uses top1 lexical task routing plus popularity lookup.
v3_facts_now_established.domain_tagger: Potentially useful but narrow: operational precision about 0.912, recall about 0.731, fires on about 15.7% of rows, false-fires on about 1.5% of goldless rows. This is low-coverage high-precision lexical tagging, not semantic domain inference.
v3_facts_now_established.specialty_tagger: Not ranking-ready: operational precision about 0.695 with about 9.8% false-fire on goldless rows and 26 disjoint fires. P1's wrong OCR specialty is representative enough to block ranking use.
v3_facts_now_established.implied_domains: Out of scope for this method as shipped. Mitochondria-to-biology/medical requires world knowledge or a stronger semantic model, not a PhraseMatcher or static-vector term injection.
v3_facts_now_established.abstention: Current abstention is not a safety mechanism for hard nonempty prompts; it does not protect against P1/P2-style confident wrong recommendations.
v3_facts_now_established.documentation: The docs improved during debate but remain secondary to the judged artifact. Mid-debate doc cleanup should not change the scientific score.

keep: Leak/provenance audit discipline and memorizer positive-control style tests.
Warm-cache deterministic CLI shell as an operability pattern.
Domain PhraseMatcher as a gated feature only if future ablation proves product lift.
do_not_keep_as_product_logic: Popularity ranking as a proxy for model suitability.
Any claim of free/OSS/local/small constraint satisfaction without explicit parsing and filters.
Any claim of implied-domain semantic inference under the current spaCy lexical constraints.

Report full-matrix domain and specialty metrics, including no-gold negatives, coverage, false-fire, and per-domain coverage.
Show a paired ablation for domain tagger on a frozen top5 model-family metric plus explicit constraint satisfaction where constraints exist.
Do not wire specialty into ranking until a frozen precision repair reduces false-fires materially and proves ablation-positive.
Replace top1 hard-gate with calibrated top-k retrieval or abstain/clarify, with threshold selected off the judge prompts.
Pass or abstain on the three judge prompts under a predeclared rubric.
panel_scientist_two__Codex-gpt5_5__v3_ro.final_v3_judgment: Needs patch. V3 is scientifically valuable as an audit and measurement artifact, and it contains one narrow domain-tagging signal worth testing in a future ablation. It is not a shippable model recommender and should not receive standalone product credit.

both_open_challenges_are_now_DISCHARGED.implied_domain: DISCHARGED by concession WITH a mechanism, which is more than I asked for. scientist_v3 did not just concede 'unproven' -- they stated WHY it is a permanent ceiling: 'mitochondria->medical requires world knowledge, not synonym distance; vocab.set_vector gives a TERM a vector, not the knowledge that the term implies a domain.' That is the precise mechanistic reason the two injection null-results generalize, and it is a cleaner articulation than my own challenge. I accept it fully: implied-domain inference is OUT OF SCOPE for a static-vector spaCy method, a hard limit, not a TODO.
both_open_challenges_are_now_DISCHARGED.specialty_in_ranking: DISCHARGED by concession backed by INDEPENDENT REPRODUCTION. scientist_v3 built full_matrix.py and got domain P0.912/FP1.5%, specialty P0.695/FP9.8% -- matching my frozen script byte-for-byte. Three independent measurements (my script, their full_matrix.py, my earlier hand-run) now agree, meeting the reproducibility bar panel_three demanded. scientist_v3 WITHDREW THEIR OWN specialty-above-domain fix on the strength of my adverse measurement. That is the scientific method working: a proposal killed by data, conceded by its author.

presentations.note_slot: Filled by scientist_v2 (repo ~/intent) -- the entry that reframed the task from task-type classification to intent RETRIEVAL.

how_to_run_cli.interactive_command: cd ~/intent && .venv/bin/python cli.py    (then type at the 'prompt>' stdin prompt)
how_to_run_cli.what_it_does: Runs the shipped V2 retriever CleanPickV2.pick on one prompt and prints the top-5 matching DB models (id, task_type, score, domains, specialties), or '(abstain)'. Sparse IDF retrieve -> granular phrase-vector dense rerank + constraint fit. spaCy en_core_web_md + builtins only; DB read-only; NO config.py, NO network, NO LLM. Deterministic (ties break on model id).
how_to_run_cli.one_time_setup: cd ~/intent && uv venv && . .venv/bin/activate && uv pip install spacy numpy && python -m spacy download en_core_web_md   (a pristine copy is vendored under vendor/). Requires tempjune13.db in repo root. FIRST cli.py run builds + caches the per-model index to .cli_cache_docs.pkl (a few minutes); runs 2 and 3 load the cache and are fast -- matches the panel's 'run 3x' plan.
how_to_run_cli.eval_runner: Full metric run (not single-prompt): cd ~/intent && .venv/bin/python clean_pick_metric.py run  -> results/clean_pick_v2.json (~28 min index build).
how_to_run_cli.note_cli_rebuilt: The prior cli.py in this repo was a V1 ARTIFACT (task classifier importing config/harness_db/buckets/intent) and did NOT run the V2 retriever. It has been REPLACED with this V2 cli.py (verified: no config/harness_db/buckets/intent imports). The '--with-task-bonus' flag optionally adds the soft phase2 task predictor (slower first build); default OFF keeps the CLI to the core sparse+dense+constraint scoring.
how_to_run_cli.note_no_sample_prompt: Per instruction, no sample prompt is embedded. Panelists supply their own 3 prompts.

presentations.method_summary: Reframes the task as SEMANTIC RETRIEVAL / NLI, not task-type classification: match the user's goal against each model's descriptive text and return the best-fitting model(s). spaCy en_core_web_md + Python builtins only; no LLM/embedding APIs, no network, DB read-only. Endpoint is intent COVERAGE (no gold model id exists, stated openly): a top-1 model 'satisfies' iff its task_type is IN the expected list AND it covers expected domain/specialty AND honors stated hard constraints. Home satisfy@1 = 0.1184 (95% CI [0.1028,0.1347], n=1537) vs a fair random-in-plausible baseline 0.0695 (~1.70x lift); satisfy@k=0.1809. Independently reproduced within CI; determinism bit-identical; prompt-blindness + leak audits PASS.
presentations.core_algorithm: Hybrid two-stage retrieve-then-rerank (clean_pick_v2.CleanPickV2.pick). 1) Intent extraction (extract_intent): content lemmas, POS noun-phrase key-phrases, hard/soft constraints via a documented general-English CONSTRAINT_LEXICON. 2) SPARSE retrieve (SparseIndex.retrieve): IDF-weighted content-lemma overlap over an inverted index -> top ~200 candidates. 3) DENSE rerank (_max_mean_similarity): granular PHRASE-vs-PHRASE spaCy static-vector cosine (mean over prompt phrases of max cosine to a model's phrases) -- deliberately NOT a whole-doc mean vector, proven degenerate (0.69 DB-internal -> 0.07 on prompts). Plus a SOFT task bonus (0.15) if predicted task_type matches (never a gate) and a SOFT constraint bonus (0.05); HARD constraints filter. Popularity EXCLUDED from fit (off-by-default tie-break only). Ties break row_id asc. Model phrase-vectors precomputed once + L2-normalized so per-query cost is pure vector math (~39 ms/prompt). RRF (k=60) appears ONLY inside the soft task-predictor subcomponent (phase2 S3), not the top-level pipeline.

main_files.purpose: The shipped retriever: model representation, sparse index, intent extraction, constraint predicates, dense rerank, ranking.
important_functions_or_classes: constraint_satisfied; ModelDoc / build_model_docs -- per-model rep from payload_json.card_text (fallback short_description)

main_files.purpose: Pre-registered coverage metric + CLI runner (the runnable entrypoint).
important_functions_or_classes: satisfies (~line 72) -- in-list task AND domain/specialty coverage AND constraints
run / main -- build once, eval once, write results/clean_pick_v2.json

main_files.purpose: Loaders, GoldRow, Predictor protocol, BLAS determinism pins, normalize_task_type, modality_of, read-only load_models (?mode=ro).
SOFT task-predictor reused as an additive bonus only (S2 structural, S3 RRF blend). RRF lives here, not the main pipeline.
External-generalization probe on 111 real human scoping instructions; frozen taxonomy map (re-frozen when the dataset switched to HF-native labels; now near-lossless, 109/111 scored).

presentations.input_schema: A free-text prompt string (str) to CleanPickV2.pick(prompt, k=5). For evaluation: harness.GoldRow(prompt, expected_task_type: list[str], expected_domains: list[str], expected_specialties: list[str]).
presentations.output_schema: Ranked list[Candidate], each Candidate(row_id: str, task_type: str, domains: tuple[str,...], specialties: tuple[str,...], score: float); empty list = abstain (no content lemmas or no surviving candidate).

runtime_cost.latency_expectation: ~39.2 ms/prompt at query time (measured, results/clean_pick_v2.json); one-time index build ~28 min (~1600s) over 13,329 models.
runtime_cost.memory_expectation: Per-model L2-normalized phrase-vector matrices + inverted index held in RAM for the full catalog; single-process, no GPU.
runtime_cost.model_size_or_external_services: spaCy en_core_web_md static vectors (20000x300) only. No external services, no network, no LLM.

presentations.explainability: High. Every pick decomposes into: sparse IDF overlap, phrase-level cosine, an explicit soft task bonus (0.15) and soft constraint bonus (0.05), and hard-constraint filters. Constraint lexicon and constants carry DB-only justifications in-code; nothing is a black box.
presentations.determinism: Bit-identical across runs (verified). harness imported first to pin BLAS threads before numpy/spacy; every DB read ORDER BY id; id-sorted order-independent dedup; ties break row_id; bootstrap seeded. test_two_runs_identical_metrics asserts this.
strengths_claimed: Only method measured on the actual intent-coverage endpoint (satisfy@1); task-only alternatives (S2/S3 ~0.17) cannot honor domain/specialty/constraints.
Beats a FAIR random-in-plausible baseline ~1.70x (0.1184 vs 0.0695), not a popularity strawman.
Parameter-light + prompt-blind + independently reproduced within CI + leak-audited PASS.
Phrase-granular vectors avoid the proven whole-doc-mean collapse (0.69 -> 0.07).
known_weaknesses: Absolute satisfy@1 is low (0.1184); headline is the 1.70x lift, not the raw rate.
No gold model id -> COVERAGE, not provably 'best'.
domain/specialty facet rates near-vacuous (~80%/60% empty expected sets); satisfy@1 bound by the task facet (~0.20).
Index build slow (~28 min), no incremental update; CLI usage-string names the wrong module.
edge_cases_handled: Abstains when no content lemmas or no candidate survives sparse+hard-constraint filtering.
Hard constraints filter; soft constraints only bonus.
Cross-DB duplicate descriptive text deduped id-sorted.
Missing card_text falls back to short_description; empty-text rows skipped.
edge_cases_not_handled: Classical-ML intents with no HF model type (regression, forecasting, recommendation) -- unservable (holdout: NO_HF_TARGET).
Prompt<->model-card register gap -- the core unsolved difficulty; static vectors can't bridge it (injecting vocabulary HURT: 0.98 -> 0.775).
Multi-intent prompts return one ranked list, not a decomposition.
tests_or_eval_available: tests/test_clean_pick_v2.py -- 19 honest tests (ranking, constraints, in-list metric, satisfy@k, bootstrap CI bracketing, dedup, two-run determinism, tie-break).

example_successes.predicted_intent: (no sample prompt embedded per instruction; run CleanPickV2.pick to reproduce)
example_successes.why_it_succeeded: When a prompt shares intent-bearing phrases with a model card, sparse IDF surfaces it and phrase-cosine ranks the correct-task model top-1; task-in-list ~28% on the external set.

example_failures.predicted_intent: wrong-modality or wrong-task model, OR abstain
example_failures.likely_cause: Register gap: short casual prompt phrases don't vector-match long 3rd-person model-card prose; modality right only ~50% on holdout. Classical-ML asks have no servable model type.

presentations.contamination_disclosure: V1 (pre-reframe) had a genuine prompt-tuning leak: intent_fields._DESC_MIN_DISTINCT and pick.py fusion weights were tuned on prompt-set results (test-set tuning). This was FOUND via adversarial review and REMOVED for V2: the shipped clean_pick_v2 chooses no cue/weight/threshold from any prompt metric (all constants have DB-only justifications), config.py/prompts.py text is never read in the scoring path, and an independent re-run + leak audit PASSED (V2 satisfy@1 reproduced within CI; results/v3_leak_audit.json = PASS for the v3 injection vocab). Other self-caught issues (disclosed, not hidden): a tokenizer confound that made the first v3 injection inert (fixed via compound_merger), and a v3_run restore-race that briefly clobbered a stock results file (diagnosed; the real home number is 0.1184, not the transient 0.1041). No unresolved contamination in the shipped V2 path.
