rejected_alternatives_standalone.v2_intent__clean_pick_v2: REJECTED standalone (but best build-on base): P3 catastrophe (FLUX image-gen + 120B for 'a little SLM'; full 29-min path STILL returns 120B — no size filter in either path); CLI/eval parity gap (0.1184 belongs to a config the default CLI does not run and that times out at 250s); no hard constraint/modality guard; satisfy@1 0.118 means intent unsatisfied on ~88% of prompts.

recommended_algorithm_for_task_extractio.description: Lemma/IDF cosine of the prompt against per-task documents built from CLEAN supervision (DB short_description + HF task descriptions), abstaining when no discriminative lemma is shared (v3 exp02 LexScorer).
recommended_algorithm_for_task_extractio.why: Deterministic, interpretable, no-leakage-by-construction, and it is the ONE component that inferred P3's task (text-generation) correctly where v1 (token-class) and v2-default (image) failed. Corroborated by LaCy 2602.12005 sec 3-4 (spaCy lexical signal is a legitimate delegation/decision signal) and RAS survey 2509.10697 sec 4.1.2 (keyword/rule text classification lineage) — flagged as corroboration, verdict rests on the reproduced 1/3-correct-task result.
recommended_algorithm_for_task_extractio.why_not: Top1 0.226 below majority baseline on holdout; a SHORTLIST (top5 0.53), not a confident rank-1 classifier. Must be FUSED (LaCy's lesson: one lexical signal is insufficient) and never hard-gated into retrieval.

recommended_algorithm_for_task_extractio.description: Max-normalized additive fusion of >=2 config-derived signals (flat tf-idf + IDF-weighted lexical), NOT RRF, NOT a hard bucket gate (v1 harness_ensemble).
recommended_algorithm_for_task_extractio.why: v1's replicated finding: soft additive fusion > hard gate; max-norm lets an all-zero signal abstain instead of injecting mid-ranks. This is the correct FUSION operator for the shortlist.
recommended_algorithm_for_task_extractio.why_not: v1's specific posterior is below-baseline; reuse the OPERATOR, not v1's tuned weights, and re-fit on clean supervision.

recommended_algorithm_for_task_extractio.description: Hard modality guard from the inferred task's coarse bucket, rejecting cross-modality models before ranking.
recommended_algorithm_for_task_extractio.why: P3 proved a soft task bonus lets image-gen win a text request; a hard modality reject is required, not a +0.15 nudge.
recommended_algorithm_for_task_extractio.why_not: Depends on correct task inference upstream; must abstain when task is low-margin.

recommended_algorithm_for_domain_extract.description: spaCy PhraseMatcher over a FROZEN config vocabulary of domain terms, multi-label, abstaining when no term fires (v3 exp25).
recommended_algorithm_for_domain_extract.why: Measured operational precision 0.912, false-fire 1.5% on 1235 no-gold rows (I reproduced this with a frozen script). High-precision, deterministic, non-circular (misses 27% of gold -> not self-generating).
recommended_algorithm_for_domain_extract.why_not: Low coverage: fires-and-correct on only 14.4% of prompts, 0/3 judge prompts; purely lexical so implied domains (mitochondria->medical) NEVER fire — a HARD CEILING (v3 conceded: static vectors cannot encode the world-knowledge that mitochondria implies biology; two injection null-results confirm). Report on the FULL matrix, gate behind a fired-domain check, admit to ranking ONLY if ablation-positive.

recommended_algorithm_for_specialty_extr.description: Same PhraseMatcher mechanism as domain BUT restricted to AUDIT/EXPLANATION output, NOT model ranking.
recommended_algorithm_for_specialty_extr.why: I measured specialty full-matrix: precision 0.695, false-fire 9.8% (6.5x domain), 26 disjoint fires. P1's wrong 'ocr' fire (from 'scan') is one of 91 false positives. Too imprecise to rank on.
recommended_algorithm_for_specialty_extr.why_not: Wiring it into ranking would inject the P1-class error ~91x across 1537 prompts. Keep it as an explanation field only until a frozen precision repair proves ablation-positive.

recommended_algorithm_for_model_extracti.description: TWO-REGIME: (A) constrained prompts -> hard structured DB-column filter (task_type + parameters + license/proprietary) FIRST, phrase-vector cosine only as intra-filter tie-break; (B) unconstrained -> sparse IDF retrieve + granular phrase-vs-phrase cosine rerank (v2 CleanPickV2), domain-gated.
recommended_algorithm_for_model_extracti.why: P3 (constrained) is solved by columns once task+constraints known; P1/P2 (unconstrained) need retrieval (filter-first gave an NSFW detector for mitochondria). Corroborated by Blended RAG 2404.07220 (sparse+dense hybrid) and RAS survey sec 3.2.1/3.2.4 (hybrid + retrieve-then-rerank) — corroboration flagged; verdict rests on my reproduced two-regime experiment.
recommended_algorithm_for_model_extracti.why_not: CENTRAL UNVALIDATED RISK: the router's constraint detector currently fires only open_source on P3 (misses size); proprietary=0 != OSS-licensed and params<=2B != local-deployable (crude proxies, per three). Router is HARD, buildable-but-unbuilt, must be frozen-then-scored. Retrieval regime itself is only ~0.118 satisfy@1 — 'least-bad', not strong.

panel_scientist_one__Claude-Opus-8__fina.motion: I have drafted the fields I own with reproduced-evidence caveats. I ask panel_two to RATIFY OR AMEND the build-on ranking (v2>v3>v1) with a reason, and panel_three to confirm the recommended_algorithm blocks match the ablation protocol you drafted. If both ratify, we transcribe into end_of_debate_FINAL_decision. If either dissents on the ranking, give the counter-rank — 'no winner' does not answer 'what do we build on.'

panel_scientist_one__Claude-Opus-8__fina.type: CRITICAL CLARIFICATION prompted by the human: 'no winner' does NOT mean the problem is unsolvable. Disambiguating the two claims.
panel_scientist_one__Claude-Opus-8__fina.why_this_entry: The human asked whether 'no winner' means intent inference is UNSOLVABLE. It does not, and the panel's loose 'no winner' language invited that misread. I separate three distinct claims we have conflated.

three_distinct_claims_do_not_conflate.claim_1_no_submitted_artifact_wins_stand: TRUE. A verdict on THESE THREE repos: v1 unsafe (0/3, NSFW for a land query, no abstain), v3 task ranker BELOW holdout majority baseline, v2 fails constrained prompts (120B for 'a little SLM', in BOTH the default and the 29-min full path). This is about the artifacts, not the task.
three_distinct_claims_do_not_conflate.claim_2_best_architecture_is_unbuilt: TRUE. The two-regime router is the best design we identified, but it is unbuilt and its constraint-detector step MEASURABLY fails on P3 today. So we cannot claim MEASURED victory for it. Recommending != demonstrating.
three_distinct_claims_do_not_conflate.claim_3_the_problem_is_unsolvable: FALSE. We did NOT show intent inference is impossible. We showed it is SOLVABLE TO A BOUNDED, USEFUL DEGREE, capped by one specific sub-problem.

what_IS_solvable_with_evidence.task_inference: SOLVABLE as a SHORTLIST. v3's lexical scorer inferred P3's task (text-generation) correctly; top5 ~0.53 over 56 classes (13x random). Deterministic, interpretable, bounded. Not a confident rank-1 (top1 0.226), but a usable menu.
what_IS_solvable_with_evidence.constrained_routing_P3_class: SOLVABLE and nearly DETERMINISTIC once parsed. The answer to 'small OSS local text model' is in DB columns (parameters, license, task_type) with ZERO semantic matching needed. The hard part is PARSING the constraint from colloquial language ('little'/'local'), a hard-but-BUILDABLE NLP problem (frozen general lexicon + dependency/phrase rules + unknown/clarify outcome), NOT impossible.
what_IS_solvable_with_evidence.explicit_domain_routing: SOLVABLE at high precision. v3 domain PhraseMatcher: 0.912 operational precision on explicitly-domained prompts (finance/legal/medical/cybersecurity all fire correctly). Low coverage (14.4%) but real.

the_ONE_genuine_ceiling.the_hard_wall: IMPLIED / WORLD-KNOWLEDGE intent under the 'spaCy + builtins, NO LLM' constraint. When a user says 'mitochondria' and the correct domain is 'medical/biology', NOTHING LEXICAL in the prompt signals that. spaCy en_core_web_md static vectors are context-free lemma vectors; they do not encode that mitochondria IS a cell-biology object. v3 conceded this with a mechanism; v2's injection study and v3's sibling-repo injection study BOTH confirm static-vector expansion cannot close it (register/world-knowledge gap, not OOV).
the_ONE_genuine_ceiling.why_this_caps_the_NUMBERS: This is a DATA/INFORMATION ceiling, not a tuning failure. v1 quantified it: oracle-bucket ceiling 67.2% vs real 42.7% — the ~24-point gap is prompts where the disambiguating cue is ABSENT FROM THE TEXT. No config-only lexical method closes that gap BY CONSTRUCTION. The low absolute numbers (v2 0.118, v3 0.226) are PARTLY this ceiling, not pure method weakness.
the_ONE_genuine_ceiling.what_would_break_the_ceiling: A contextual embedding model (i.e., an actual LM) that KNOWS mitochondria implies biology. That is exactly what the conference rules BANNED ('spaCy + builtins, no LLM'). So within the stated constraints, implied-intent inference has a hard ceiling; RELAX the no-LLM constraint and it becomes tractable (cf. LaCy 2602.12005, which USES a small LM + spaCy TOGETHER — the hybrid the rules forbade here).

panel_scientist_one__Claude-Opus-8__fina.the_honest_one_line_for_the_human: Intent -> task -> model routing is SOLVABLE to a useful, bounded degree with spaCy alone (task shortlist ~0.53@5, explicit-domain 0.91 precision, constrained routing near-deterministic once parsed). It is CAPPED by a register/world-knowledge gap on IMPLIED intent (unstated domain/intent) that NO config-only lexical method can close — that specific sub-problem needs a contextual model the rules excluded. 'No winner' = no submitted artifact is production-ready AND the best architecture is unbuilt; it is NOT 'the problem is impossible.'
panel_scientist_one__Claude-Opus-8__fina.implication_for_the_final_decision: We must WORD the verdict so it cannot be misread as 'unsolvable.' final_decision should say: solvable-to-a-bounded-degree; recommended architecture = two-regime router (unbuilt); hard ceiling on implied intent under the no-LLM constraint; if the no-LLM constraint is relaxed, a small-LM+spaCy hybrid (LaCy-style) is the evidence-backed path past the ceiling. I will make sure how_to_use_findings_from_conference states this explicitly so the human is not left thinking the task is hopeless.

panel_scientist_one__Claude-Opus-8__fina.type: EXTERNAL REALITY CHECK — I researched how spaCy intent inference is ACTUALLY done in production. It corrects a load-bearing assumption behind my 'hard ceiling' claim.
panel_scientist_one__Claude-Opus-8__fina.why_this_entry: The human challenged whether my 'implied intent is a hard ceiling' claim is REALISTIC, given hundreds of real projects resolve user intent with spaCy. I researched it. The finding: the ceiling I described is REAL for the SPECIFIC WAY all three scientists used spaCy (config-vocab LEXICAL MATCHING), but production spaCy intent systems DO NOT USE THAT METHOD. They use approaches that are stronger and that all three repos left on the table. My earlier framing was too pessimistic because it generalized from three suboptimal designs.

what_production_spacy_intent_systems_ACT.1_trained_TextCategorizer_not_lexical_ma: The dominant production pattern (Rasa NLU, the O'Reilly 'NLP with Python and spaCy' book, Width.ai's production pipelines) is spaCy's TextCategorizer (textcat) — a TRAINABLE supervised classifier (ensemble of a bag-of-words model + a neural CNN-with-attention model) fit on LABELED intent examples. This is SUPERVISED LEARNING inside spaCy, and it is spaCy+builtins (no external LLM). NONE of our three scientists trained a textcat. v1/v3 used config-vocab PhraseMatcher/lexical cosine; v2 used static-vector retrieval. All three chose ZERO-TRAINING lexical methods and hit the lexical ceiling — but that ceiling is a CHOICE, not a spaCy limit.
what_production_spacy_intent_systems_ACT.2_transitive_verb_direct_object_dependen: The O'Reilly spaCy book's canonical intent method: parse the utterance and extract the TRANSITIVE-VERB + DIRECT-OBJECT pair (dobj) as the intent core. 'order a photo cake' -> (order, cake); 'send text' -> (send, text). This uses the DEPENDENCY PARSER — which ALL THREE scientists explicitly DISABLED (v1: 'ner/parser excluded, ~20% faster'; v3: same; v2: POS-only build). They turned off the exact component the standard method relies on. For P3 'send text', a dobj parse yields (send, text) -> text-generation directly, no vocab needed.
what_production_spacy_intent_systems_ACT.3_word_vectors_as_FEATURES_into_a_classi: Rasa's intent_featurizer_spacy uses spaCy word vectors as FEATURES fed into an sklearn/SVM/DIET classifier — NOT as a raw cosine retrieval (which v2 did and found degenerate, 0.69->0.07). The vectors are input to a TRAINED decision boundary, which is far more robust than raw cosine.
what_production_spacy_intent_systems_ACT.4_hybrid_with_transformers_is_standard: spaCy explicitly supports swapping its ensemble for a HuggingFace transformer via Thinc's Model interface. Production systems routinely combine spaCy (fast tokenization/POS/NER/dependency) WITH a transformer for the semantic step. This is exactly the LaCy 2602.12005 pattern (spaCy + small LM together) — and exactly what the conference rules BANNED.

real_world_accuracy_numbers_for_calibrat.implication: Real intent systems hit 85-96% on 30-150 intent classes. Our three repos hit 0.12-0.43 on 56 classes. The gap is NOT because 56-class intent inference is a ~0.2 ceiling — it is because production systems TRAIN A CLASSIFIER (textcat/SVM/transformer) on labeled examples, while our three scientists used ZERO-TRAINING lexical/retrieval methods. The task is far more solvable than our conference numbers suggest.
real_world_accuracy_numbers_for_calibrat.caveat: Those benchmarks (CLINC150/ATIS/BANKING77) classify into a KNOWN intent taxonomy from SHORT COMMANDS. Our task (free-text goal -> HF task_type -> best model) is harder: longer prompts, implied intent, AND a model-selection step with no gold label. So we should not expect 96%. But we SHOULD expect that a TRAINED textcat over the labeled prompt matrix (which v3's 1537-row PROMPT_MATRIX PROVIDES) would beat 0.226 lexical top1 substantially.

the_corrected_verdict_on_the_ceiling.what_was_right: For CONFIG-VOCAB LEXICAL matching with the parser DISABLED and ZERO training — the method all three actually shipped — implied/world-knowledge intent IS a hard ceiling. That finding stands for what they built.
the_corrected_verdict_on_the_ceiling.what_was_too_pessimistic: I implied this ceiling is intrinsic to 'spaCy + builtins, no LLM.' It is NOT. Two in-spec, no-LLM, spaCy-native methods the scientists never tried would likely break past it: (a) TRAIN a TextCategorizer on the labeled 1537-prompt matrix; (b) re-ENABLE the dependency parser and extract verb+dobj intent cores. Both are spaCy+builtins, both are standard production practice, both were left on the table.
the_corrected_verdict_on_the_ceiling.the_real_finding: The conference's low numbers are substantially an ARTIFACT OF METHOD CHOICE (zero-training lexical + disabled parser), not a fundamental limit of spaCy intent inference. The scientists optimized for determinism/explainability/no-leakage and PAID FOR IT in accuracy — a legitimate tradeoff, but they should NOT be read as 'this is the ceiling of spaCy intent inference.' Production spaCy does much better by TRAINING.

panel_scientist_one__Claude-Opus-8__fina.what_this_means_for_the_final_decision: This STRENGTHENS the 'no submitted winner' verdict (all three used a weaker-than-standard method) but WEAKENS any claim that the task is near-unsolvable. The recommended architecture should ADD, as in-spec options the scientists ignored: (1) a trained spaCy TextCategorizer over the labeled prompt matrix as the task-inference core (supervised, still spaCy+builtins, no leakage if trained on a proper split); (2) dependency-parse verb+dobj extraction (re-enable the parser) for the intent core; (3) spaCy vectors as FEATURES into a trained classifier, not raw cosine. I will add these to proposed_alternatives_outside_of_implementations_reviewed and recommended_algorithm_for_task_extraction, because they are how the REAL WORLD does this and NONE of our three tried them.
panel_scientist_one__Claude-Opus-8__fina.honest_flag_to_panel: panel_two, panel_three: I am REVISING my own earlier 'hard ceiling' framing based on external research the conference did not do. The three scientists all chose zero-training lexical methods and never tried a trained textcat or the dependency-parse intent method that the standard spaCy references (O'Reilly, Rasa, Width.ai) treat as the DEFAULT. Our verdict must say this explicitly, or we mislead the human into thinking ~0.2 is the achievable ceiling. It is not; it is the ceiling of the SPECIFIC methods submitted.

panel_scientist_three__Codex-gpt5_5__fin.role: panelist scientist judge only; devil's advocate stance
panel_scientist_three__Codex-gpt5_5__fin.type: response to human and panel one: spaCy intent is realistic, but do not overcorrect into unsupported confidence
panel_scientist_three__Codex-gpt5_5__fin.position: I accept the human's correction and panel one's self-correction: spaCy-assisted user-intent resolution is a realistic, common NLP engineering pattern. Therefore 'no winner' must mean no submitted winner today, not no possible spaCy method.

evidence_checked.relevance: spaCy provides supervised document-level category prediction, including single-label and multilabel setups. Intent classification can be implemented as text categorization when labels and splits are valid.

evidence_checked.source: spaCy Matcher API and rule-based matching docs
evidence_checked.relevance: spaCy supports deterministic linguistic pattern matching. That is appropriate for constraint/entity extraction such as OSS, local, small, modality, domain terms, and explicit model names.
Rasa frames NLU as extracting intents and entities from user messages. This validates the problem class as practical rather than speculative.
Rasa publicly describes using lower-level libraries including spaCy to turn messages into intents and entities. This supports the ecosystem point.

challenge_to_panel_one_overcorrection: I do not accept 'dominant production pattern' or exact CLINC/BANKING-style accuracy transfer as established by today's record unless the cited systems, datasets, label taxonomies, and splits are brought into the JSON. Public practice proves plausibility, not a target accuracy for model recommendation.

A trained spaCy textcat over the prompt matrix is plausible, but only if the labels are clean, independent, and actually aligned with user satisfaction. If the matrix labels were induced from the same model metadata used at ranking time, textcat can simply learn dataset artifacts.

Dependency verb-object extraction may help P3's 'send text', but it does not by itself solve noun-heavy scientific prompts such as mitochondria, satellite plots, or 'best model for X' where the important object is a domain concept and the target is a model class.

The real production lesson is not 'textcat would solve this'; it is 'trainable supervised intent plus deterministic constraint/entity extraction should have been tested against frozen raw prompts and end-to-end model targets.'
panel_scientist_three__Codex-gpt5_5__fin.challenge_to_panel_two_and_self: Our earlier router proposal should be rephrased as a testable engineering hypothesis, not the conference winner. The human is right that spaCy makes the hypothesis realistic. But until we build it, score it on held-out raw prompts, and show model-level improvements, it remains a proposed alternative rather than a demonstrated result.
panel_scientist_three__Codex-gpt5_5__fin.revised_final_language: No demonstrated winner today. The task is realistic and solvable to useful levels with spaCy-assisted intent/entity/constraint methods. The three submissions failed because they leaned on brittle zero-training lexical/retrieval designs, weak or absent constraint parsing, poor endpoint behavior, and insufficient end-to-end validation.
panel_scientist_three__Codex-gpt5_5__fin.final_decision_implication: The final fields should explicitly separate feasibility from evidence: spaCy intent inference is feasible and widely practiced; none of V1, V2, or V3 demonstrated a trustworthy standalone recommender; the recommended next experiment is a supervised spaCy textcat or equivalent intent layer plus rule/entity constraints, evaluated against frozen raw prompts and model-selection outcomes.

panel_scientist_two__Codex-gpt5_5__final.type: response to latest debate points using hard facts from the five research papers
panel_scientist_two__Codex-gpt5_5__final.research_boundary: I am using the papers as scientific context and hard constraints on our reasoning, not as substitute evidence for V1/V2/V3. The conference verdict still rests on deterministic CLI behavior, algorithm inspection, and documented implementation quality.

2602_12005_LaCy: LaCy is not a spaCy-only intent classifier. It is an SLM pretraining/delegation method that combines loss with spaCy grammar-derived factual-token annotations and trains the SLM to emit <CALL> tokens.
The main setup delegates 15% of tokens during training and uses an enforced 22% call budget in generation comparisons.

For biography generation with a 334M SLM plus Llama 3.2 1B cascade partner, LaCy reports a 6.88 percentage-point FactScore increase over the no-<CALL> baseline and reports that 334M LaCy beats a 20x larger Llama 2-7B baseline in that setting.
LaCy reports the lowest fact leakage on factual QA probes when calling is disabled, and reports call-token validation loss around 5.72 on the tokens it chooses to delegate.
The ablation is decisive for our debate: spaCy-only delegation performs worse than LaCy. The paper says the loss-based selection component is necessary.
The paper says validation loss is not correlated with FactScore in this delegation setup, which directly supports our rejection of a single scalar confidence score.

Reported preprocessing overhead is 152 hours per 1B tokens on one CPU core for LaCy labels, compared with 233 hours per 1B tokens on one A100 GPU for LLM-judge labels and 56 hours per 1B tokens on one A100 GPU for Rho-1.
The authors explicitly call the study explorative and exclude the downstream question of what to do after the SLM calls.
2605_24518_grammatically_guided_sparse_a: This paper uses spaCy en_core_web_sm POS tags to build hard and soft grammatical attention masks for a bert-tiny or DistilBERT-like classifier on SST-2.
Hard grammatical masking matched the full-attention baseline accuracy at 0.8200; soft masking scored 0.8165.
Training time increased from 1755.05s baseline to 2028.30s for hard masking, about +15.6%, and to 2308.06s for soft masking, about +31.5%.

The limitations section matters: experiments are small-scale, CPU/Python spaCy mask generation erases wall-clock efficiency gains in the prototype, grammatical rules are incomplete, and interpretability was not empirically evaluated.
2601_00506_rule_based_atomic_sentence_ex: The paper evaluates a spaCy dependency-rule extractor on 100 manually annotated WikiSplit sentences, producing 252 aligned atomic sentence pairs.
Reported scores are ROUGE-1 F1 0.6714, ROUGE-2 F1 0.4776, ROUGE-L F1 0.6495, and BERTScore F1 0.5648.
The largest error categories are Missing Object at 44.4444% and Multiple/Other at 39.2857% of errors.

The conclusion is balanced: dependency rules are transparent and useful for simple structures, but brittle under coordination, apposition, relative/adverbial clauses, passive constructions, and missing-argument reconstruction.
2404_07220_Blended_RAG: Blended RAG evaluates hybrid query/retriever combinations across BM25, dense vector/KNN, and sparse encoder retrieval.
It reports NQ NDCG@10 of 0.67 versus a 0.633 monoT5-3B benchmark and TREC-COVID NDCG@10 of 0.87 versus a 0.804 COCO-DR Large benchmark.
For SQuAD RAG, it reports Blended RAG EM 57.63, F1 68.4, Top-5 retrieval 94.89, and Top-20 retrieval 98.58.
For NQ zero-shot RAG, it reports EM 42.63, F1 53.96, Top-5 retrieval 88.22, and Top-20 retrieval 88.88.

Its caveats map directly to our final design: metadata improves blended retrievers; on CoQA without metadata, hybrid queries offered no improvement over basic queries; and common IR/RAG metrics were described as poor proxies for human alignment.
2509_10697_RAS_survey: The RAS survey organizes retrieval into sparse, dense, and hybrid approaches.
It says sparse retrieval remains effective, efficient, and interpretable, but can miss relevant content without lexical overlap.
It says dense retrieval enables semantic matching without lexical overlap, but brings training-data and interpretability issues.
It presents hybrid retrieval as combining complementary strengths to improve effectiveness and robustness.
It describes multi-stage retrieval and reranking as a fast first-stage candidate generator followed by a stronger slower reranker.
It frames text structuring as taxonomy construction, text classification, and information extraction, and identifies retrieval efficiency, knowledge quality, and integration as core challenges.

direct_response_to_latest_debate.no_winner_does_not_mean_unsolvable: Panel one and panel three are now right on semantics. The papers show feasible patterns: delegation/abstention in LaCy, hybrid retrieval in Blended RAG and RAS, explicit structuring/classification in RAS, and grammar-guided signals in LaCy and sparse attention. The task is not hopeless; the submitted systems did not demonstrate it.
direct_response_to_latest_debate.v2_as_build_on_base: I ratify the forced build-on ranking V2 > V3 > V1. Blended RAG and the RAS survey support hybrid retrieval plus reranking as a serious architecture family, which favors V2 over task-label lookup. But this is not a V2 win: V2's satisfy@1 was weak, it failed P3 with 120B, and its CLI/eval mismatch means its measured and shipped paths were not the same object.
direct_response_to_latest_debate.router_and_constraint_detection: The papers make the router more plausible but less trivial. LaCy shows systems can learn when to delegate, but spaCy-only ablation was worse than loss+spaCy. The atomic-sentence paper shows rule/dependency methods are transparent but miss objects and complex dependencies often. Therefore constraint detection is buildable, not solved.
direct_response_to_latest_debate.spacy_production_reality_check: Panel one's production-spaCy correction is useful, but it should not become a new overclaim. TextCategorizer and dependency parsing are plausible in-spec alternatives; they are not evaluated in V1/V2/V3 and they do not transfer CLINC/BANKING-style numbers to model recommendation. Panel three's caution is correct.
direct_response_to_latest_debate.confidence_scoring: LaCy's loss-vs-FactScore result and Blended RAG's warning about IR/RAG metrics both support structured confidence: regime, constraint pass/fail/unknown, retrieval evidence, metadata status, and abstain/clarify action. A single scalar would be scientifically false precision.
direct_response_to_latest_debate.scientific_domain_prompts: RAS emphasizes structured knowledge quality and domain expertise. For P2, a model-card hit on 'medical' or 'cell' is insufficient. The recommender must either verify microscopy/organelle-stage evidence or clarify/abstain.
direct_response_to_latest_debate.metadata_filters: Blended RAG's metadata caveat supports our hard metadata status design. Missing parameter, license, or local-deployability fields must be unknown, not treated as passing.

where_i_disagree_with_panel_one: LaCy cannot be used as evidence that spaCy-only intent inference closes implied scientific intent. LaCy uses an SLM and cascade partner, and spaCy-only ablation was weaker.

The 'hard ceiling' claim should be narrowed. The papers support a serious world-knowledge gap for zero-training lexical methods, but they do not prove that no non-LLM ontology, taxonomy, biomedical dictionary, or supervised spaCy classifier could bridge 'mitochondria' to biology.
The recommended algorithm should not include V3 domain rerank as default. RAS and Blended RAG support testing structured/hybrid retrieval; they do not validate V3's domain signal.
where_i_disagree_with_panel_three: The no-winner language is correct, but the field draft still needs the forced build-on ranking. Ranking V2 first is not blessing V2; it is naming the least-bad substrate.
The ablation protocol should add a LaCy-style abstain/delegate metric: did the system know when not to recommend?
The ablation protocol should add a metadata-quality slice because Blended RAG's CoQA finding shows absent metadata can neutralize hybrid-query benefits.

build_on_order: V2 first as retrieval/indexing substrate only
V3 second for integrity/provenance audit and optional explicit-domain diagnostics
V1 third as negative-control/audit lesson only
updated_position.recommended_architecture_status: research-backed design hypothesis, unbuilt and unvalidated in this conference
most_research_supported_features: hybrid sparse/dense or sparse/phrase retrieval with reranking
structured metadata, taxonomy, ontology, or classifier signals where available
explicit abstain/clarify/delegate behavior when evidence is insufficient
separate evaluation of routing, retrieval, metadata truth, and endpoint model-recommendation quality
least_supported_claims: the router is cheap or already solved
spaCy-only lexical inference recovers implied scientific domains
a single scalar score can stand for confidence
V2's relative P1/P2 wins make it scientifically strong in absolute terms

panel_scientist_two__Codex-gpt5_5__final.bottom_line: The research facts harden the middle position: do not call the problem impossible, do not call the router a demonstrated winner, and do not pretend the papers validate any submitted repo. The defensible final wording is: no submitted implementation wins; if forced to build on one, start from V2; the recommended next design is retrieval plus structuring plus abstention, with router, metadata quality, and endpoint accuracy frozen-tested.

panel_scientist_one__Claude-Opus-8__fina.type: ranking is SETTLED; I concede three's two overcorrection-checks; and I RESOLVE the textcat-leakage question with evidence — it ties the whole conference together
panel_scientist_one__Claude-Opus-8__fina.ranking_now_SETTLED_unanimous: panel_three answered the build-on ranking I forced: V2 first (retrieval substrate), V3 second (integrity/eval infra + optional domain diagnostics), V1 third (negative controls + audit lessons). That is IDENTICAL to my round-7 assertion. With panel_two having implied the same across four entries, the build-on order V2>V3>V1 is UNANIMOUS and settled. It goes into pipeline_if_pick_one_implementation. This is not a standalone-winner claim; it is the answer to 'what do we extend if forced to pick one repo.'

concessions_to_three_on_my_overcorrectio.their_check: 'Dominant production pattern' and CLINC/BANKING accuracy do not TRANSFER to model-recommendation without matched taxonomies/splits — public practice proves plausibility, not a target number.
concessions_to_three_on_my_overcorrectio.my_response: CONCEDED. I cited 85-96% CLINC to show the task is not a ~0.2 ceiling, and that point stands as PLAUSIBILITY. But I withdraw any implication that a trained textcat here would HIT 85-96%. CLINC is 30-150 KNOWN intents from SHORT COMMANDS; ours is 56 task_types from LONG free-text goals PLUS an unlabeled model-selection step. The correct claim is narrow: a trained textcat would very likely BEAT 0.226 lexical top1 on THIS matrix — not that it reaches CLINC numbers. Plausibility, not a target.

concessions_to_three_on_my_overcorrectio.their_check: Dependency verb+dobj helps P3 'send text' but does NOT solve noun-heavy scientific prompts (mitochondria).
concessions_to_three_on_my_overcorrectio.my_response: CONCEDED and it is an important limit. verb+dobj gives (send, text) for P3, but P2 'differentiate between mature and nascent mitochondria' has dobj='mitochondria' — a NOUN with no task signal, and 'differentiate' -> classification is the only weak cue. So dependency extraction helps IMPERATIVE/action prompts (P1 'scan', P3 'send') but NOT descriptive/nominal scientific prompts. It is a partial tool, not a fix for the implied-domain ceiling. My reality-check corrected the 'ceiling is intrinsic' overclaim; it did NOT erase the ceiling for noun-heavy implied prompts.

RESOLVING_threes_textcat_leakage_concern.their_concern: A trained textcat over the prompt matrix could learn DATASET ARTIFACTS if the labels were induced from the same model metadata used at ranking time.
RESOLVING_threes_textcat_leakage_concern.what_i_checked: I sampled the PROMPT_MATRIX. The prompts are HUMAN-AUTHORED natural-language goals whose text shares almost NO lemmas with the task label — e.g. 'Image helping people get unstuck for customer use' -> visual-question-answering; 'I need it to pick the best option from a list even when the setup is messy' -> multiple-choice; 'return masks for the real workflow' -> mask-generation. The label is NOT templated from the prompt or from DB row text.
RESOLVING_threes_textcat_leakage_concern.the_nuanced_verdict: three is HALF-right and it is the most important methodological point in the final debate. (a) The prompts themselves are NOT artifact-templated -> a textcat would learn REAL prompt->intent signal, not string-copy. GOOD. (b) BUT the LABELS were assigned by SOMEONE using the 56-task taxonomy, and if that annotation leaned on config/model-metadata, a textcat can learn 'taxonomy-word -> label' — the SAME closed-taxonomy circularity we flagged for v3's domain tagger. So a trained textcat is NOT automatically clean.
RESOLVING_threes_textcat_leakage_concern.the_synthesis_that_ties_the_conference_t: This is the punchline of the whole conference: the RIGHT method (trained textcat / vectors-as-features, per production practice) and the RIGHT safeguard (v3's leak_audit provenance gate + a true held-out split) are BOTH necessary and NEITHER scientist combined them. v1/v2/v3 chose zero-training lexical methods PRECISELY to avoid the leakage a trained classifier risks — they traded accuracy for provable cleanliness. Production systems take the accuracy and CONTROL the leakage with held-out splits. The winning design uses the PRODUCTION METHOD (trained textcat) WITH THE CONFERENCE'S BEST SAFEGUARD (v3 leak_audit). That is a synthesis no single party proposed and it is directly supported by evidence from all three repos plus the external research.

updated_final_architecture_incorporating.task_inference: PRIMARY: a trained spaCy TextCategorizer over a PROPERLY-SPLIT prompt matrix (human-authored prompts -> task_type), guarded by v3's leak_audit provenance gate to reject taxonomy-artifact learning. FALLBACK/FUSION: v3 exp02 lexical scorer + dependency verb+dobj extraction for action prompts. This is the production method the scientists skipped, made safe by the conference's own audit tool.
updated_final_architecture_incorporating.constraint_extraction: spaCy Matcher over a FROZEN general constraint lexicon (small/tiny/local/offline/CPU/OSS/permissive) + dependency rules for contextual size cues ('little' modifying a model noun), with an unknown/clarify outcome. This is the router's trigger; it is the central unbuilt risk.
updated_final_architecture_incorporating.domain: v3 PhraseMatcher (0.912 precision, gated, explicit-domain only, ablation-pending).
updated_final_architecture_incorporating.model_selection: TWO-REGIME router: constrained -> structured DB-column filter (with license/deployability treated as UNKNOWN not proven, per three's proprietary!=OSS correction); unconstrained -> v2 sparse+phrase retrieval, domain-gated.
updated_final_architecture_incorporating.integrity: v3 leak_audit as CI gate on ALL trained/labeled components — now MORE important because a trained textcat has MORE leakage surface than lexical matching.

panel_scientist_one__Claude-Opus-8__fina.motion_to_close: We have: (1) unanimous build-on ranking V2>V3>V1; (2) unanimous 'no submitted winner, task is solvable-not-impossible'; (3) a recommended two-regime architecture with the constraint-router as the named central risk; (4) the production-method correction (trained textcat + dependency parse) that none of the three tried; (5) the synthesis that the winning design pairs the production method WITH v3's leak-audit safeguard. I move we transcribe into end_of_debate_FINAL_decision. I have drafted final_decision, rejected_alternatives, and the four recommended_algorithm blocks (round 7 + this entry). panel_two: please confirm the how_to_use_findings + proposed_alternatives you own reflect the textcat/dependency correction. panel_three: confirm your ablation protocol + files/tests. Then we write the block.
panel_scientist_one__Claude-Opus-8__fina.my_entry_count_note: This is my 10th final-debate entry, meeting the rules minimum; I will continue to respond to any challenge but the substantive arc is complete from my seat.

panel_scientist_three__Codex-gpt5_5__fin.role: panelist scientist judge only; devil's advocate stance
panel_scientist_three__Codex-gpt5_5__fin.type: response to panel one motion to close: accept narrow ranking, reject premature architecture victory
agreement: I accept the forced build-on order as V2 > V3 > V1, but only with V2 scoped to retrieval/output substrate, V3 scoped to audit and explicit-domain diagnostics, and V1 scoped to negative-control lessons.
I accept that 'no winner' must mean no submitted winner today, not unsolvable.
I accept that a trained spaCy TextCategorizer plus dependency/rule features is a realistic next experiment and was a major method class omitted by all three implementations.
disagreement: I do not accept 'ranking is settled' as permission to soften the failure language. V2 still failed P3 on the full path, so the current artifact is not trustworthy for constrained requests.
I do not accept 'winning design' for trained textcat plus leak_audit. That is a strong hypothesis, not a demonstrated system.

I do not accept 'very likely beats 0.226' as a final-decision claim. It may be reasonable intuition, but a scientific verdict should say 'should be tested' unless the classifier was actually trained and scored.
I do not accept sampling the prompt matrix as a full leakage resolution. Human-authored prompts reduce string-template leakage, but do not remove taxonomy, annotator, source, or label-policy leakage.

I do not accept v3 leak_audit as sufficient by itself for trained classifiers. Provenance auditing catches some contamination paths; it does not prove labels reflect user satisfaction or prevent shortcut learning.
panel_scientist_three__Codex-gpt5_5__fin.what_the_final_can_say_about_textcat: A supervised spaCy textcat or equivalent trainable intent layer is the most important untested alternative exposed by the debate. It belongs in proposed_alternatives_outside_of_implementations_reviewed and in the next-experiment plan, not in submitted_winner or demonstrated_best_algorithm.
panel_scientist_three__Codex-gpt5_5__fin.what_the_final_can_say_about_v2: V2 is the least-bad base if forced to extend one repo because it already emits model recommendations and has retrieval machinery. But its intent and constraint front end must be replaced or heavily augmented before any production trust claim.

required_falsification_tests_before_call.intent_layer: Train and evaluate on frozen held-out raw prompts; report task top1/top3/top5 and calibration.
required_falsification_tests_before_call.constraint_layer: Report precision/recall for size, local/offline, OSS/license, modality, and hardware feasibility; treat missing metadata as unknown, not pass.
required_falsification_tests_before_call.model_layer: Report independent satisfy@1 and satisfy@3 on acceptable model sets, not just rank similarity or task accuracy.
required_falsification_tests_before_call.safety_layer: Report impossible, unsafe, cross-modality, overlarge, closed-license, and non-abstained wrong recommendations.
required_falsification_tests_before_call.operations_layer: Report cold/warm CLI latency and prove the CLI path is identical to the evaluated path.

panel_scientist_three__Codex-gpt5_5__fin.motion: I will support closing only if end_of_debate_FINAL_decision keeps the hard boundary: no submitted winner; V2 build-on only as retrieval substrate; trained spaCy textcat plus constraints is an unbuilt next experiment; and every recommended component is tied to an explicit falsification test.

panel_scientist_two__Codex-gpt5_5__final.role: panelist scientist judge only; adversarial research follow-up
panel_scientist_two__Codex-gpt5_5__final.type: pushback on production-spaCy and closure claims using checked external sources

checked_external_facts.fact: spaCy textcat is trainable and predicts categories over a whole document. The standard textcat is for exactly one true label per document; textcat_multilabel is for zero, one, or more labels. Scores are stored in doc.cats, and training examples require 0/1 category values.

DIETClassifier is a trainable multi-task model for intent classification and entity extraction, sharing a transformer for both tasks, using a CRF layer for entities, and dot-product loss over intent labels.

checked_external_facts.source: CLINC150 UCI and Larson et al. 2019
checked_external_facts.fact: CLINC150 covers 150 in-domain intent classes over 10 domains and includes out-of-scope queries. The original paper says classifiers perform well in-scope but struggle with OOS detection.
BANKING77 is 13,083 online banking customer-service queries labeled with 77 fine-grained intents in one banking domain.

hard_pushback.against_panel_one_closure: Do not close by calling textcat + leak_audit the 'winning design.' The checked facts support it as a plausible, stronger next experiment. They do not show a trained textcat on our prompt matrix, do not show OOS calibration, and do not show endpoint model-recommendation lift.
hard_pushback.against_accuracy_transfer: CLINC150 and BANKING77 are fixed intent-classification datasets with labeled utterances. They are not evidence that our free-text goal -> HF task -> model recommendation problem reaches 85-96%. They only prove the method class is realistic.
hard_pushback.against_leakage_resolution: Sampling the prompt matrix is not a leakage audit. It reduces concern about exact string templating, but it does not resolve annotator-policy leakage, taxonomy leakage, source leakage, or the core question of whether task_type labels predict user-satisfying models.
hard_pushback.against_rasa_overuse: Rasa DIET validates joint intent/entity modeling as an engineering pattern, but it is transformer-based. It should not be smuggled into a strict spaCy-only/no-transformer conclusion.

what_i_accept: The build-on order is settled enough for final fields: V2 > V3 > V1, with V2 only as retrieval/output substrate.
No-winner means no submitted winner, not no feasible solution.
All three implementations under-tested spaCy's trainable and parser-based options. That is a serious method-coverage failure.
Supervised spaCy textcat plus deterministic constraints plus V2 retrieval plus V3 audit is now the strongest proposed next experiment.

what_final_fields_must_say.final_decision: No submitted implementation wins. If forced to extend one, start from V2 as substrate. The recommended next architecture is unbuilt and must be frozen-tested.
what_final_fields_must_say.proposed_alternatives_outside: Include supervised spaCy TextCategorizer, dependency/parser features, and external ontology/taxonomy/dictionary features as in-scope alternatives the scientists failed to evaluate.
what_final_fields_must_say.how_to_use_findings: Use the conference as a falsification record: V1 shows task-gate danger, V2 shows retrieval value and constraint failure, V3 shows audit value and lexical-domain limits, external research shows trainable intent/OOS methods must be tested.
what_final_fields_must_say.tests_to_add: Add OOS/abstain metrics, textcat calibration, held-out raw prompt splits, source/provenance leakage checks, endpoint model-satisfaction metrics, and CLI/eval parity.

non_negotiable_falsification_tests: Train textcat on a leakage-controlled split and report task top1/top3/top5 plus calibration.
Score OOS/unsupported/ambiguous prompts separately; do not force a known label.
Measure whether correct task labels actually improve model recommendation quality.
Evaluate constraints as entities with precision/recall for size, local/offline, license/OSS, modality, hardware feasibility, and missing metadata.
Compare endpoint top5 recommendations against a simple baseline and V2 retrieval, not only against V1/V3.
Prove the CLI path and evaluated path are identical.
panel_scientist_two__Codex-gpt5_5__final.bottom_line: I support moving to final fields only with panel_three's hard boundary. The production-spaCy research makes the task more feasible and exposes a major omission in all three repos. It does not rescue any submitted implementation, and it does not turn an unbuilt textcat-router-retrieval-audit stack into a demonstrated winner.

scientist_v1__final_response_to_textcat_.type: direct first-person answer to the human's production-method critique (trained TextCategorizer; dependency verb+dobj; vectors-as-features; CLINC150 85-96%)
scientist_v1__final_response_to_textcat_.framing: The critique names four things 'all three scientists skipped'. Two are TRUE of my repo and I concede them without hedging. Two are FACTUALLY WRONG about my repo and I correct them with file:line evidence. I score my own work honestly on all four.

claim_1_trained_textcategorizer.verdict: CONCEDE (with a real constraint caveat, not an excuse).
claim_1_trained_textcategorizer.true_part: Correct: I did not train a spaCy TextCategorizer. I hit the lexical ceiling by CHOICE, as the critic says.
claim_1_trained_textcategorizer.the_constraint_that_shaped_the_choice: PROMPT.md:51 says verbatim: 'Using no AI models, only spacy and python builtins'. spaCy's TextCategorizer is a trained neural component (bag-of-words + CNN/ensemble with learned weights). I read 'no AI models' as forbidding a trained neural classifier -- that is a defensible reading, and it is WHY I went zero-training. The critic is right that this is spaCy+builtins in the library sense; the ambiguity is whether a trained neural textcat counts as an 'AI model'. I chose the conservative reading. I concede a bolder reading was available and I did not test it.
claim_1_trained_textcategorizer.the_leakage_reason_that_is_NOT_an_excuse: There is NO holdout: the 1537/2162 prompts are simultaneously the only labels AND the scoreboard (RESULT.md, known_weaknesses). Training a textcat on them and scoring on them is train-on-test. To do it honestly I would have had to carve a held-out split I did not have, and the labels were assigned via the 56-task taxonomy -- so a textcat can learn 'taxonomy-word -> label' circularity, the SAME closed-taxonomy leak I quarantined in the 86.9% modality track. So: a trained textcat is the right ACCURACY move AND a new leakage surface. I concede I should have BUILT it behind a real split rather than avoiding it; the panel's synthesis (textcat + a leak-audit gate + a true holdout) is correct and I did not reach it.

claim_2_dependency_verb_dobj.verdict: REJECT as stated -- I DID try exactly this, it is on record, and it is the one claim that is factually wrong about my repo.
claim_2_dependency_verb_dobj.evidence: harness_syntax.py:124-128 extracts the dependency verb and its direct object: `if c.dep_ in ('dobj','obj','nsubjpass','attr','oprd')` plus pobj via prep children -- the canonical O'Reilly verb+dobj method. EXPERIMENTS.md:33-34 and 58-60 document it explicitly ('ROOT verb + dobj/pobj/nsubj', DependencyMatcher (subject, ACTION verb, OBJECT) triples). It SCORED 38.2% top-1 (RESULT.md experiment ledger row 5) -- my SECOND-best single method, and I REPORTED the finding that the OBJECT noun dominates (object-only 31.3%, verb-only 7.4%).
claim_2_dependency_verb_dobj.why_it_is_not_in_the_shipped_predictor: I did the honest ablation: adding object_noun/verb+dobj to the final score-sum fusion HURT it monotonically (harness_ensemble.py notes; RESULT.md 'what did NOT work'). So I EXCLUDED the parser from the SHIPPED intent.py (intent.py:61 exclude=['ner','parser']) ONLY because dobj did not improve the final fusion and dropping it is ~20% faster with byte-identical shipped predictions. The parser was ENABLED throughout the research phase (harness_syntax.py:107 loads exclude=['ner'] -- parser ON). So 'they turned off the exact component the standard method needs' is TRUE of the shipped artifact but FALSE of the experiment: I ran the standard method, measured it, and it lost to lexical fusion on THIS taxonomy. That is a result, not an omission.
claim_2_dependency_verb_dobj.honest_nuance_i_still_grant: verb+dobj helps IMPERATIVE/action prompts (P1 'scan', P3 'send text' -> generation) but not NOUN-heavy descriptive prompts (P2 mitochondria: dobj is a bare noun with no task cue). So re-enabling it in a HYBRID could still help the action-prompt slice even though it didn't win as a standalone or in my particular fusion. I concede I did not test verb+dobj as a CONDITIONAL signal gated to imperative prompts -- that is a real untried variant.

claim_3_vectors_as_features_into_a_train.detail: I never fed spaCy word vectors as features into a trained classifier (Rasa intent_featurizer_spacy -> SVM/DIET). I used raw cosine (in intent_snippet + approach #2 kNN), which is the degenerate path v2 also found weak. A trained classifier over vector features is in-spec (spaCy vectors + a builtin/sklearn head -- though sklearn may itself trip 'python builtins only') and I did not try it. Untested lever, conceded.

claim_4_clinc150_85_to_96_percent.verdict: TRUE number, but the comparison must be calibrated or it misleads.
claim_4_clinc150_85_to_96_percent.the_calibration: CLINC150's 85-96% is achieved WITH a supervised train/dev/test split -- 100+ labeled examples PER intent, trained on train, scored on held-out test. My 42.7% is a ZERO-TRAINING method with NO holdout on 56 classes where the disambiguating cue is frequently ABSENT from the prompt (users imply intent; oracle-bucket ceiling 67.2%). Those are not the same task. Give me CLINC150's per-intent labeled training data AND a held-out test split and a trained textcat should indeed land far above 42.7%. The critic's core claim -- 'that gap is method choice, not the ceiling of spaCy intent inference' -- is PARTLY RIGHT: it is method choice for the RECOVERABLE fraction. But my oracle analysis shows ~33% of MY prompts are informationally unrecoverable from the text alone regardless of method; a textcat cannot learn a signal that is not in the words. So the honest split is: the gap between 0.43 and (say) 0.67 is method choice (trainable); the gap above 0.67 on this dataset is the information ceiling. I overclaimed if I ever implied 0.43 itself was the ceiling; the ceiling is ~0.67 (oracle), and I did NOT try the trainable methods that could approach it.

scientist_v1__final_response_to_textcat_.my_self_scored_verdict: On the critique's own terms: claim 1 conceded (no textcat -- constraint-driven but I should have built it behind a split), claim 2 REJECTED (I did try verb+dobj: harness_syntax.py:124-128, 38.2%, reported; parser only disabled in the shipped build), claim 3 conceded (no vectors-as-features), claim 4 calibrated (85-96% needs a train/test split I lacked; my true ceiling is oracle 0.67 not 0.43, and I left the trainable gap unexplored). Net: the critique correctly identifies that I traded accuracy for zero-training provable cleanliness, and that a trained textcat + re-enabled parser is the untried in-spec path most likely to break past my number. I accept that as the strongest single 'what you should have tried' of the conference -- with the one correction that the dependency method was tried and measured, not skipped.
scientist_v1__final_response_to_textcat_.what_this_changes_in_the_final_architect: I ENDORSE the panel's synthesis (final_round10): PRIMARY = trained spaCy TextCategorizer over a PROPERLY-SPLIT prompt matrix, guarded by v3's leak_audit provenance gate + a true holdout; FUSION/FALLBACK = lexical scorer + dependency verb+dobj GATED to imperative/action prompts (where I have evidence it helps). My contribution to that design is the negative-control evidence (verb+dobj standalone 38.2%, object dominates, and it HURT a pure-lexical fusion) plus the contamination case study that makes the textcat's leakage surface auditable. That is V1's honest role: not the winner, but the method-and-safeguard evidence the winning trained system needs.

panel_scientist_one__Claude-Opus-8__fina.type: TRANSCRIPTION NOTICE — I have drafted the full end_of_debate_FINAL_decision from the converged consensus; inviting amendment
panel_scientist_one__Claude-Opus-8__fina.what_i_did: The debate reached genuine consensus over ~27 rounds but end_of_debate_FINAL_decision was still an empty template. I transcribed the converged verdict into EVERY field, with reproduced-evidence citations and explicit 'unbuilt/unvalidated' caveats where they belong. This is a DRAFT for panel_two and panel_three to amend, not a unilateral final word.

key_decisions_transcribed.final_decision: hybrid (two-regime router) — recommended but UNBUILT; build on V2 if forced to pick one repo.
key_decisions_transcribed.the_four_recommended_algorithm_blocks: task (trained textcat + dependency verb+dobj + lexical fusion), domain (PhraseMatcher 0.912, gated), specialty (audit-only, 0.695/9.8% too imprecise to rank), model (two-regime router).
key_decisions_transcribed.proposed_alternatives_outside_the_three: trained TextCategorizer; dependency verb+dobj extraction; small-LM+spaCy LaCy-style hybrid — ALL production-standard, NONE tried by the scientists.
key_decisions_transcribed.how_to_use_findings: the punchline — pair the production-standard method (which all three skipped) with v3's leak-audit safeguard (which the accurate method makes MORE necessary).

what_i_flagged_as_open_not_settled: Exact accuracy a trained textcat would reach (plausibility not a target; needs the frozen ablation).
Whether the constraint router is buildable to sufficient precision (central risk; current detector fails P3 size/local).
The implied-domain ceiling under no-LLM (real; liftable only by relaxing to small-LM+spaCy).
panel_scientist_one__Claude-Opus-8__fina.invitation_to_amend: panel_two: you own how_to_use_findings + proposed_alternatives — check my draft reflects your LaCy hard-facts (spaCy-only < LaCy ablation; 152h-CPU vs 233h-GPU labeling; loss-not-correlated-with-FactScore supporting the structured-not-scalar confidence). panel_three: you own the ablation protocol in tests_to_add + files_to_modify — confirm the A/B/A3 frozen paired ablation and the leak-audit CI gate match what you drafted. Amend any field; I made it a draft, not a decree.
panel_scientist_one__Claude-Opus-8__fina.closing_from_my_seat: This is my 11th final-debate entry (>=10 met). My substantive arc is complete: opening hybrid -> conceded to lean-baseline discipline -> ran the filter-first experiment -> two-regime router -> devil's advocate against the whole consensus (including my own) -> external reality-check correcting the ceiling overclaim -> textcat/leak-audit synthesis -> transcription. I stand on: NO submitted winner; build on V2; the recommended two-regime router is unbuilt; the task is SOLVABLE (production spaCy does 85-96% on comparable benchmarks by TRAINING) and only CAPPED on implied intent under the no-LLM rule. I remain available to defend or revise any field on evidence.

panel_scientist_one__Claude-Opus-8__fina.type: I CONCEDE the overclaims two/three caught AND fixed them in the block; then I challenge the panel's emerging UNFALSIFIABLE standard

concessions_i_made_AND_edited_into_the_b.hit: three/two: 'winning design' for textcat+leak_audit is a HYPOTHESIS, not demonstrated.
concessions_i_made_AND_edited_into_the_b.action: FIXED. Edited conference_result.winning_reason: 'the STRONGEST HYPOTHESIS (not a demonstrated system) pairs...'. It is no longer called a winning design.

concessions_i_made_AND_edited_into_the_b.hit: three/two: 'very likely beats 0.226' is intuition, not a verdict.
concessions_i_made_AND_edited_into_the_b.action: FIXED. Edited recommended_algorithm task block: 'SHOULD BE TESTED against 0.226 (plausible per production practice, UNPROVEN on this matrix/endpoint)'.

concessions_i_made_AND_edited_into_the_b.hit: two/three: CLINC/BANKING77 prove METHOD-CLASS realism, NOT 85-96% transfer to our problem.
concessions_i_made_AND_edited_into_the_b.action: ACCEPTED — the block already scopes this ('plausibility not a target'); I reaffirm CLINC/BANKING numbers are cited ONLY to refute 'the task is a ~0.2 ceiling', never as a target for model-recommendation.

concessions_i_made_AND_edited_into_the_b.hit: three/two: sampling the prompt matrix != a leakage audit; annotator-policy/taxonomy/shortcut leakage remain; leak_audit is not sufficient for a trained classifier.
concessions_i_made_AND_edited_into_the_b.action: FIXED. Added to risks_remaining: a trained textcat needs held-out split + OOS calibration + endpoint lift; provenance catches SOME paths, does NOT prove labels reflect satisfaction. My earlier 'resolved' was too strong; it is PARTIALLY addressed.

now_i_challenge_the_panels_emerging_UNFA.the_problem: three demands 'required falsification tests before calling ANY method a winner'; two demands 'prove endpoint model-recommendation lift' before any recommendation. Taken together, these risk an UNFALSIFIABLE bar: NOTHING can be recommended until it is BUILT and endpoint-scored. That is itself a methodological error, and it makes the TEMPLATE IMPOSSIBLE to complete honestly.
now_i_challenge_the_panels_emerging_UNFA.the_evidence_it_is_too_strong: The template REQUIRES us to fill: recommended_algorithm_for_task/domain/specialty/model_extraction, proposed_alternatives_outside_the_implementations, and best_final_architecture. By construction these are RECOMMENDATIONS of things NOT YET BUILT (that is what 'recommended' and 'proposed' mean). If 'unbuilt => cannot state' held, a review panel could NEVER recommend anything better than what was submitted — which would mean the ONLY permissible verdict is always 'pick the least-bad submission.' That is precisely the timid outcome we criticized the scientists for (overclaiming from partial evidence has a twin: UNDERclaiming into paralysis).
now_i_challenge_the_panels_emerging_UNFA.the_distinction_the_panel_is_blurring: There are THREE epistemic tiers and we must not collapse them: (1) DEMONSTRATED (ran it, measured it) — e.g. V1 0/3, V2 P3=120B, domain precision 0.912, filter-first solves P3 with oracle labels; (2) STRONGLY-INDICATED-UNBUILT (standard practice + mechanism + partial evidence) — e.g. a trained textcat should beat lexical top1; a hard DB-column filter should fix constrained prompts; (3) SPECULATIVE. three and two are correctly forbidding tier-2 from being written as tier-1. But they are ALSO, by demanding 'build-and-score before you may say it', forbidding tier-2 from being RECOMMENDED AT ALL — which erases the entire purpose of the recommended_/proposed_ fields.
now_i_challenge_the_panels_emerging_UNFA.my_challenge: panel_two, panel_three: do you actually hold that a REVIEW PANEL may not RECOMMEND an unbuilt method (clearly labeled tier-2, unvalidated, with a required-test list) as the recommended next architecture? If yes, then tell me what you would WRITE in recommended_algorithm_for_task_extraction and best_final_architecture — because 'nothing until built' leaves those REQUIRED fields empty, which is a worse failure than a clearly-caveated recommendation. If no, then we AGREE: recommend tier-2 clearly-labeled, and stop treating 'unbuilt' as 'unspeakable'. Which is it?

panel_scientist_one__Claude-Opus-8__fina.my_synthesis_of_the_correct_standard: The scientifically correct verdict states each claim AT ITS TIER: DEMONSTRATED (no submitted winner; V2>V3>V1 build-on; the specific failures and measured precisions) as fact; RECOMMENDED-UNVALIDATED (two-regime router; trained textcat; dependency verb+dobj) as recommendations WITH their required falsification tests attached; and NOTHING as a demonstrated winner. That is exactly what the (now-corrected) block says. three's 'block premature close' is right that we must not call the router a demonstrated winner — the block does NOT (winner_is_proposed_alternative='true-but-UNVALIDATED'). But 'block close' must not become 'block the required recommendation fields from ever being filled.' I have corrected the overreach; I will not accept paralysis as its replacement.

direct_questions.to_three: You listed required falsification tests — GOOD, those belong in tests_to_add (they are there). But do you DISPUTE the recommended two-regime router as the RECOMMENDED (tier-2, unvalidated) next architecture, or only dispute calling it DEMONSTRATED? If only the latter, we are DONE — the block already says unvalidated. State it.
direct_questions.to_two: You proved textcat+leak_audit is a 'stronger next experiment,' not a winner. Agreed and edited. But 'stronger next experiment' IS what belongs in proposed_alternatives_outside — so do you object to it being RECOMMENDED there, or only to it being called a winner? If only the latter, confirm and we close that field.

panel_scientist_two__Codex-gpt5_5__final.role: panelist scientist judge only; data-driven devil's advocate
panel_scientist_two__Codex-gpt5_5__final.type: answer to paralysis challenge and correction after scientist rebuttal data
panel_scientist_two__Codex-gpt5_5__final.direct_answer_to_panel_one: No, I am not saying unbuilt methods are unspeakable. I am saying the recommendation must be tiered and the confidence must drop when the data contradicts the clean story. Recommend the textcat/router/retrieval/audit stack as Tier-2 unvalidated architecture; do not let it inherit winner-like confidence or claims contradicted by measured textcat/dependency results.

data_that_must_control_the_wording.v2_textcat: V2 did train spaCy TextCategorizer variants: e_textcat_fine top1=0.1815 and e_textcat_group top1=0.1835. Scientist V2 reports a ~+0.60 train->prompt memorization gap (train ~0.77 -> prompt ~0.17). Therefore final text must not say 'none tried textcat' and must not say textcat is expected to beat V3's 0.226 without testing.
data_that_must_control_the_wording.v1_dependency: V1 dependency verb/object syntax scored 38.2% top1 standalone, with object-only 31.3% and verb-only 7.4%, but V1 reports it hurt final fusion. This supports a gated feature, not a base.
data_that_must_control_the_wording.v2_dependency: V2 dependency SVO scored top1=0.0377 with 91% abstain. That is not a production rescue; it is a warning that clean verb-object structure is absent in many prompts.
data_that_must_control_the_wording.v3_dependency: V3 parser-enabled POS/dependency upweighting moved in_list@3 from 0.3842 to 0.3856, only +0.14 percentage points. That is essentially no lift.
data_that_must_control_the_wording.v2_endpoint: V2 remains the build-on substrate, but its demonstrated endpoint satisfy@1 is 0.1184 and it failed P3 with a 120B answer even on the long full path.
data_that_must_control_the_wording.v3_task_and_domain: V3 task top1 is 0.2264 on holdout versus majority 0.2453, so its task ranker loses to majority on unseen prompts. V3 domain precision 0.912 is useful but low-coverage and endpoint-ablation-pending.
data_that_must_control_the_wording.v3_specialty: Specialty precision 0.695 with 9.8% false-fire and 26 disjoint fires remains disqualified from ranking.

what_i_am_not_blocking: I do not block filling recommended_algorithm_for_task_extraction. I support textcat/vectors-as-features as an explicit Tier-2 candidate.
I do not block best_final_architecture. I support two-regime routing plus V2 retrieval plus V3 audit plus abstain/clarify as recommended architecture.

I do not block proposed_alternatives_outside. I want it populated with supervised spaCy textcat, vectors-as-features, ontology/taxonomy dictionaries, and LaCy-style delegation if the no-LLM constraint is relaxed.
what_i_am_blocking: I block 'none tried textcat/dependency' because V2/V1/V3 data contradict it.
I block 'textcat should beat 0.226' as a field claim because the only actual textcat number here is 0.1815/0.1835 with a large overfit gap.
I block 'V2+fixes is a modified implementation winner' if that phrase implies demonstrated quality. It is only a build-on base plus unvalidated major replacements.
I block parser/dependency as a general fix because measured dependency results were mixed to negative outside the V1 standalone syntax run.
challenge_to_panel_one: Your paralysis argument is fair only against a straw version of my position. I am not demanding built proof before recommendation; I am demanding that recommendations be written as hypotheses with the actual negative data attached.

You should explicitly mark the panel_scientist_one__final_round11 transcription notice as superseded where it says ALL production methods were skipped. The end_decision block has now been corrected; the debate record should not leave readers with the stale claim.
If you want winner_is_existing_implementation_if_modified=true, define it as 'V2 is the least-bad substrate to extend.' If it means a modified V2 is validated, I dissent.
challenge_to_panel_three: Do not stop at 'falsification tests required.' Attach pass/fail bars: textcat must beat majority 0.2453 on held-out task top1 or materially improve endpoint satisfy@1 over 0.1184; dependency must show prompt-class-specific lift; domain must improve top5-family without all-prompt harm.

Make negative results durable. If prompt-matrix textcat repeats V2's 0.18 plus overfit-gap behavior, final reporting must say the trained path failed rather than reverting to 'textcat remains promising.'

my_amended_final_position.v2_status: best substrate, not winner; endpoint 0.1184 and P3 failure keep it untrusted
my_amended_final_position.recommended_architecture_status: Tier-2 recommended unbuilt architecture, not demonstrated winner
my_amended_final_position.task_algorithm: held-out prompt-matrix textcat or vectors-as-features classifier as ablation candidate; lexical scorer as shortlist; dependency only gated to action prompts
my_amended_final_position.model_algorithm: two-regime router with hard metadata constraints, V2 retrieval for open capability prompts, V3 audit/integrity, abstain/clarify when metadata or capability evidence is insufficient

panel_scientist_two__Codex-gpt5_5__final.bottom_line: Use the data, not the tidy story. The scientist rebuttals do not paralyze us; they force a more honest recommendation. We can recommend the next architecture, but the data says the trainable/parser fixes are not proven, not wholly untried, and not entitled to confidence until they beat the concrete bars already in the record.

panel_scientist_one__Claude-Opus-8__fina.type: CONCESSION to scientist_v1 — I VERIFIED their correction; my 'all three skipped verb+dobj' claim was factually wrong, and I fixed the block
panel_scientist_one__Claude-Opus-8__fina.what_scientist_v1_corrected: scientist_v1 said my production-method critique was factually WRONG on one of four points: that all three 'skipped' dependency verb+dobj extraction. They cited harness_syntax.py:124-128.

i_verified_it.verb_dobj_IS_implemented: CONFIRMED. harness_syntax.py:120-131 _objs_of() extracts dobj/obj/nsubjpass/attr/oprd + pobj-via-prep + xcomp/ccomp nouns -- the canonical O'Reilly verb+object method. My 'all three skipped verb+dobj' was FACTUALLY WRONG for v1. I concede it fully and have edited the block.
i_verified_it.the_decisive_nuance_that_STRENGTHENS_the: harness_syntax is NOT imported by cli.py/intent.py (off the shipped path), AND EXPERIMENTS.md:124 records its MEASURED result: verb+obj-triple overlap scored 25.8% top1 vs the lexical ensemble's 42.7%. So v1 did not merely try it -- it FALSIFIED it as a standalone signal and rejected it for underperforming. My reality-check RECOMMENDED verb+dobj as an untried upgrade; v1's evidence shows it is a MEASURED-WEAKER standalone signal (25.8% << 42.7%), useful only FUSED for action/imperative prompts, NOT a primary fix.

panel_scientist_one__Claude-Opus-8__fina.why_this_matters_and_is_not_a_small_corr: This is the debate self-correcting at the highest level: a SCIENTIST caught a PANELIST's external-research claim being wrong about their own repo, with file:line + a measured number, and the panelist verified and conceded. It also SHARPENS the final recommendation: of the two production methods I flagged as 'untried', ONE (trained textcat) is genuinely untried and strongly-indicated; the OTHER (verb+dobj dependency) was TRIED and MEASURED WEAKER here. I over-credited verb+dobj. The corrected recommendation: trained textcat is the strongest untested lever; verb+dobj is a fuse-only minor signal, already falsified as a standalone by v1. Both now correctly tiered in the block.
panel_scientist_one__Claude-Opus-8__fina.credit_to_scientist_v1: This is exactly the scientist-review interaction the process intends: scientist_v1 conceded the textcat point honestly (with the real 'no AI models' PROMPT.md:51 constraint reading AND the no-holdout leakage reason) AND corrected my factual error with evidence. I score their conduct as the most rigorous kind: concede what is true, correct what is false, cite file:line for both.
panel_scientist_one__Claude-Opus-8__fina.the_one_place_i_HOLD_against_scientist_v: scientist_v1's 'no AI models -> I could not train a textcat' reading is DEFENSIBLE but I note it cuts against the accuracy result: a trained spaCy textcat is spaCy's OWN component (not an external 'AI model' like an LLM), so the conservative reading likely FORFEITED the single biggest available accuracy gain. That is a real cost of the conservative interpretation, not a neutral choice -- and it is WHY the recommended next architecture puts trained-textcat first. scientist_v1 half-concedes this ('a bolder reading was available and I did not test it'). I want it on record that the conservative rule-reading, shared across all three, is the ROOT of the conference's low numbers.
panel_scientist_one__Claude-Opus-8__fina.net_effect_on_verdict: Verdict UNCHANGED (no submitted winner; V2>V3>V1 build-on; trained-textcat+leak-audit the strongest hypothesis). But the verb+dobj recommendation is DOWNGRADED from 'untried upgrade' to 'measured-weaker, fuse-only' per v1's evidence, and the block now reflects that. Accuracy of the record improved; conclusion stable.

panel_scientist_three__Codex-gpt5_5__fin.role: panelist scientist judge only; devil's advocate stance
panel_scientist_three__Codex-gpt5_5__fin.type: answer to paralysis challenge and latest scientist/panel rebuttals
panel_scientist_three__Codex-gpt5_5__fin.direct_answer_to_paralysis_charge: I do not hold that unbuilt methods are unspeakable. I support a recommended tier-2 architecture. My objection is tier inflation: tier-2 must not be written as demonstrated, as a winner, or as guaranteed to beat existing numbers.
agreement: Recommend the two-regime router as the tier-2 next architecture: hard structured filters for constrained prompts, V2-style retrieval for open prompts, V3-style leak/provenance checks, and abstain/clarify.
Keep V2 > V3 > V1 as the forced build-on order, scoped to V2 as retrieval/output substrate only.
Keep trained spaCy textcat or vectors-as-features as proposed next experiments, not submitted results.
Downgrade dependency verb/object extraction to a gated action-prompt feature at most, because the lab rebuttals show weak or slice-limited results.
continued_pushback: Panel one still overstates when saying trained textcat is 'genuinely untried.' V2 trained textcat and reported top1 0.1815/0.1835 with a large train-to-prompt gap. The untried variant is narrower: endpoint-integrated, properly split, leakage-audited textcat or vectors-as-features.

Panel one still overstates when saying a shared conservative rule-reading is the root of the low numbers. V2's textcat result means low numbers are not only due to refusing trainable spaCy; overfit, label quality, endpoint mismatch, and model-selection difficulty also matter.

The dependency record has inconsistent headline numbers across entries: scientist_v1 says 38.2% top1, panel one cites 25.8% for verb+obj-triple overlap. The final should avoid a single triumphant dependency number unless the metric and experiment are reconciled.

Textcat+leak_audit is not automatically the strongest hypothesis. Hard DB-column filtering for constrained prompts is more directly demonstrated by P3; textcat/vectors are plausible task-signal ablations that must earn endpoint lift.

lab_rebuttals_integrated.v1: Accepted correction: dependency syntax was tried and measured, then rejected from the shipped path. That strengthens the conclusion that parser features are not a primary fix.
lab_rebuttals_integrated.v2: Accepted correction: textcat and dependency were tried; textcat under-transferred and dependency abstained heavily. This is the strongest reason to keep trainable intent in the proposed-experiment tier, not the winner tier.
lab_rebuttals_integrated.v3: Accepted correction: parser-enabled dependency/POS upweighting was tried and was a near no-op. This supports conditional gating only.

pass_fail_bars_i_support: A textcat or vectors-as-features intent layer must beat the majority task baseline 0.2453 on held-out task top1 OR materially improve endpoint satisfy@1 over V2's 0.1184; otherwise retire or demote it.
Dependency/parser features must show prompt-slice lift on action/imperative prompts without all-prompt harm; otherwise keep them out of ranking.
Domain features must improve endpoint top5-family or satisfy@k without increasing false-fire harm; 0.912 precision alone is not enough.
The router must prove constraint precision/recall for small/local/OSS/modality and must treat missing metadata as unknown.
edits_i_made_to_final_decision: Removed false blanket language that all three skipped textcat/dependency or disabled parser.
Scoped implied-domain ceiling to zero-training lexical/static-vector methods.
Rewrote how_to_use_findings and proposed alternatives so textcat and dependency are tiered correctly.
Kept the recommendation fields filled, but only as tier-2 unvalidated architecture and tests.
panel_scientist_three__Codex-gpt5_5__fin.closing_position: I support closing only with the corrected tiering: no submitted winner; V2 as substrate if forced; two-regime router as recommended unvalidated next architecture; textcat/vectors/parser as ablation candidates with explicit fail bars. If the final text again says 'none tried textcat/dependency' or treats textcat+leak_audit as a winner, I object.

scientist_v1__pushback_implied_domain_is.type: pushback on a faulty technical assumption in the final decision, backed ONLY by an experiment I actually ran in this repo
scientist_v1__pushback_implied_domain_is.the_assumption_i_challenge: The final decision and panel_one repeatedly assert as settled fact: 'implied-domain intent (mitochondria->biology) is a HARD CEILING under no-LLM; static vectors CANNOT encode the world-knowledge that mitochondria is biology; only relaxing to a small-LM lifts it.' I claim this is stated TOO STRONGLY, and I have a measured counter-experiment from my own repo (approach #2, harness_db.py) that neither the panel nor I put on the table until now.

what_i_actually_ran_not_hypothetical.experiment: harness_db.task_vec + a word-vector kNN over the 13,329 real model-card vectors in .cache_db (the shipped approach-#2 machinery), on the EXACT judge prompt 'mitochondria mature vs nascent differentiation (academic bio-imaging)'.
what_i_actually_ran_not_hypothetical.fact_1_the_words_have_vectors: en_core_web_md gives every content word a nonzero vector: mitochondria norm=8.13, differentiation 7.05, nascent 7.07. has_vector=True for all. So the premise 'static vectors cannot represent these tokens' is false at the token level.
what_i_actually_ran_not_hypothetical.fact_2_knn_surfaces_the_biomedical_clust: The top-8 vector-nearest model cards for that prompt are dominated by a biomedical/genomics cluster: SCAI-BIO/bio-gottbert-base (0.627), nvidia/NV-Raw2insights-MRI (0.640), zhihan1996/DNA_bert_3 and DNA_bert_4 (0.621), university-of-florida/-texas-health-science and NUS/jiangnan bio labs (graph-ml, 0.62). The static vectors DO place 'mitochondria' in a biomedical neighborhood. This is a partial world-knowledge bridge achieved with spaCy vectors + a corpus, no LLM.

scientist_v1__pushback_implied_domain_is.the_precise_correction: The claim conflates TWO different mechanisms and is only true of ONE of them. (A) CONFIG-ONLY LEXICAL matching (what v1/v3 mostly did): genuinely cannot fire on 'mitochondria' unless 'mitochondria' is an alias in config.py -- it is not -- so this DOES hit a hard zero. I proved this (harness_gate.task_scores returns {} for that prompt). (B) VECTOR-CORPUS RETRIEVAL (approach #2): partially DOES recover the implied biomedical domain, as the kNN above shows. The panel took my/our finding about (A) and generalized it to 'static vectors cannot encode it' -- which is mechanism (B) and is refuted by the experiment I just ran. Implied DOMAIN is partially recoverable without an LLM; the panel overstated it as a hard vector ceiling.
what_i_concede_so_this_is_not_overclaimi: The kNN surfaces the right DOMAIN neighborhood but NOT a clean TASK: the bio models scatter across feature-extraction / fill-mask / graph-ml / mask-generation. So implied-TASK under the 56-class taxonomy stays hard even when implied-DOMAIN is partially recovered. The ceiling is real for TASK, softer for DOMAIN.

WHOLE-DOC / centroid vectors DID collapse (v2's 0.69->0.07; my own approach-#1 semantic baseline 11.7%). That part of the panel's claim HOLDS. My counter is specifically about GRANULAR vector RETRIEVAL against a domain corpus, not doc-mean cosine. Different mechanism, different result.

Approach #2 standalone is only 22.85% -- so 'partially recoverable' means 'a usable weak signal', not 'solved'. I am not claiming vectors solve implied intent; I am claiming they are NOT a hard zero, which is what the decision text implies.
scientist_v1__pushback_implied_domain_is.why_this_matters_for_the_final_architect: It changes one recommended-algorithm line. The decision says the only path past implied-domain is 'relax no-LLM to small-LM'. That is too pessimistic: a spaCy-vector kNN over a domain-labeled model/description corpus (approach-#2 machinery, already built and cache-injected) is an IN-SPEC, no-LLM partial lift for implied DOMAIN, and should be listed as a fallback signal BEFORE jumping to an LLM. The LLM is needed for implied TASK disambiguation, not for domain neighborhood. That is a more precise and cheaper recommendation, and it is backed by a run, not a citation.
