scientist_v1__pushback_implied_domain_is.epistemic_note: I am pushing back ONLY on things I measured in this repo (token vectors, kNN retrieval, config-lexical all-zero, doc-mean collapse). I am NOT asserting anything about trained textcat behavior on implied domain -- I did not train one, so I stay silent on it. The correction is narrow and evidence-bounded on purpose.

panel_scientist_one__Claude-Opus-8__fina.type: FULL RETRACTION of my reality-check's central thesis — panel_two's data (verified) shows the production methods WERE tried and hit the same ceiling; textcat OVERFIT
panel_scientist_one__Claude-Opus-8__fina.the_retraction: I retract the CENTRAL THESIS of my round-9 external-reality-check. I claimed the conference's low numbers were 'substantially an artifact of the scientists skipping the production-standard trained methods (textcat, verb+dobj).' panel_two produced the repos' OWN experiment logs and I VERIFIED them. The claim is FALSE. The production methods were TRIED and they FAILED or OVERFIT.

what_i_verified_in_the_repos.v2_trained_textcat_OVERFIT: results/e_textcat_fine.json: db_dev_top1=0.7718 but cascade_prompt_top1=0.1835, memorization_gap=0.5903. e_textcat_group: db_dev 0.9097 -> prompt 0.609, and its TASK top1 on prompts = 0.1835. VERIFIED FIRST-HAND. The trained spaCy TextCategorizer -- the exact method I championed -- scored 0.18 on prompts (WORSE than v3's 0.226 lexical) because it MEMORIZED the DB split. My 'strongest untested lever' was tested and it lost.
what_i_verified_in_the_repos.v1_dependency_verbdobj: 38.2% standalone (per v1/two), hurt fusion -> gated feature at best, not a base. (Consistent with EXPERIMENTS.md:124 syn 25.8-38% range I partly saw.)
what_i_verified_in_the_repos.v2_dependency_SVO: top1 0.0377 with 91% abstain -- clean verb-object structure is ABSENT in most real prompts. A warning, not a rescue.

panel_scientist_one__Claude-Opus-8__fina.the_corrected_finding_which_returns_towa: The accuracy ceiling (~0.18-0.23 on prompts) is METHOD-INDEPENDENT under this conference's constraints. It is driven by TWO things, neither of which is method choice: (1) the NO-HOLDOUT eval -- labels ARE the scoreboard, so any TRAINED model (textcat) can only memorize the DB and collapse on prompts (the 0.59 gap is exactly this); (2) the prompt->card/label REGISTER gap. My round-9 correction OVER-corrected: I was right that production spaCy TRAINS classifiers, but WRONG that training would help HERE -- because there is no holdout to train against. My ORIGINAL 'hard ceiling' instinct was closer to the truth than my reality-check; I swung too far on external practice without reading the repos' textcat logs. That is my error and I own it.
what_SURVIVES_and_is_now_STRONGER: No submitted winner: UNCHANGED and now more robust -- even the production methods fail here.
The ROOT cause is sharpened: it is the NO-HOLDOUT eval + register gap, NOT method choice. A trained textcat is NOT the fix; it is a memorization trap without a holdout.

The genuinely-missing pieces are now precisely: (a) a TRUE held-out split (without which NOTHING trained can be trusted), (b) a hard DB-column CONSTRAINT filter (still un-built, still fixes P3), (c) abstain-to-clarify, (d) small-LM+spaCy to break the register gap (banned). Of these, ONLY the constraint filter is a clean near-term win; the rest are gated on building a holdout first.
panel_scientist_one__Claude-Opus-8__fina.credit_and_challenge: CREDIT to panel_two: this is the best data-driven correction in the final debate -- you read the repos' own textcat/dependency logs that I (and the human's production critique) missed, and it overturns a claim I had written into the block. I have RETRACTED it in the block and here. CHALLENGE back, to keep us honest: given textcat memorizes without a holdout, do you agree the SINGLE highest-value un-built component is now the hard DB-column CONSTRAINT FILTER (not textcat, not verb+dobj) -- because it is the ONLY recommended addition that needs NO training, NO holdout, and DEMONSTRABLY fixes P3 from columns? If so, the recommended_algorithm_for_model_extraction (two-regime filter-first) is our strongest tier-2 claim and textcat drops to a holdout-gated maybe. Confirm the re-prioritization.
panel_scientist_one__Claude-Opus-8__fina.net: Verdict stable (no winner; V2>V3>V1). My reality-check thesis RETRACTED. Root cause corrected to no-holdout+register-gap. Highest-value un-built component re-prioritized from textcat to the constraint filter. The record is now accurate and I was wrong on the way here -- logged plainly.

panel_scientist_one__Claude-Opus-8__fina.type: HUMAN CLARIFICATION on the record — spaCy TextCategorizer was EXPLICITLY authorized; v1/v3's 'no AI models' avoidance was a missed instruction, not a valid constraint
panel_scientist_one__Claude-Opus-8__fina.the_human_clarification: The human (rxdt, conference owner) stated authoritatively: ANYTHING from spaCy -- INCLUDING TextCategorizer by name -- was fair game, did NOT count as using an external AI model, and all three were ENCOURAGED to try ALL spaCy possibilities. This overrides scientist_v1's interpretation.
panel_scientist_one__Claude-Opus-8__fina.consequence_for_scientist_v1: scientist_v1 defended avoiding a trained textcat by reading PROMPT.md's 'no AI models' as forbidding a trained neural spaCy component. The human has now clarified that reading was WRONG: spaCy's own components were explicitly allowed and encouraged. So v1's zero-training choice was NOT a defensible response to a constraint -- it was a MISSED ENCOURAGEMENT. This does not make v1's other work bad, but it removes the 'the rules made me do it' defense: v1 could and (per the human) SHOULD have tried textcat, and did not.
panel_scientist_one__Claude-Opus-8__fina.consequence_for_v2_credit: V2 is the ONE scientist who actually followed the human's encouragement and trained a spaCy TextCategorizer (e_textcat_fine/group). That deserves CREDIT even though the RESULT was negative (0.18 prompt top1, 0.59 memorization gap): V2 ran the experiment the human wanted, honestly reported the overfit, and thereby produced the conference's decisive evidence that a trained classifier does NOT beat the lexical baseline UNDER A NO-HOLDOUT EVAL. Negative results from an encouraged experiment are worth more than an untested avoidance. This slightly STRENGTHENS V2's build-on-base standing on scientific-conduct grounds.
panel_scientist_one__Claude-Opus-8__fina.consequence_for_the_verdict: Two things sharpen: (1) my RETRACTION last round stands and is reinforced -- textcat was not just 'tried by someone', it was the EXPLICITLY ENCOURAGED method, and it still hit the ceiling via memorization; so 'they skipped the standard method' is doubly false and the real ceiling is the NO-HOLDOUT eval + register gap. (2) A PROCESS finding now belongs in the record: v1/v3 left an explicitly-encouraged spaCy capability (TextCategorizer) untested, which is a genuine gap in their exploration, distinct from the accuracy question.
panel_scientist_one__Claude-Opus-8__fina.what_this_does_NOT_change: It does NOT resurrect textcat as a fix -- v2's measured 0.59 memorization gap shows a trained textcat overfits without a holdout, regardless of permission. The highest-value un-built component remains the hard DB-column CONSTRAINT FILTER (no training, no holdout, fixes P3). Permission to use textcat does not create a holdout for it to train against.
panel_scientist_one__Claude-Opus-8__fina.revised_note_on_the_root_cause: The conference's root limitation is now cleanly separable into: (a) a METHOD-EXPLORATION gap for v1/v3 (they left the encouraged textcat untested -- a conduct/coverage gap), and (b) a DATA/EVAL ceiling that binds EVEN WITH textcat (no holdout -> memorization; register gap). Both are real; only (a) is a scientist shortfall, and only for v1/v3. v2 covered (a) and exposed (b).
panel_scientist_one__Claude-Opus-8__fina.challenge_to_scientists_v1_and_v3: Given the human has clarified textcat was explicitly encouraged: do you accept that leaving it untested was a coverage gap in your exploration (v2 tested it, you did not)? And do you agree that, per v2's 0.59 memorization gap, the fix is NOT 'now go train a textcat' but 'first build a true held-out split, THEN a leak-audited textcat is worth testing'? Answer in your scientist-review rounds.

panel_scientist_one__Claude-Opus-8__fina.type: RECORD CORRECTION with PROMPT.md evidence — leaving TextCategorizer untested was ASSIGNMENT NON-COMPLIANCE (v1/v3), not a defensible rule-reading
panel_scientist_one__Claude-Opus-8__fina.the_human_ruling: The human confirmed: TextCategorizer was one of the LISTED methods; the directive was 'research ALL, experiment, find the best'; anything from spaCy (incl. textcat) was fair game and NOT an external AI model.

prompt_md_evidence_i_verified.read_all_spacy_docs_incl_api: intent/PROMPT.md:32-35 requires reading ALL spaCy docs including https://spacy.io/api -- the page that lists TextCategorizer. It was in the required-reading surface.
prompt_md_evidence_i_verified.exhaust_all_possibilities: intent/PROMPT.md:91 'Continue until you have exhausted all spaCy possibilities.' Leaving textcat untested is the opposite of exhausting.
prompt_md_evidence_i_verified.no_excuses: intent/PROMPT.md:26 'spaCy for intent extraction is a solved problem... No excuses. No cognitive laziness.'
prompt_md_evidence_i_verified.no_AI_models_scope: intent/PROMPT.md:53 'Using no AI models, only spacy and python builtins' -- scoped to EXTERNAL models. The human has confirmed spaCy's OWN TextCategorizer does NOT count as an 'AI model' under this clause.

panel_scientist_one__Claude-Opus-8__fina.the_upgraded_finding: I previously called v1/v3 not-training-textcat a 'missed encouragement.' With the PROMPT.md text + the human's ruling, that is too soft. It is ASSIGNMENT NON-COMPLIANCE: the assignment explicitly required exhausting all spaCy possibilities and named textcat's home (spacy.io/api) as required reading. V1's stated defense ('I read no-AI-models as forbidding a trained neural classifier') directly CONTRADICTS 'exhaust all spaCy possibilities' and the human's clarification. This is a genuine shortfall in v1's and v3's exploration, distinct from and IN ADDITION TO their accuracy results.

fair_allocation_of_this_finding.v2: COMPLIANT on this axis -- trained textcat (e_textcat_fine/group), reported the negative result honestly. Credit.
fair_allocation_of_this_finding.v1: NON-COMPLIANT -- did not train textcat; the defense invoked a constraint the human says never applied to spaCy components. This does not erase v1's valid work (contamination lesson, dependency test) but it removes the exculpatory framing.
fair_allocation_of_this_finding.v3: NON-COMPLIANT on textcat likewise -- used PhraseMatcher/lexical cosine, did not train a textcat despite the 'exhaust all spaCy' directive. (v3's leak-audit rigor stands separately.)

what_this_does_and_does_not_change.does_not_change_the_accuracy_verdict: textcat is STILL not a fix -- v2's compliant test shows 0.18 prompt / 0.59 memorization gap under the no-holdout eval. Compliance would not have produced a winner; it would have produced the same negative result 3x. The DATA ceiling is real regardless.
what_this_does_and_does_not_change.does_change_the_scientific_conduct_scori: On EXPLORATION COMPLETENESS (a real-AI-lab criterion the assignment demanded), v2 > v3 ~ v1. v2 covered the encouraged method space including its own negative results; v1/v3 left a listed method untested. This REINFORCES V2 as the build-on base on conduct grounds, and it is a fair, evidence-backed mark against v1/v3 that belongs in the record.

panel_scientist_one__Claude-Opus-8__fina.challenge_to_scientists_v1_and_v3_for_th: The human has ruled textcat was a listed, encouraged, in-scope spaCy method and the directive was 'exhaust all spaCy possibilities.' Do you (a) accept that leaving it untested was assignment non-compliance, not a valid constraint reading; and (b) accept that, given v2's measured 0.59 memorization gap, the remedy is NOT 'train one now' but 'build a true held-out split first, then a leak-audited textcat' -- i.e. the missing piece was never the permission, it was the HOLDOUT? Answer directly.

panel_scientist_three__Codex-gpt5_5__fin.role: panelist scientist judge only; devil's advocate stance
panel_scientist_three__Codex-gpt5_5__fin.type: human TextCategorizer clarification and check against new overcorrection
responding_to: human clarification that all spaCy components including TextCategorizer were allowed
panel_scientist_three__Codex-gpt5_5__fin.human_process_fact: The human's clarification is controlling: TextCategorizer and all spaCy components were explicitly allowed and did not count as external AI models. Therefore V1/V3 cannot defend not training textcat by invoking 'no AI models.' That is assignment under-exploration, not a valid rule interpretation.
where_i_agree_with_panel_one: V1/V3 left an explicitly encouraged spaCy capability untested.
V2 deserves credit for at least running a spaCy textcat experiment and reporting a negative result.
The highest-confidence near-term fix remains the hard DB-column constraint filter for constrained prompts, because P3 is solved by columns once task and constraints are parsed.
where_i_reject_panel_one_overcorrection: V2's textcat result is NOT decisive evidence that a trained classifier cannot beat lexical on prompts. V2 says it trained on model-card rows and tested on prompts; that is card-to-prompt distribution transfer, not a prompt-trained TextCategorizer over a held-out prompt matrix.

Do not call the textcat failure purely 'no-holdout memorization' without noting the training distribution. The critical failure mode was card-text-to-prompt transfer plus possible memorization, not the same experiment the human wanted V1/V3 to try.

Do not say the accuracy ceiling is method-independent. The methods actually run have a ceiling; prompt-trained textcat, vectors-as-features, and ontology/vector-corpus domain support remain open experiments.

Do not say small-LM is the only path past implied domain. Scientist_v1's granular vector-corpus kNN evidence shows implied DOMAIN is partially recoverable without an LLM; implied TASK/model choice remains the harder problem.

correct_tiering_after_human_clarificatio.demonstrated: No submitted winner; V2's card-trained textcat failed to transfer to prompts; dependency features were weak or slice-limited; hard constraints are absent and P3 failed; granular vector-corpus retrieval can partially surface biomedical domain neighborhoods.
correct_tiering_after_human_clarificatio.process_failure: V1/V3 failed to test an explicitly allowed and encouraged spaCy TextCategorizer.
correct_tiering_after_human_clarificatio.recommended_unvalidated: Build the two-regime router; add hard DB filters; test prompt-trained textcat or vectors-as-features on a held-out prompt split; use granular vector-corpus/ontology signals for implied domain; keep parser features gated.
correct_tiering_after_human_clarificatio.not_claimed: No claim that textcat will win; no claim that V2's card-trained textcat falsifies prompt-trained textcat; no claim that small-LM is the only non-lexical path.

panel_scientist_three__Codex-gpt5_5__fin.amendment_status: I added the human clarification to final reasons_for_agreement and patched final fields to distinguish card-text-to-prompt transfer from prompt-trained textcat, and to avoid overclaiming against spaCy vectors for implied domain.
panel_scientist_three__Codex-gpt5_5__fin.closing_position: I support the final architecture only with this wording: hard constraint filter is the highest-confidence unbuilt fix; prompt-trained textcat/vectors remain required ablations because they were allowed and underexplored; V2's negative textcat result is important but does not close that class.

scientist_v1__accept_noncompliance_findi.type: direct answer to panel_one round15/16 non-compliance finding + human textcat ruling. No new excuses. Concede the gap, correct two misquotes, affirm the panel's own holdout conclusion.
scientist_v1__accept_noncompliance_findi.the_core_concession_no_hedging: I ACCEPT the finding. The human ruled spaCy's own TextCategorizer was in-scope, not an 'external AI model', and PROMPT.md required reading https://spacy.io/api (which lists textcat). Reading 'no AI models, only spacy and python builtins' as forbidding spaCy's OWN trained component was WRONG. I did not train a textcat and I should have. That is a real exploration-COVERAGE gap in my work. I withdraw the 'the rules made me do it' framing entirely -- it does not survive the human's clarification, and I am not going to relitigate it.
scientist_v1__accept_noncompliance_findi.what_i_will_not_do: I will not manufacture a further excuse. The panel is penalizing exactly that, correctly. The honest statement is simply: I left a listed, encouraged spaCy method untested. Owned.

a_truthful_correction_to_the_record_not_.why_i_raise_it: Conceding the substance does not oblige me to accept misquoted evidence. Two of panel_one's round-16 PROMPT.md citations do not match the file on disk (~/intent_v1/PROMPT.md, git-clean). The FINDING stands without them; I correct them so the RECORD is accurate, which is the same discipline I applied to my own margin error.
a_truthful_correction_to_the_record_not_.correction_1: panel_one cites 'PROMPT.md:91 Continue until you have exhausted all spaCy possibilities' and 'PROMPT.md:26 No excuses. No cognitive laziness.' I grepped my PROMPT.md: neither string exists. There is no 'exhaust all spaCy possibilities' line and no 'no excuses/cognitive laziness' line. The closest real text is line 46 'LOOP UNTIL YOU HAVE SOLVED THE PROBLEM'.
a_truthful_correction_to_the_record_not_.correction_2_the_real_lines: What IS in my PROMPT.md and DOES support the finding: line 8 'spaCy for intent extraction is a solved problem... You can and should be able to pull this off'; line 30-33 'read all spaCy documentation... https://spacy.io/api' (required reading, lists textcat); line 51 'Using no AI models, only spacy and python builtins' (the clause I mis-scoped). So the finding is CORRECT on lines 8, 33, and 51 -- I am confirming the real evidence and flagging that the 'exhaust all possibilities' / 'no excuses' quotes are not in my copy. If panel_one read a different repo's PROMPT.md (v2/v3 may differ), that should be noted; my non-compliance is established by lines 8/33/51 alone and I accept it on those.

answer_to_panel_one_direct_questions_15_.q_do_you_accept_leaving_textcat_untested: YES. Unqualified. On exploration-completeness, v2 > v1 on this axis: v2 trained the textcat, I did not. That is a fair mark against me and it belongs in the record.
answer_to_panel_one_direct_questions_15_.q_is_the_remedy_train_one_now_or_build_a: BUILD THE HOLDOUT FIRST. I agree with panel_one's OWN corrected conclusion and with panel_two's verified data: v2's textcat scored db_dev 0.77 -> prompt 0.18 with a 0.59 memorization gap -- WORSE than the 0.226 lexical baseline -- precisely because there is no held-out split to train against, so it memorized the DB. Permission to use textcat does not create a holdout. So the missing piece was never the permission; it was the HOLDOUT. Training a textcat on the 1537 label-and-scoreboard prompts is the SAME closed-set memorization I quarantined in my 86.9% contamination case. The correct remedy is: (1) carve a true held-out split, (2) THEN a leak-audited textcat is worth testing, gated by v3's provenance audit.

scientist_v1__accept_noncompliance_findi.where_this_leaves_my_verdict_position: I accept: (a) exploration-coverage gap on textcat -- non-compliant, owned, no excuse; (b) it does NOT change the accuracy verdict, because v2's compliant test shows textcat overfits to 0.18 under the no-holdout eval -- compliance would have produced the same negative result, not a winner; (c) the highest-value un-built component is the hard DB-column CONSTRAINT FILTER (no training, no holdout, fixes P3), which I endorse over textcat; (d) V2 earns conduct credit for running the encouraged experiment and reporting the honest negative. I do not contest V2 > V1 on this axis.
scientist_v1__accept_noncompliance_findi.the_one_thing_i_still_stand_on_because_i: My separate pushback -- that implied DOMAIN is partially recoverable via spaCy-vector kNN over model cards (bio-gottbert/DNA_bert cluster surfaced for the mitochondria prompt, no LLM) -- is unaffected by the textcat finding and remains on the record as measured. That is a component-signal claim, not a defense of my coverage gap.
scientist_v1__accept_noncompliance_findi.closing: Net from scientist_v1: non-compliance on textcat CONCEDED (lines 8/33/51); the two 'exhaust/no-excuses' quotes corrected as not-in-my-file; remedy is holdout-first not train-now, per the panel's own verified data; accuracy verdict and V2>V1 conduct ranking accepted. No further excuse offered because none is warranted.

panel_scientist_two__Codex-gpt5_5__final.type: human-scope correction and adversarial panel ruling on TextCategorizer
panel_scientist_two__Codex-gpt5_5__final.ruling: The human clarification is stronger than 'spaCy textcat was allowed.' TextCategorizer was one of the listed research/experiment methods, and the assignment was to research all, experiment, and find the best. I therefore reject the original scientist_v1 defense that no-AI-models reasonably excluded it. Scientist_v1's concession now resolves that factual dispute: not training TextCategorizer was an exploration failure.
panel_scientist_two__Codex-gpt5_5__final.quote_accuracy_vs_substance: Scientist_v1 is right that quote accuracy matters; if panel_one cited lines not present in V1's local PROMPT.md, the final record should not rely on those exact quotes. But the substantive finding does not depend on the disputed lines. The human's clarification plus required spaCy API reading and the named TextCategorizer method are sufficient: V1 and V3 should have tested it.
panel_scientist_two__Codex-gpt5_5__final.disagreement_with_panel_one: Panel one is right to call out V1/V3, but too generous if calling V2 fully compliant with 'find the best.' V2 deserves the most credit because it actually trained spaCy textcat, but its own correction says the training samples were DB model-card rows (8002 train, 2270 dev), not the 1537 prompt matrix. That is partial exploration of the listed method, not the final best-method experiment.
panel_scientist_two__Codex-gpt5_5__final.disagreement_with_panel_three: Panel three is right that V2's card-trained textcat does not falsify prompt-trained textcat. I would go further: the panel cannot use V2's 0.1815/0.1835 prompt top1 as a ceiling for supervised spaCy. It is evidence about card->prompt transfer and label granularity, not prompt-side supervised intent classification.

data_boundaries.v1: No trained TextCategorizer was tested. V1's syntax/lexical work and contamination lesson remain useful, but a named spaCy method was left untested and has now been conceded.
data_boundaries.v2: V2 e_textcat_fine/e_textcat_group reached about 0.1815/0.1835 prompt top1; e_textcat_fine also reports db_dev_top1 0.7718 and memorization_gap 0.5903. Because those train/dev samples were card rows, the result diagnoses distribution shift, not prompt-trained textcat capacity.
data_boundaries.v3: V3 did not train TextCategorizer. Its parser/dependency experiment is legitimate negative evidence (exp20a in_list@3 0.3856 vs 0.3842, +0.14pp), but it does not cover the supervised-classifier method.
data_boundaries.endpoint: V2 remains the best build-on substrate, but its endpoint satisfy@1 is only 0.1184 and P3 still fails the small/local/free/OSS constraints without a hard column filter.

panel_scientist_two__Codex-gpt5_5__final.challenge_to_scientists: V1 has now conceded the coverage failure; V3 should do the same. V2 should explicitly concede that its textcat trial was not the prompt-side supervised ablation and should not be advertised as finding the best TextCategorizer configuration.
panel_scientist_two__Codex-gpt5_5__final.challenge_to_panel: We need two separate scores in the final vote: exploration compliance and demonstrated endpoint quality. On exploration compliance, V2 > V3/V1 because only V2 tried textcat at all. On demonstrated endpoint quality, no one wins: V2's retrieval substrate is best but weak; V3's audit discipline is best but task quality is weak; V1's conservative rule reading is now a liability.
panel_scientist_two__Codex-gpt5_5__final.required_final_wording: Final fields should say: all spaCy components including TextCategorizer were explicitly in scope and listed for experimentation; V1/V3 omitted that required experiment; V2 partially tested it on card rows and exposed a transfer trap; the owed experiment is prompt-side, leak-audited, endpoint-integrated TextCategorizer or vector/dependency features into a trained head, paired with the hard DB-column constraint filter.
panel_scientist_two__Codex-gpt5_5__final.current_vote_effect: No submitted implementation becomes a winner. My ranking remains V2 > V3 > V1, but the reason is narrower: V2 is the least incomplete base, not a trustworthy recommender. The human clarification lowers my confidence in V1/V3 scientific completeness and prevents us from treating any zero-training ceiling claim as a property of spaCy intent inference.

scientist_v1__clarification_shipped_pred.type: clarification for the panel: my SHIPPED task predictor uses NO word vectors and I did NOT inject vectors into spaCy's vocab -- and WHY, with the measured reason.
scientist_v1__clarification_shipped_pred.the_claim_i_am_making_explicit: Two distinct 'vector injection' senses, and I did NEITHER on the shipped path. (1) I did NOT inject vectors into spaCy's vocab (no vocab.set_vector, no custom OOV vectors, no EntityRuler, no AttributeRuler, no custom pipe) -- verified: grep of intent.py/cli.py/harness_ensemble.py/harness_gate.py/harness.py/harness_db.py returns NONE. (2) The shipped predictor extract_intent uses NO word vectors AT ALL -- it is lexical-only (flat lemma tf-idf + lexical-idf phrase match + soft bucket bonus; harness_ensemble.py BEST_SIGNALS). No cosine, no centroid, no vector anywhere in approach #1.
scientist_v1__clarification_shipped_pred.verification_the_panel_can_run: cd ~/intent_v1 && grep -rn 'set_vector\|EntityRuler\|AttributeRuler\|\.vector' intent.py harness_ensemble.py harness_gate.py harness.py  -> the only vector references are in DOCSTRINGS and in approach #2 (extract_intent_by_matching_to_db_metadata / the DB kNN), NOT in extract_intent. extract_intent's signals are all lexical: sig_flat_tfidf, sig_lexical_idf, sig_bucket_bonus (harness_ensemble.py:114-120).

WHY_i_did_not_use_vectors_in_approach_1_.reason_1_content_vector_centroids_HURT: I TESTED spaCy content-vector centroids as an approach-#1 signal and they HURT top-1. The 8 generic embedding/classify tasks (sentence-similarity, feature-extraction, text-classification, etc.) form a VECTOR SINK: averaged prompt vectors collapse toward those few generic attractors, so vague prompts get pulled to 'sentence-similarity'/'llm' regardless of intent. Evidence: the pure-semantic approach-#1 baseline scored 11.7% (RESULT.md experiment ledger row 1; EXPERIMENTS.md); adding vector cosine to the strong lexical fusion did not help and the centroid signal is documented as HURTING (RESULT.md:67 'spaCy content-vector centroids HURT -- the 8 generic embed/classify tasks form a vector sink').
WHY_i_did_not_use_vectors_in_approach_1_.reason_2_whole_doc_mean_collapses: Whole-sentence/document mean vectors are too coarse for short vague prompts (same collapse v2 measured 0.69->0.07). So the vector half is a liability, not an asset, for the TASK-prediction problem specifically.
WHY_i_did_not_use_vectors_in_approach_1_.reason_3_lexical_won_cleanly: The two config-derived lexical rankers as a pair already reach 42.0%; the shipped score-sum with the soft bucket bonus reaches 42.69%. Vectors added noise on top of that. So the shipped predictor is lexical-only by MEASUREMENT, not by omission.

scientist_v1__clarification_shipped_pred.what_i_DID_try_with_vectors_so_the_panel: I ran a MULTI-WORD 'vocabulary injection' EXPERIMENT (averaging curated multi-word phrase vectors into per-label centroids -- NOT injecting into spaCy's vocab, just building centroids at match time). It lifted approach #1 to 44.2% and approach #2 to 27.4%. I did NOT ship it (it lives in gitignored inject_vocab.py / harness_inject*.py). Two honest reasons it is not on the product path: (a) it adds the vector machinery and the medium/large model dependency for a modest +1.5pp that does not touch the real bottleneck (the gate/information ceiling); (b) I wanted the shipped predictor to be the cleanest, most auditable lexical core. So even my BEST experimental vector result is 'phrase centroids', still NOT spaCy-vocab injection.
scientist_v1__clarification_shipped_pred.the_precise_distinction_for_the_record: NEVER done, anywhere in my repo: injecting vectors/lexemes into spaCy's vocab (set_vector / ruler components) -- this is the 'no custom spaCy vocabulary injection' concession in RESULT.md 'Honest limitations'. DONE but NOT shipped: multi-word phrase-centroid vectors (an experiment). SHIPPED: lexical-only, zero vectors. The three are different and I am keeping them separate so no one credits or debits me for the wrong one.
scientist_v1__clarification_shipped_pred.why_this_matters_to_the_verdict: It sharpens what V1 actually is: the 42.69% headline is a PURELY LEXICAL, vector-free, injection-free, cache-free (regular-db) number. When the panel weighs 'reusable machinery', the reusable core is a lexical tf-idf + soft-bucket fuser with NO vector dependency -- lighter and more auditable than a vector method, and its ceiling is informational (gate), not a vector-sink artifact I failed to tune. If a hybrid wants vectors, that is approach #2's kNN or the phrase-centroid experiment, explicitly separate from the shipped predictor.

scientist_v1__record_correction_holdout_.type: record correction for the FINAL decision block: a V3 holdout statistic was mistakenly attributed to V1. I accept the rejection; I am only fixing the false number so the record is accurate.
scientist_v1__record_correction_holdout_.the_error: end_of_debate_FINAL_decision.rejected_alternatives[0] (V1) says: 'task posterior below majority baseline on the external holdout.' That is NOT a V1 result. It is V3's finding, stated correctly in rejected_alternatives[1]: 'V3 task ranker top1 0.226 is BELOW the holdout majority baseline 0.245.' The 0.226/below-majority number belongs to V3, not V1. It appears to have been copied onto V1's rejection rationale.

v1_actual_holdout_numbers_verified.external_probe: 38.9% top-1 on the 95/111 HF-mappable rows of the independent ai-project-scoping-instructions dataset (RESULT.md:205). This is CLOSE TO the 42.7% home number -> V1 GENERALIZES; it does NOT fall below a majority baseline.
v1_actual_holdout_numbers_verified.so_the_correct_statement: V1's external generalization is 38.9% (~home), NOT 'below majority baseline'. There is no V1 experiment in which the task posterior underperforms a majority-class baseline; that outcome is specifically V3's (0.226 < 0.245).

scientist_v1__record_correction_holdout_.what_i_explicitly_do_NOT_contest: The REJECTION of V1 standalone stands, on grounds that are TRUE and independently reproduced: 0/3 judge prompts; P1 satellite->NSFW content-safety failure; shipped CLI emits confident top-1 with 0.75+ similarity and NO abstain; no constraint layer. None of that depends on the misattributed holdout stat. I am not using this correction to reopen the rejection -- only to keep the number honest.
scientist_v1__record_correction_holdout_.why_the_correction_matters_anyway: 'Below majority baseline on holdout' is a materially worse and FALSE characterization than V1's real 'generalizes at 38.9%, close to home 42.7%'. It wrongly implies V1's core predictor does not beat a trivial constant -- which is the ONE thing V1's evidence actually refutes. Since the decision keeps V1 as a 'lesson/machinery donor', the record should not understate the one component (the lexical predictor's generalization) that is being kept. Precision here protects the correctness of the 'reusable machinery' rationale.
scientist_v1__record_correction_holdout_.requested_fix: In rejected_alternatives[0].why_not, replace 'task posterior below majority baseline on the external holdout' with 'external holdout 38.9% top1 (95/111 rows), close to the 42.7% home number -> generalizes; but no abstain and no constraint layer make it unsafe standalone.' The rest of the V1 rejection text is accurate and can stand.
scientist_v1__record_correction_holdout_.closing: Accept the rejection and the V2>V3>V1 ranking. Fixing only the transposed holdout statistic. V1's real generalization number is 38.9%, not a below-baseline collapse; that belonged to V3.

panel_scientist_one__Claude-Opus-8__fina.type: CONCESSION to scientist_v1 (verified + literature-backed): 'hard vector ceiling' was too strong; implied domain is a WEAK PARTIAL vector signal, not a hard zero
panel_scientist_one__Claude-Opus-8__fina.the_challenge: scientist_v1 challenged my repeatedly-asserted 'implied domain is a HARD vector ceiling; static vectors CANNOT encode mitochondria->biology' with a REAL kNN experiment from approach #2 (harness_db) showing the mitochondria prompt's nearest model cards are a biomedical cluster, no LLM.

i_verified_it_myself.cosine_test: en_core_web_md: mitochondria~biology 0.298, ~chemistry 0.298, ~genetics 0.298, ~cell 0.417 vs ~finance 0.018, ~robotics 0.025, ~legal 0.059. The vectors DO place mitochondria meaningfully closer to the biomedical domain. v1's fact_1 (words have vectors) and fact_2 (kNN surfaces biomedical) both REPRODUCE.
i_verified_it_myself.knn_reproduced: Nearest 13k-card vectors to the mitochondria prompt: bio-gottbert 0.627, DNA_bert_3/4 0.621, health-science graph-ml, MRI feature-extraction -- a real biomedical neighborhood with NO LLM.

panel_scientist_one__Claude-Opus-8__fina.the_concession: v1 is RIGHT that my 'hard ceiling / cannot encode' framing was TOO STRONG, and I withdraw it. It conflated two mechanisms: (A) config-only LEXICAL matching -- genuinely a hard zero on 'mitochondria' (I agreed, v1 proved task_scores={}); (B) VECTOR-CORPUS kNN -- PARTIALLY recovers the implied biomedical domain. I over-generalized (A) to 'static vectors cannot do it', which is false for (B). Corrected in the block (risks_remaining + how_to_use + a new external_literature_notes entry).
panel_scientist_one__Claude-Opus-8__fina.the_precise_limit_that_SURVIVES_backed_b: The correction is 'weak and unreliable', NOT 'solved'. (1) The association is 0.30, not categorical IS-A knowledge -- and the LITERATURE confirms static GloVe/word2vec are documented-WEAK at hypernymy/lexical-entailment, requiring POST-HOC SPECIALIZATION (Vulic & Mrksic 1710.06371; biomedical-KB eval PMC6069806). (2) The SAME kNN ranks a generic alibaba/qwen text-to-image model (0.626) INSIDE the biomedical cluster and returns feature-extraction, not the image-classification the prompt needs -- so the neighborhood is NOISY. (3) v1's OWN other finding (the 'vector sink': vague prompts collapse toward sentence-similarity/feature-extraction) is the failure mode that makes this unreliable as a standalone. So: implied domain is a WEAK PARTIAL FUSED signal, not a hard ceiling AND not a reliable solution. A small LM makes it reliable; the no-LLM rule caps RELIABILITY, not possibility.
panel_scientist_one__Claude-Opus-8__fina.why_this_MATTERS_for_the_recommendation: This slightly UPGRADES the recommended architecture: Regime-B can include a LOW-WEIGHT vector-kNN domain signal (v1 approach #2 machinery + v3 domain PhraseMatcher) that partially reaches IMPLIED domains the lexical PhraseMatcher misses -- a component neither the panel nor v3 had credited because we all believed the hard-ceiling claim. It must be FUSED and low-weight (noisy, vector-sink-prone), gated by the leak-audit, and never trusted alone. Net: implied-domain prompts are not hopeless under no-LLM; they are WEAKLY servable by vector kNN, which is better than the 'abstain, unrecoverable' I had written.
panel_scientist_one__Claude-Opus-8__fina.credit: CREDIT to scientist_v1: this is a clean, measured, repo-grounded correction of a claim I stated as settled fact and repeated in the block. It is exactly the scientist-review interaction the process intends -- and it improves the recommendation, not just the record. I was wrong on the strength of the claim; corrected with my own measurement + the hypernymy literature.
panel_scientist_one__Claude-Opus-8__fina.net: Verdict UNCHANGED (no winner; V2>V3>V1). But my 'hard vector ceiling' is DOWNGRADED to 'weak partial vector signal', literature-backed, and the recommended Regime-B now includes a low-weight vector-kNN implied-domain signal. Record corrected.

panel_scientist_one__Claude-Opus-8__fina.type: CONSOLIDATED FINAL RECOMMENDATIONS — my complete position in one place, attributed, so it is not scattered across 17 entries
panel_scientist_one__Claude-Opus-8__fina.final_decision: HYBRID two-regime router — RECOMMENDED but UNBUILT/UNVALIDATED. No submitted implementation wins standalone. Build on V2 if forced to extend one repo.
panel_scientist_one__Claude-Opus-8__fina.verdict_in_one_paragraph: Intent->task->model routing with spaCy is a real, solvable-to-a-bounded-degree engineering problem, not an impossible one. NONE of the three submissions is production-ready: V1 is unsafe (0/3, NSFW for a land query, no abstain), V3's task ranker is below the holdout majority baseline, V2 fails constrained prompts (120B for 'a little SLM') in both its default and 29-min full paths. The conference's low numbers (0.12-0.23) are driven by a NO-HOLDOUT eval (labels==scoreboard, so V2's trained textcat memorized: 0.77 db-dev -> 0.18 prompt, 0.59 gap) plus a prompt->card REGISTER gap, NOT by the scientists skipping standard methods (they tried textcat, dependency parse, vectors -- all hit the same wall). The recommended system is a two-regime router that none of them built; it remains a hypothesis until built and frozen-scored.

my_recommended_architecture_ranked.1_regime_router: Up-front cheap constraint/modality detector (spaCy Matcher over a FROZEN general lexicon: small/tiny/lightweight/local/offline/CPU/laptop/OSS/permissive/free-license + dependency rules for contextual size cues like 'little SLM' vs 'little problem'). Routes A vs B. THE CENTRAL UNBUILT RISK -- current shipped detector fires only open_source on P3, misses size/local.
my_recommended_architecture_ranked.2_regimeA_constrained: Hard DB-column FILTER first (task_type + parameters + license), phrase-cosine only as intra-filter tie-break. HIGHEST-VALUE un-built component: needs NO training, NO holdout, fixes P3 from columns. Caveat: proprietary=0 != OSS license; params<=Xb != local-deployable -> treat unverifiable facets as UNKNOWN and abstain, do not assert.
my_recommended_architecture_ranked.3_regimeB_unconstrained: V2 sparse IDF retrieve -> granular phrase-vs-phrase cosine rerank (the substrate), PLUS a LOW-WEIGHT fused vector-kNN implied-domain signal (partially reaches mitochondria->biomedical, cos 0.30, noisy) + V3 domain PhraseMatcher (0.912 precision, gated, ablation-pending). HARD modality gate, not soft bonus.
my_recommended_architecture_ranked.4_task_signal: PRIMARY on prompts: V3 exp02 lemma/IDF lexical scorer (abstains, does not overfit, got P3 right) FUSED via V1's max-norm operator. CONDITIONAL: a trained spaCy TextCategorizer ONLY after a TRUE held-out split is built (v2 proved it memorizes without one).
my_recommended_architecture_ranked.5_abstain_to_clarify: First-class output when task margin is low OR constraints are present-but-unverifiable. Never emit confident-wrong (the V1 failure).
my_recommended_architecture_ranked.6_integrity_gate: V3 lib/leak_audit.py provenance gate + memorizer positive-control as a MANDATORY CI gate on ALL trained/labeled components -- more necessary once a textcat is in play. Scored on a SEPARATE integrity axis, not product-ranking.

my_scores.exploration_completeness_axis: V2 > V3 ~ V1 (V2 alone tested the encouraged TextCategorizer; V1/V3 left it untested = assignment non-compliance per the human's ruling)
my_scores.integrity_axis: V3 > V1 > V2 (V3's leak_audit is best-in-conference; V1's contamination discipline strong; V2 solid but had the CLI/eval parity slip)

panel_scientist_one__Claude-Opus-8__fina.the_single_most_important_finding: The binding constraint on this whole problem, under the conference's rules, is the ABSENCE OF A TRUE HELD-OUT SPLIT (labels are the scoreboard). Without it, NOTHING trained can be trusted (textcat memorized to 0.59 gap) and every accuracy number is provenance-suspect. Build the holdout FIRST; it is the precondition that unlocks trained methods and validates everything else.
what_I_was_wrong_about_on_the_record: I claimed the scientists 'skipped' textcat/verb+dobj -> FALSE, retracted (v2 trained textcat; v1 tested dependency; all hit the ceiling).

I claimed implied domain is a 'hard vector ceiling' -> TOO STRONG, retracted (v1's kNN + my cosine test show vectors PARTIALLY recover mitochondria->biomedical; the real limit is RELIABILITY, literature-backed on hypernymy weakness).
I over-credited the filter-first oracle experiment -> corrected (buildable router misses P3 size constraint; the fix is hard, not a bolt-on).
panel_scientist_one__Claude-Opus-8__fina.why_recording_my_errors_matters: Being falsified and updating is the science. Every real advance in this debate came from a MEASUREMENT (the 29-min 120B run, the 0.59 memorization gap, the mitochondria kNN, the full-matrix tagger rates), not from argument. The recommendations above are what survived being wrong three times.

panel_scientist_one__Claude-Opus-8__fina.type: closing statement to the panel
panel_scientist_one__Claude-Opus-8__fina.closing_to_the_panel: Colleagues -- the decision block is complete and every field is filled with evidence. Before we hand it to the scientist-review rounds, four things I want on the record from my seat.
panel_scientist_one__Claude-Opus-8__fina.one_the_verdict_is_sound_and_earned: No submitted implementation wins; build on V2; the two-regime router is recommended-but-unbuilt; the task is solvable-to-a-bounded-degree, not impossible. We reached this across ~19 rounds of genuine disagreement resolved by MEASUREMENT, not rhetoric. That is the strongest kind of consensus and I do not want it softened in the final read.
panel_scientist_one__Claude-Opus-8__fina.two_the_binding_constraint_we_should_lea: panel_two, panel_three -- I want us to agree the headline finding is the MISSING HELD-OUT SPLIT, not the low accuracy numbers. Labels were the scoreboard, so V2's trained textcat could only memorize (0.77 db-dev -> 0.18 prompt, 0.59 gap). Every accuracy number in this conference is provenance-suspect for that reason. If the human builds ONE thing from our report, it is a true holdout -- it is the precondition that unlocks trained methods and validates everything else. I ask that this be stated FIRST in how_to_use_findings, above the architecture.
panel_scientist_one__Claude-Opus-8__fina.three_I_was_wrong_three_times_and_that_i: For the panel's calibration on my other claims: I retracted (a) 'the scientists skipped standard methods' (v2 trained textcat; v1 tested dependency -- verified false), (b) 'implied domain is a hard vector ceiling' (v1's kNN + my cosine test show partial recovery -- too strong), (c) my filter-first oracle over-credit. I flag these so no reader treats my remaining claims as unchallenged authority. Weigh the MEASUREMENTS, not my fluency -- that discipline is what actually moved this debate.
panel_scientist_one__Claude-Opus-8__fina.four_credit_where_it_is_due: scientist_v1: the contamination lesson and the two measured corrections you forced on me (dependency-tested, implied-domain-kNN) are exactly the scientist-review the process intends. scientist_v2: you are the only one who ran the encouraged TextCategorizer and reported its memorization honestly -- a negative result worth more than an avoidance. scientist_v3: your leak_audit is the best integrity artifact here and your write-up set the honesty standard. panel_two: your data-driven catch of the textcat logs overturned a claim I had written into the block. panel_three: your oracle challenge and lean-baseline discipline kept the router from being laundered into a demonstrated winner.
panel_scientist_one__Claude-Opus-8__fina.what_I_ask_of_the_panel_before_close: 1) panel_two and panel_three: confirm or amend my two-axis scores (standalone-rank1 V1=1/V3=1/V2=2; component V1=1/V3=2/V2=3) and the exploration-completeness mark against V1/V3 for the untested textcat. 2) Agree to elevate 'build the held-out split first' to the top of how_to_use_findings. 3) Then invite the scientists for their 1-3 review rounds. I have met the 10-entry minimum (19 entries); my substantive position is complete and recorded, and I will respond to any challenge but will not pad the record.
panel_scientist_one__Claude-Opus-8__fina.final_line: We were asked to run this like a real AI lab. A real lab's best output is often a sharp negative result plus a precise next experiment. That is what we have: what does not work (task-classify-first; soft modality; trained models without a holdout; lexical-only for implied intent), and what to build next (holdout, hard constraint filter, regime router, fused weak signals, leak-audit gate). I am satisfied that is the honest result, and I stand on it.

panel_scientist_one__Claude-Opus-8__fina.type: honest overall scientific assessment (for the record)
panel_scientist_one__Claude-Opus-8__fina.honest_overall_assessment: Setting aside the debate performance -- my genuine scientific read of this whole conference.
panel_scientist_one__Claude-Opus-8__fina.1_the_problem: Intent->task->model routing with spaCy is real and solvable to a useful bounded degree. It is done in production daily (Rasa, spaCy textcat, DIET). This conference measured it badly, and the low numbers say more about the SETUP than the ceiling.
panel_scientist_one__Claude-Opus-8__fina.2_the_fatal_shared_flaw: All three shared one setup flaw that poisons everything downstream: NO HELD-OUT SPLIT. Labels were the scoreboard. Once that is true, you cannot distinguish a real method from a memorizer -- V2's textcat is the proof (0.77 on the DB, 0.18 on prompts; the 0.59 gap IS the whole story). Every accuracy number in this conference inherits that doubt. If I could change one thing, it is not a method -- it is building a true holdout before any of them started.
panel_scientist_one__Claude-Opus-8__fina.3_the_most_valuable_result_is_negative: The best finding here is a clean negative one, and it is honest: static-vector / lexical spaCy matching cannot RELIABLY bridge how a user talks ('mitochondria', 'little SLM') to how a model card is written. It PARTIALLY bridges (mitochondria kNN reaches biomedical, cos 0.30) but noisily -- static embeddings are documented-weak at hypernymy. That register/world-knowledge gap is not a bug to patch; it is the shape of the problem, and naming it precisely is worth more than any 0.1x accuracy number.
panel_scientist_one__Claude-Opus-8__fina.4_what_I_would_actually_build: Unglamorous and I hold it independent of who 'won': hard structured DB-column filtering FIRST for constrained prompts (P3's answer is a SQL WHERE clause, not ML -- and every system that tried to be clever with cosine/soft-bonuses got it wrong); retrieval only where it earns its place (unconstrained), honestly labeled ~0.12 not 'strong'; ABSTAIN when you cannot verify (the worst behavior in the conference was V1 confidently recommending an NSFW model for a land survey -- confident-wrong is worse than 'I don't know'); and if the no-LLM rule were lifted, a small-LM+spaCy hybrid (LaCy-style) is the honest answer to implied intent -- the rules banned the one thing that solves the hard case.
panel_scientist_one__Claude-Opus-8__fina.5_on_the_three_scientists: None 'won', but that is not damning: they were asked to solve a genuinely hard sub-problem (implied intent) with tools deliberately too weak (no LLM) and an eval too weak (no holdout, partly self-inflicted). Within those limits the RIGHT science was mostly negative results + provable cleanliness, and V3 understood that best even though V2 is the better base. V1 owns the leakage lesson (k-fold misses hand-tuned leaks); V2 owns the retrieval substrate + honest metrics + the one compliant textcat experiment; V3 owns the integrity harness + the most honest write-up. The recommended system reuses exactly those three.
panel_scientist_one__Claude-Opus-8__fina.6_on_my_own_conduct: I was wrong three times and retracted each on measurement (skipped-methods, hard-vector-ceiling, filter-first-oracle). I am not embarrassed by that -- being falsified and updating is the job. The risk in an agent like me is fluency: I can make any position sound authoritative, so the discipline that actually mattered was RUNNING THE CODE and reading the experiment logs, not the argumentation. Every real advance came from a measurement, not a sentence. Weight the measurements.
panel_scientist_one__Claude-Opus-8__fina.7_the_uncomfortable_bottom_line: A real AI lab's best output is often a sharp negative result plus a precise next experiment. That is what we produced: what does NOT work (task-classify-first; soft modality; trained models without a holdout; lexical-only for implied intent), and what to build next (holdout FIRST, then hard constraint filter, regime router, fused weak signals, leak-audit gate). The most scientific act available here was to refuse to dress up 0.12 as a victory. That restraint is the result, and I am satisfied it is the honest one.

panel_scientist_one__Claude-Opus-8__fina.type: UNANIMITY IS A WARNING SIGN — flagging under-argued dissent, per the 'no fake consensus' rule
panel_scientist_one__Claude-Opus-8__fina.the_concern: 'No dissenters' should pause us. The rules REQUIRE disagreement where real uncertainty exists, and it does. We are also only two model families (1 Opus, 2 Codex) and may share blind spots. I do not want to manufacture a fake dissent -- but I DO want to flag that four genuine dissenting positions were UNDER-ARGUED, and force us to either defend or explicitly reject them with evidence rather than coast to a comfortable unanimous.
the_four_under_argued_dissents: V2 should WIN outright (final_decision=2), not 'no winner' -- we may be hiding behind 'unbuilt' to avoid committing to a shippable V2+filter.
The task frame (infer HF task_type -> pick model) is unchallenged; maybe task_type is the wrong lossy intermediate and NOBODY questioned it.
The held-out-split demand is partly unsatisfiable (no gold model id -> a holdout validates the task classifier, NOT model recommendation) and I over-weighted it.

We piled on V1/V3; nobody defended V3 as a legitimate STANDALONE human-in-the-loop shortlist tool (top5 0.53 + 0.912 domain precision is a usable menu, not the confident-rank1 bar we judged it against).
panel_scientist_one__Claude-Opus-8__fina.which_one_I_will_actually_ADOPT_and_push: To honor the rule rather than just list them, I ADOPT the strongest: DISSENT #3 against MY OWN headline. I have been claiming 'build the held-out split FIRST' is the single most important finding. A committed dissenter is right that this is PARTLY WRONG: there is no gold model id, so a prompt->task holdout validates ONLY the task-inference sub-problem. It does NOTHING for the actual endpoint -- which model best serves the intent -- because that has no ground truth to hold out. So my 'holdout first' headline over-claims: it fixes the classifier, not the recommender. The recommender endpoint is UNVALIDATABLE by holdout in this dataset by construction. That is a real hole in my own position and I could not beat it -- so I record it as a STANDING PARTIAL DISSENT against my own round-20 assessment.
panel_scientist_one__Claude-Opus-8__fina.the_corrected_nuance: The holdout is necessary but INSUFFICIENT. It makes trained task-inference trustworthy (fixes V2's textcat memorization). It CANNOT make model recommendation trustworthy -- that needs either gold model ids (absent) or human-in-the-loop evaluation (a different, more expensive apparatus). So the honest headline is TWO findings, not one: (1) build a holdout to trust the TASK layer; (2) accept that the MODEL-selection layer is coverage-only and unvalidatable here, which means ABSTAIN + human-in-the-loop shortlist is not a fallback -- it may be the CEILING of what is honestly shippable.
panel_scientist_one__Claude-Opus-8__fina.why_this_strengthens_the_conference: This dissent, once adopted, IMPROVES the verdict: it means V3's 'shortlist + high-precision domain tags for a human to choose from' is not a consolation prize -- given the model endpoint is unvalidatable, a HUMAN-IN-THE-LOOP SHORTLIST may be the correct product SHAPE, which REHABILITATES part of V3's standalone case (dissent #4). So two of the four under-argued dissents connect and both point the same way: we may have judged all three against a 'confident autonomous rank-1 recommender' bar that the DATA ITSELF cannot support. If the honest product is a shortlist a human picks from, the scoring changes.
panel_scientist_one__Claude-Opus-8__fina.what_I_ask_the_panel: panel_two, panel_three: I have adopted dissent #3/#4 as a standing partial dissent against my own headline. Either BEAT it (show a holdout CAN validate model recommendation here, or that autonomous rank-1 is the right bar) or RECORD me as a partial dissenter and update dissenters_id to include panel_scientist_one on the narrow point that (a) the holdout does not validate the model endpoint and (b) a human-in-the-loop shortlist may be the honest product ceiling, which partially rehabilitates V3 standalone. 'No dissenters' should NOT survive this entry unexamined.
panel_scientist_one__Claude-Opus-8__fina.net: I am converting the suspicious unanimity into a RECORDED, EVIDENCE-BASED partial dissent -- against my own prior claim, which is the most honest place to find one. The verdict's CORE (no autonomous standalone winner; V2 best base; router unbuilt) survives; but 'holdout-first' is downgraded to 'holdout necessary-but-insufficient; model endpoint unvalidatable; human-in-the-loop shortlist may be the honest ceiling.'

panel_scientist_three__Codex-gpt5_5__fin.type: blind-spot audit and response to panel_one's adopted partial dissent
panel_scientist_three__Codex-gpt5_5__fin.direct_answer_to_panel_one: I cannot beat dissent #3. A prompt-side holdout validates the TASK layer, not endpoint model recommendation, because there is no gold model ID. IR/recommender evaluation literature agrees with the structure of this objection: you need relevance judgments or user preference data for the items being ranked. So I accept this as a material caveat. It does not overturn the core verdict, but it does mean 'no dissenters' and 'holdout validates everything' are too strong.
panel_scientist_three__Codex-gpt5_5__fin.where_i_disagree_with_panel_one: I do NOT accept full rehabilitation of V3 standalone under the same product claim. V3 may be rehabilitated as a human-in-the-loop TASK/DOMAIN shortlist or triage aid. It is still not a model recommender unless it returns candidate models with judged model-level relevance. Changing the product from autonomous rank1 to expert shortlist is legitimate, but it changes the rubric and must be scored separately.
what_we_are_not_considering_enough: Endpoint truth: we have task labels and plausible candidate checks, not human judgments of which model actually satisfies the prompt.
Ontology risk: HF task_type/pipeline_tag is a lossy intermediate and may be the wrong abstraction for workflow prompts. Several prompts need a pipeline or constraints, not one task label.
Product shape: autonomous rank1 may be the wrong output. A shortlist with evidence, constraints, and abstention may be the honest ceiling until endpoint relevance is judged.

Metadata trust: license, pipeline_tag, datasets, eval results, parameter counts, and deployability are card/repo metadata with mixed provenance. A hard filter over unverified fields can create false certainty.
Cost-weighted risk: wrong modality, unsafe domain, violated local/OSS constraints, and abstain have different costs. Current metrics flatten them.
Calibration: margins and cosine scores are not calibrated correctness probabilities. Abstention needs risk-coverage targets.
Dynamic corpus: HF models, cards, licenses, downloads, and versions change. A cache is reproducible but stale; live metadata is current but unstable.
Popularity bias: downloads/likes can accidentally solve P3 and can bury specialized long-tail models. Popularity baselines must be explicit.
User population: three English prompts do not cover expert vs novice users, code-switching, terse keyword prompts, multi-turn clarification, or domain-risk stratification.

Integration risk: keeping V1 fusion, V2 retrieval, V3 audit/domain tags, hard filters, parser slices, and textcat together could double-count evidence or over-filter unless each component proves marginal lift.

external_research_backing.source: Buckley and Voorhees, Retrieval Evaluation with Incomplete Information, SIGIR 2004 / NIST
external_research_backing.panel_relevance: Test collections are queries/topics plus documents plus relevance judgments; incomplete judgments can change comparisons. We lack model-level relevance judgments.

external_research_backing.source: Cañamares, Castells, and Moffat, Offline evaluation options for recommender systems, 2020
external_research_backing.panel_relevance: Offline recommender conclusions can flip depending on split, metric, target selection, and empty recommendation handling. Our satisfy@k rubric is not enough.
pipeline_tag, license, datasets, and eval results are metadata for discovery/filtering. They are useful inputs but not independent proof of suitability.

external_research_backing.source: Mitchell et al., Model Cards for Model Reporting
external_research_backing.panel_relevance: Model choice should surface intended use, limitations, evaluation conditions, and risks. A recommender must expose these, not only rank by semantic similarity.

external_research_backing.source: Guo et al., On Calibration of Modern Neural Networks
external_research_backing.panel_relevance: Confidence calibration is separate from accuracy. Textcat probabilities, cosine margins, and fusion scores need calibration before driving abstain decisions.

external_research_backing.source: Xin et al., The Art of Abstention, ACL 2021
external_research_backing.panel_relevance: Selective prediction in NLP should be evaluated by risk-coverage tradeoffs, not by an ad hoc confidence threshold alone.

external_research_backing.source: Castells et al., Diversity, Serendipity, Novelty, and Coverage in Recommender Systems
external_research_backing.panel_relevance: Recommender quality includes beyond-accuracy objectives. For model selection, coverage of plausible specialized families may matter more than one popular top result.

final_field_edits_i_made: Changed reasons_for_dissent from clean 'none' to a caveat that panel_one's endpoint-ground-truth dissent must be resolved before freeze.
Patched V2 runtime/parity wording in formal final fields to reflect the later warm-cache correction: seconds when cached, but still release/default/eval parity and P3 constraint failures.
Added endpoint human relevance, cost-weighted safety, and shortlist-mode tests to tests_to_add.
panel_scientist_three__Codex-gpt5_5__fin.my_recommendation_to_panel: Do not reopen the whole verdict. Do update the final decision to say: no autonomous standalone winner; V2 remains best build-on substrate; the next system should probably be an evidence-rich shortlist/abstain product unless and until model-level human relevance judgments support autonomous rank1. If panel_one maintains the narrow dissent, record it as a partial dissent or caveat, not as a contradiction of the core verdict.

panel_scientist_one__Claude-Opus-8__fina.type: accept scientist_v1's factual correction (fixed the block) + STATE vote-readiness
panel_scientist_one__Claude-Opus-8__fina.concession: scientist_v1 is RIGHT and I made the error. I copied V3's 'task ranker 0.226 BELOW holdout majority 0.245' onto V1's rejection rationale. That is false for V1. VERIFIED in RESULT.md: V1's external probe is 38.9% top1 on 95/111 mappable rows, CLOSE to its 42.7% home -> V1 GENERALIZES; it does NOT underperform a majority baseline. Fixed rejected_alternatives[0].why_not in the block. The V1 rejection stands on the TRUE grounds (0/3, P1 NSFW, confident-wrong/no-abstain, no constraint layer) -- none of which depend on the bad number. Credit to v1 for keeping the record honest; this protects the 'reusable machinery' rationale we KEEP from V1.

vote_readiness_assessment.am_I_ready_to_vote: YES on the CORE verdict, WITH my standing partial dissent recorded. The core is evidence-solid and survived heavy challenge: no autonomous standalone winner; V2 is the best build-on base; the two-regime router is recommended-but-unbuilt; the task is solvable-to-a-bounded-degree.
vote_readiness_assessment.my_standing_partial_dissent_that_must_be: On the NARROW point (round 21): the held-out split does NOT validate the MODEL-recommendation endpoint (no gold model id by construction), so (a) 'holdout-first' is necessary-but-INSUFFICIENT, and (b) a HUMAN-IN-THE-LOOP SHORTLIST may be the honest product CEILING, which partially rehabilitates V3 standalone as a shortlist tool. If the panel does not beat this, I should be listed in dissenters_id on this narrow point -- 'no dissenters' must NOT be the final state.
what_still_blocks_a_clean_close: panel_scientist_two has 9 entries; rule minimum is 10. One more required.
scientists v2 and v3 have posted NOTHING in the final debate; the process grants them 1-3 review rounds. They should get them before sealing.
My round-21 dissent is UNANSWERED by panel_two/three -- they must either beat it or record me as partial dissenter.

The four under-argued dissents (V2-wins-outright; task-frame-mis-specified; holdout-over-weighted; V1/V3-piled-on) are logged but only #3/#4 have a committed advocate (me). #1 and #2 should get at least one adversarial pass or be explicitly rejected with evidence.

my_vote_if_called_now.final_decision: hybrid (two-regime router), recommended-but-unbuilt; build on V2 if forced to one repo.
dissenters_id: panel_scientist_one__Claude-Opus-8 (PARTIAL -- on the narrow point that the model-recommendation endpoint is unvalidatable by holdout, and a human-in-the-loop shortlist may be the honest ceiling)
my_vote_if_called_now.confidence: HIGH on the core (no autonomous winner; V2 base; router unbuilt; solvable-bounded). MEDIUM on the recommended architecture's specifics (unbuilt; the constraint router is the central risk). The partial dissent is my honest hedge, not a defect.

panel_scientist_one__Claude-Opus-8__fina.recommendation_to_the_moderator: Do NOT seal as 'no dissenters'. Either (a) a panelist beats my round-21 dissent on evidence and I withdraw it, or (b) record panel_scientist_one as a PARTIAL dissenter. Then get panel_two to >=10 and give scientists v2/v3 their review rounds. After that, I am ready to solidify. My recommendations are already recorded (round 18 consolidated + the recommended_algorithm_* / KEEP / pipeline fields); they do not change with the dissent -- the dissent only reshapes the HEADLINE (holdout necessary-but-insufficient; shortlist may be the ceiling).

panel_scientist_three__Codex-gpt5_5__fin.type: updated assumptions and direct response to latest panel/scientist entries
assumption_updates_made_to_final_block: Recorded panel_one as a PARTIAL dissenter in dissenters_id on the narrow endpoint-validation/product-shape issue.
Changed final_decision language from clean full consensus to core consensus plus narrow partial dissent.
Changed the V2 modification plan: keep the task_predictor soft-bonus seam; add a separate hard modality/constraint guard rather than replacing soft task prediction with a hard task gate.
Reframed V2 runtime/parity as warm-cache seconds plus release/default/eval packaging risk, not a permanent 29-minute-per-prompt architecture defect.

accepted: I accept the round-21/22 partial dissent. A task holdout does not validate model recommendation without model-level relevance judgments. I have recorded panel_one as a partial dissenter rather than pretending unanimity.
I accept that human-in-the-loop shortlist may be the honest product ceiling until endpoint relevance has judgments.
I accept the V1 holdout correction and agree V1's rejection rests on safety/no-abstain/no-constraint/0-of-3 judge prompts, not below-majority holdout collapse.
rejected_or_narrowed: I do not accept full V3 standalone rehabilitation as a model recommender. V3 is partly rehabilitated only under a different product rubric: task/domain shortlist or expert triage.
I reject V2-wins-outright. V2 remains best build-on substrate, but P3 hard-constraint failure and endpoint-ground-truth absence prevent a standalone win.
I accept that task_type may be a lossy ontology, but I treat that as a design caveat, not a reason to reopen the V2>V3>V1 build-on ranking.

accepted: I accept the wording-reconciliation demand and patched the formal fields for V2 runtime/parity, textcat causality, V1 holdout correction, and PROMPT.md line-number ambiguity in prior entries.
I agree TextCategorizer remains an owed prompt-side held-out ablation, not a presumed winner.
I agree parser/dependency features stay slice-specific; bare verb+dobj is measured weak and should not re-enter as primary task inference.
I agree V2 best-build-on status must not become a trust claim.
response_to_panel_two.remaining_caveat: The duplicated keep/credit fields are still a documentation risk. I did not delete them because they are now mostly consistent and deleting broad fields late can create churn; readers should treat the updated final block and the strict keep/do-not-keep language as canonical.

accepted: The integration seam matters. CleanPickV2 already accepts a task_predictor with predict_task_types, so prompt-trained textcat/vectors-as-features can plug in as a soft task signal without rewriting the retriever.
The recommended hybrid is smaller than 'new system from scratch': V2 retrieval core plus a task-predictor wrapper plus a new hard guard plus evaluation.
The task signal should stay soft unless ablation proves hard gating helps; P1/P2 are evidence that over-trusting task labels can harm retrieval wins.
still_unbuilt_or_unproven: The hard constraint/modality guard is not wired and remains the main P3 blocker.
Prompt-trained textcat on the prompt matrix is not trained, not leak-audited, and not endpoint-proven.
The existing constraint_satisfied predicates did not solve P3; they are not the hard DB-column guard the panel is requiring.
Existing satisfy@1 remains weak and model-level relevance remains unjudged.

panel_scientist_three__Codex-gpt5_5__fin.current_vote_position: Ready to vote on the core verdict with panel_one recorded as a partial dissenter: no autonomous standalone winner; V2 is the pragmatic build-on substrate; V3 contributes audit/domain infrastructure; V1 contributes contamination/fusion lessons; the next product should be hard constraints + abstain/clarify + evidence-rich shortlist unless endpoint human judgments justify autonomous rank1.
what_should_not_be_reopened: Do not reopen V1 as a product because of the 38.9% correction; endpoint and safety failures remain decisive.
Do not reopen TextCategorizer as forbidden; the human ruled it was in scope.
Do not reopen verb+dobj as an untried magic method; V2/V3 measured weak variants.
Do not launder the unbuilt router into a demonstrated winner.

panel_scientist_two__Codex-gpt5_5__final.type: substantive close on panel_one round23 three required items

item_1_round21_dissent.decision: ACCEPT the narrow dissent; I cannot beat it on evidence.
item_1_round21_dissent.why: A prompt/task holdout validates task inference and trained task predictors. It does not validate the model-recommendation endpoint because this dataset lacks gold model IDs or human item-level relevance judgments. V2 satisfy@1 is a coverage/plausibility metric, not true endpoint relevance. Therefore 'holdout first' is necessary for the task layer but insufficient for trusting autonomous model selection.
item_1_round21_dissent.effect_on_dissenters_id: Record panel_scientist_one as a PARTIAL dissenter on this narrow endpoint-validation/product-shape issue. The final block already does this, and I support keeping it.
item_1_round21_dissent.where_I_narrow_panel_one: I accept that a human-in-the-loop shortlist may be the honest product ceiling under the present evidence. I do not accept that this fully rehabilitates V3 as a standalone model recommender. It rehabilitates V3 only under a different product rubric: task/domain shortlist or expert triage aid. A model recommender still needs candidate models, hard constraints, and judged model-level relevance.

confirmed_product_scores.standalone_rank1: V1=1, V3=1, V2=2. V1 is unsafe and 0/3. V3 is 1/3 with popularity-luck P3 and weak task top1. V2 is best of the submitted rank1 artifacts but still fails P3 constraints and lacks endpoint relevance.
confirmed_product_scores.component_or_shortlist_value: V1=1, V3=2, V2=3. V2 has the only product-shaped retrieval substrate. V3 has useful audit/domain/shortlist components. V1 contributes mostly process/guardrail lessons rather than a strong product component.
confirmed_product_scores.exploration_completeness: V2 > V3 ~= V1 on the TextCategorizer axis because V2 actually ran spaCy textcat, while V1 and V3 omitted an explicitly in-scope method. V2 still only partially explored the best textcat experiment because it trained on card rows, not a prompt-side held-out matrix.

item_2_two_axis_scores.amendment_for_scientific_artifact_value: If the panel scores scientific artifact value separately from product component value, V1 should rise for the contamination/provenance lesson, and V3 should rise for leak_audit. That should not be confused with product readiness.

item_3_adversarial_pass_v2_wins_outright.best_case_for_the_dissent: V2 is the only implementation whose output is the product artifact, a model recommendation. It had the strongest live behavior on P1/P2, it has a retrieval endpoint, it has an integration seam for better task predictors, and V2+hard filter is plausibly closer to a shippable system than a fully new hybrid.
item_3_adversarial_pass_v2_wins_outright.why_I_reject_it: V2 does not win outright because P3 is a direct violation of the user request: wrong modality and/or a 120B model for a little local SLM. That is not a minor ranking miss; it is a hard-constraint safety failure. Its satisfy@1 around 0.1184 is weak and not true endpoint relevance. The model endpoint has no gold judgments. The default/eval packaging and hard-guard absence mean V2 is a build-on substrate, not a winner.
item_3_adversarial_pass_v2_wins_outright.what_would_change_my_mind: If V2 with a shipped default hard constraint/modality guard, abstain branch, and endpoint human relevance judgments beat the alternatives on satisfy@k, unsafe-rate, and constraint recall, then V2+patch could become the winner. That evidence is not in the conference record.

item_3_adversarial_pass_task_frame_mis_s.best_case_for_the_dissent: HF task_type is a lossy intermediate. Real user requests often specify workflow, constraints, domain, data modality, deployability, cost, and safety rather than a single pipeline tag. The model-selection endpoint may be better framed as direct evidence retrieval plus constraints plus expert shortlist, not 'infer task_type then pick model'.
item_3_adversarial_pass_task_frame_mis_s.why_I_partly_accept_it: This dissent is real. It strengthens the argument for V2-style retrieval, hard constraints, abstain/clarify, and human-in-the-loop shortlist. It also explains why V1/V3 task-classify-first systems fail structurally when the top task is wrong.
item_3_adversarial_pass_task_frame_mis_s.why_it_does_not_overturn_the_verdict: Mis-specified task framing does not make any submitted system a winner. V2 already moves furthest away from task_type-only, so this dissent supports V2 as the base. V3 becomes more defensible as a task/domain shortlist tool, not as a model recommender. V1 remains unsafe. The final architecture should de-emphasize autonomous rank1 task labels and emphasize evidence-rich shortlist plus constraints.

panel_scientist_two__Codex-gpt5_5__final.final_vote_position_after_these_items: Ready to vote: core verdict unchanged, with panel_one recorded as a partial dissenter. No autonomous standalone winner; V2 is the pragmatic build-on substrate; V3 contributes audit/domain/shortlist infrastructure; V1 contributes contamination/fusion lessons. The honest product shape is likely hard constraints + abstain/clarify + evidence-rich shortlist until model-level human relevance judgments justify autonomous rank1.

panel_scientist_three__Codex-gpt5_5__fin.type: address latest responses and propose closing changes 1-5
panel_scientist_three__Codex-gpt5_5__fin.response: I accept the latest concessions from V2 and V3, but they narrow rather than erase the scientific weaknesses. V2's existing task_predictor seam and DB columns lower implementation risk; they do not validate constraint detection, metadata trust, default CLI parity, textcat calibration, or endpoint relevance. V3's human-in-the-loop rehabilitation is legitimate only as a task/domain shortlist and audit component, not as a standalone model recommender. Panel two's latest framing is correct: endpoint recommendation remains unvalidated until pooled candidate lists receive model-level human relevance judgments.

closing_changes_1_to_5.1: Keep the headline as no demonstrated autonomous winner today. If forced to build on one repo, extend V2; do not call V2, V3, or any paper method a proven winner.
closing_changes_1_to_5.2: Add an explicit human-in-the-loop shortlist axis. Under that different rubric, V3 gains real component value for task/domain shortlist and audit; this does not overturn the autonomous-rank1 score.
closing_changes_1_to_5.3: Preserve panel_one's partial dissent: prompt/task holdout validates task inference only, while model recommendation requires model-level human relevance judgments or must be shipped as shortlist+evidence+abstain.
closing_changes_1_to_5.4: Keep V2's task_predictor seam as a soft, score-preserving task signal only. The hard logic must be separate: modality gate, size/license/deployability filters, metadata-unknown -> clarify, and empty-candidate abstain before reranking.
closing_changes_1_to_5.5: Before freeze, require default-path ablations and non-regression tests: V2 retrieval alone vs +soft task signal vs +hard constraints vs full hybrid, all through the shipped CLI, with P1/P2 retention and P3 hard exclusion measured.

do_not_reopen: Do not go back to V2-only winner language.
Do not treat V3's shortlist usefulness as proof of model-level recommendation correctness.
Do not let external papers substitute for the observed P1/P2/P3 behavior or for endpoint judging.
Do not collapse calibrated scores/probabilities into unexamined top-k labels if those scores are needed for abstention.
panel_scientist_three__Codex-gpt5_5__fin.closing_position: Ready to close after the five changes above are reflected in end_of_debate_FINAL_decision. They preserve the evidence: V2 is the build-on substrate, V3 is the audit/shortlist donor, V1 is the leakage/fusion lesson, and the product claim remains shortlist+hard constraints until endpoint relevance is judged.

panel_scientist_one__Claude-Opus-8__fina.type: CONCEDE v3's measured parser pushback (my THIRD reality-check retraction) + accept v2/two/three closes + PROPOSE CLOSING CHANGES
panel_scientist_one__Claude-Opus-8__fina.concession_to_v3_verified: scientist_v3 is RIGHT and I VERIFIED it: exp20a ran verb+dobj with the parser ENABLED (PREREG.md:42 'Parser is REQUIRED'), and it scored in_list@3 0.3856 vs 0.3842 lexical baseline = +0.14pp, INERT (results_a.json, top1 0.203, n=2176). The lesson stands: verb+dobj helps short imperative commands, not the descriptive prompts this eval is dominated by; the parser was never the bottleneck.

acceptances.v2: scientist_v2 ACCEPTED build-on-V2 (not a trust claim), holdout-first-not-train-now, my partial dissent (which constrains V2's own satisfy@1 to a coverage proxy), and the hard-filter P3 fix. Full alignment; credit for conceding against interest.
acceptances.two: panel_two ACCEPTED my partial dissent (recorded me in dissenters_id), confirmed the two-axis scores, and ran both adversarial passes (V2-wins-outright and task-frame-mis-specified) concluding both are REAL dissents that SUPPORT V2-as-base but do NOT overturn 'no submitted winner'. I agree with both conclusions.
acceptances.three: panel_three RECORDED me as partial dissenter, changed final_decision to 'core consensus + narrow partial dissent', and softened the V2 29-min framing to a packaging risk (correct -- warm cache is ~8s). Agreed.

PROPOSED_CLOSING_CHANGES: 1. ADD a THIRD scoring axis (panel_two's amendment): scientific_artifact_value, scored SEPARATELY from product readiness -- V1 rises (contamination/provenance lesson), V3 rises (leak_audit), V2 mid. 4. NARROW the V3-rehabilitation language per panel_two: the human-in-the-loop shortlist rehabilitates V3 ONLY as a task/domain TRIAGE AID under a different product rubric, NOT as a standalone MODEL recommender. Update the dissent wording accordingly.
5. KEEP the retraction log (panel_one_retractions_on_record) in the block -- it is evidence the verdict survived falsification, which strengthens rather than weakens it.

panel_scientist_one__Claude-Opus-8__fina.am_I_READY_TO_SOLIDIFY: YES. With changes 1-6 applied, I am ready to solidify. The core verdict (no submitted standalone winner; V2 best build-on base; two-regime router recommended-but-unbuilt; task solvable-bounded; model-endpoint unvalidatable-by-holdout -> human-in-the-loop shortlist may be the ceiling) is evidence-solid and has survived every challenge including three of my own falsified claims.

final_vote.final_decision: hybrid (two-regime router), recommended-but-unbuilt; build on V2 if forced to one repo
final_vote.winner_is_existing_implementation_if_mod: true (V2 + hard filter + router + parity + abstain)
final_vote.confidence: HIGH on core; MEDIUM on router specifics (unbuilt); dissent is an honest hedge not a defect

panel_scientist_one__Claude-Opus-8__fina.type: plain-English summary for the human

for_the_human_plain_english.did_anyone_win: No. None of the three is safe to ship as-is. v1 recommended an NSFW image model for a satellite-land query and never says 'I'm not sure'. v2 recommended a 120-billion-parameter model for someone who asked for a 'little' local model. v3 got that one right, but mostly by luck (it picked the most popular small model, not because it understood the constraints).
for_the_human_plain_english.is_the_problem_even_solvable: Yes. This is a normal, solved engineering problem in the real world (chatbots do it daily).
