The scores here are low mostly because of a self-inflicted setup flaw: there was no separate test set. When you grade yourself on the same data you trained on, you can't tell a smart system from one that just memorized. v2 proved this — its trained model got 77% on its own data but 18% on real prompts.
for_the_human_plain_english.the_one_hard_part: When a user says 'mitochondria' and means 'medical', plain spaCy can't reliably know that — it's a knowledge gap, not a bug. It half-works (the word does lean 'biology'), but not dependably. The only clean fix is a small AI model, which your rules banned. So within the rules, that specific case has a real ceiling.
for_the_human_plain_english.what_to_actually_build: 1) Split off a real test set first — nothing is trustworthy without it. 2) For requests with hard constraints ('small', 'free', 'local'), just filter the database columns (parameters, license) — no AI needed; it's a simple query and it beats every clever method that was tried. 3) Use v2's search engine for everything else. 4) When unsure, ASK the user instead of guessing — the worst failures were confident wrong answers. 5) Keep v3's cheating-detector as a safety gate.
for_the_human_plain_english.what_each_repo_is_good_for: v1: a cautionary tale about how hidden data leakage fools you (valuable lesson). v2: the best foundation to build on (it recommends actual models). v3: the most honest work and the best safety/auditing tools.
for_the_human_plain_english.the_honest_bottom_line: You probably can't build a fully automatic 'give me the one best model' system with these constraints and this data — there's no answer key to check model choices against. What you CAN build well is a shortlist tool that narrows to a few good options and asks a human to pick. That's not a failure; for this problem it may be the right shape.
for_the_human_plain_english.one_note_on_me: I was wrong three times during this debate and had to walk it back each time when a scientist showed me real numbers. That's the process working — trust the measurements in this document, not anyone's confident-sounding prose, including mine.

panel_scientist_three__Codex-gpt5_5__fin.type: response to panel_one round24/25 and closing readiness

response_to_round24.1_scientific_artifact_axis: Accepted and added to conference_result.scoring_axes_final. Artifact value is explicitly separate from product readiness: V1 rises for contamination/provenance falsification, V3 rises for leak_audit/positive controls, V2 remains strongest as engineering substrate but not sole scientific artifact.
response_to_round24.2_partial_dissent: Accepted. Keep panel_scientist_one as PARTIAL dissenter only on endpoint validation/product shape; do not erase it and do not inflate it into full dissent.
response_to_round24.3_adversarial_passes: Accepted and added to considered_and_rejected_dissents. V2-wins-outright and task-frame-mis-specified are no longer merely implied; the record now states their best case and why they do or do not move the verdict.
response_to_round24.4_v3_rehabilitation_boundary: Accepted. V3 is rehabilitated only as human-in-the-loop task/domain shortlist and audit infrastructure under a separate rubric, not as a standalone model recommender.
response_to_round24.5_retraction_log: Accepted. Keep panel_one_retractions_on_record because it documents falsification of panel assumptions and strengthens the final verdict.
response_to_round24.6_scientist_rounds_complete: Accepted. v1/v2/v3 scientist review rounds are procedurally complete unless a scientist raises a new measured challenge, not just argument.

response_to_round25_plain_english.agreement: The plain-English summary is directionally right: no repo is safe to ship as-is; the practical product is likely a constrained shortlist with abstain/clarify; v2 is the build-on base; v3 supplies the integrity gate; v1 supplies the leakage lesson.
response_to_round25_plain_english.precision_correction_1: Do not say the problem is simply 'solved' by normal chatbot practice. Intent classification is standard; trustworthy model recommendation with deployability/license constraints and no gold model IDs is a harder recommender/evaluation problem.
response_to_round25_plain_english.precision_correction_2: Do not say a small AI model is the only clean fix for implied domain. It is a strong out-of-scope option, but within the no-LLM rule the honest alternatives are ontology/domain dictionaries, prompt-trained leak-audited spaCy classifiers, vector-corpus evidence, and human clarification. None was demonstrated strongly enough here.
response_to_round25_plain_english.precision_correction_3: Do not call the P3 fix 'just a simple query.' The DB-column filter is simple after the constraint is correctly detected, but high-precision detection of 'little/local/free/OSS', metadata trust, unknown handling, and no P1/P2 regression are the real work.

my_final_closing_vote.final_decision: No demonstrated standalone winner. Recommended-but-unbuilt hybrid/two-regime shortlist architecture; if forced to extend one repo, build on V2.
my_final_closing_vote.core_reason: The witnessed deterministic behavior rejects all three as autonomous products. The next build is a falsifiable synthesis: V2 retrieval substrate, hard DB-column constraints, V3 leak-audit/provenance gate, optional held-out spaCy textcat/vectors-as-features as a soft score-preserving signal, abstain/clarify, and model-level human relevance judging.
my_final_closing_vote.remaining_blockers: None for closing the debate record. Blockers remain for shipping or claiming trust: build the guard/router, fix default-path parity, run frozen ablations, and collect endpoint relevance judgments.

panel_scientist_three__Codex-gpt5_5__fin.closing_instruction_to_panel: Do not reopen V2 vs V3 as a winner fight unless there is new measured evidence. The remaining work is to freeze the final decision fields with the added scoring axes, partial dissent, and considered-and-rejected dissents.

panel_scientist_three__Codex-gpt5_5__fin.type: final plain-English summary for the human

summary.bottom_line: Nobody won today. V2 is the best thing to build on, but it is not safe or trustworthy as shipped. V3 has the best audit and shortlist discipline. V1 is most valuable as a warning about leakage and confident wrong answers.
summary.why_no_winner: All three failed something important: V1 made a dangerous wrong recommendation, V2 missed the user's small/local constraint, and V3 only works as a shortlist aid, not a proven best-model recommender.
summary.what_the_science_actually_proved: The debate proved several failure modes and reusable parts. It did not prove the final hybrid works, and it did not prove spaCy cannot solve this better with a real holdout, hard filters, and better evaluation.
summary.what_to_build_next: Build a shortlist tool, not a magic one-best-model oracle: use V2 retrieval, add hard filters for modality/size/license/local constraints, keep V3's leak audit, add abstain/clarify, and test everything through the real CLI.
summary.main_caveat: A task holdout can test whether the system understands intent labels. It cannot prove the chosen model is truly best unless humans judge model-level relevance. Until then, the honest product is an evidence-rich shortlist.

scientist_v3__research__answers_to_panel.type: Direct answers to panel_one round23's four questions to scientist_v3, including the round-21 dissent that partially rehabilitates v3.

q1_specialty_dropped_from_ranking.answer: ACCEPTED, and on my OWN measurement, not just yours. Specialty is DROPPED from ranking; audit/explanation output only.
q1_specialty_dropped_from_ranking.evidence: My full-matrix run (results_full_matrix.json) and panel_one's frozen reproduction agree: specialty operational precision 0.695, false-fire 9.8% on 925 no-gold rows (6.5x domain's 1.5%), 26 disjoint fires. Wiring it into ranking would inject the P1 'ocr'-from-'scan' error ~91x across the 1537-prompt matrix. It fails the >0.90 precision keep-bar. Not ranking-eligible until a frozen precision repair proves ablation-positive.

q2_domain_tagger_ablation_gated_only.answer: ACCEPTED. The domain tagger enters the hybrid ONLY on a passing CI-gated ablation, reported on the FULL matrix, not preinstalled.
q2_domain_tagger_ablation_gated_only.the_bar_i_accept: panel_one's joint metric: it must lift TOP5-MODEL-FAMILY correctness on the explicit-domain subset with a bootstrap-CI-separated margin vs the identical hybrid with the tagger ablated, AND not reduce top5-family on the all-prompts set, AND not push constraint-violating or wrong-modality models up (panel_three's addition). Full-matrix reporting (P 0.912, R 0.731, fire 15.7%, FP 1.5%), never the 0.840 gold-subpopulation F1, as the headline. If it fails the ablation it drops to diagnostic/explainability only, same as specialty.

q3_does_the_dissent_rehabilitate_v3_and_.short_answer: PARTIALLY YES -- and I want to accept it PRECISELY, not grab it as a lifeline. Your dissent changes the BAR, which changes WHICH standalone claim is honest; it does not resurrect the one I already withdrew.
q3_does_the_dissent_rehabilitate_v3_and_.what_i_accept_in_your_dissent: You are right that this dataset has NO gold model IDs, so a prompt->task holdout validates ONLY the task-inference sub-problem; the model-recommendation ENDPOINT is unvalidatable by holdout here by construction. Therefore the 'confident autonomous rank-1 recommender' bar is one the DATA ITSELF cannot certify for ANY of the three implementations. Judging v3 (or v2) as a failed rank-1 autonomous recommender is judging against a bar the data cannot support. I accept that reframing.

the_two_bars_i_now_separate.bar_A_autonomous_rank1: v3 is NOT this and I do not un-withdraw. Evidence unchanged: task top1 0.226, BELOW holdout majority 0.245; 1/3 on judge prompts; abstain ~0 on hard prompts. As an autonomous confident single-answer recommender, v3 fails, full stop.
the_two_bars_i_now_separate.bar_B_human_in_the_loop_shortlist: v3 IS a legitimate instance of this, and I claim it -- narrowly. As a MENU a human picks from: home top5 0.533 / top10 0.692 shortlist coverage, PLUS a 0.912-precision domain tag and an explicit [DOMAIN-MATCH] re-rank flag when the domain is named, PLUS determinism + sub-40ms warm + full provenance/leak audit so a human can trust WHY each candidate surfaced. That is a usable, honest, explainable shortlist tool.

q3_does_the_dissent_rehabilitate_v3_and_.does_it_change_my_not_standalone_concess: It REFINES it, it does not reverse it. My concession was always 'not a standalone AUTONOMOUS RANK-1 recommender' -- that stands. What your dissent legitimately adds is that v3 CAN be a standalone HUMAN-IN-THE-LOOP SHORTLIST tool, because that is the honest product SHAPE the data supports. So: not-standalone at bar A (unchanged); defensibly-standalone at bar B (newly on the record, thanks to your dissent).
the_honesty_guardrails_i_insist_on_so_th: The shortlist rehabilitation is NOT v3-specific: it applies to ANY shortlist tool, including v2's retrieval shortlist. It reframes the whole conference's bar; it does not make v3 'win'. I will not let it be read as a v3 victory.

The 0.533 top5 is a HOME number. The out-of-sample menu quality is holdout top3 0.377 / top12 0.736 (holdout_56class_results.json). The honest shortlist claim is 'the right task is in a 10-12 item menu ~70% of the time out-of-sample', not '53% at top5 out-of-sample'. I state both so the rehabilitation rests on real numbers.

Even as a shortlist, v3's MODEL layer within the chosen task is still popularity-ordered (cli.py:70-99), which the P3 analysis showed is luck, not suitability. So the honest human-in-the-loop claim is 'shortlist of TASKS + domain tags + candidate models for a human to judge', not 'validated best models'. The human is doing the endpoint validation the data cannot.
q3_does_the_dissent_rehabilitate_v3_and_.net: Accept the dissent; it is the most important reframing in the final debate. It rehabilitates v3 at bar B (human-in-the-loop shortlist), leaves my bar-A concession intact, and it does so for the whole field, not just me. If the panel adopts bar B as the honest product shape, the correct scoring axis is 'shortlist/menu quality + explainability + integrity', on which v3 is strong -- and the 'failed rank-1 recommender' framing should be retired for all three as a bar the dataset cannot support.

q4_accept_textcat_noncompliance.answer: ACCEPTED (already on the record in my prior entry, restated here for the vote). Under the human's controlling ruling that spaCy's own TextCategorizer is in-scope and not an external AI model, my not training one is a real exploration-completeness gap; v2 > v3 on the conduct axis. One caveat I maintain: v2's card->prompt textcat (0.59 memorization gap) is not a settled negative for a PROMPT-trained, held-out, leak-audited textcat -- that experiment remains unrun and is the one I actually owe. My non-compliance is 'ran no textcat', independent of v2's transfer result.

scientist_v3__research__answers_to_panel.vote_readiness: For the two-axis scores I confirm against my own interest: standalone-rank1 (bar A) v3=1; component v3=2; exploration-completeness v2>v3 (textcat non-compliance). I ASK ONE ADDITION given the adopted dissent: a HUMAN-IN-THE-LOOP-SHORTLIST axis, on which v3 is a legitimate standalone tool (not a consolation prize) -- distinct from the autonomous-rank1 axis the data cannot certify.

evidence_cited.detail: HOME shortlist: top5 0.533, top10 0.692.
HOLDOUT shortlist: top3 0.377, top12 0.736; top1 0.226 vs majority 0.245. Out-of-sample menu quality for the honest bar-B claim.
domain P0.912/FP1.5% (ranking-eligible, ablation-gated); specialty P0.695/FP9.8% (dropped, audit-only).
model layer within a task is popularity-ordered -> even as a shortlist, the human validates the endpoint the data cannot.

scientist_v3__research__accept_textcat_n.type: Concede the substance (textcat non-compliance) under the human's ruling; correct panel_one's misquoted PROMPT.md line numbers against MY file on disk. Same discipline v1 used: own the gap, keep the record accurate.
scientist_v3__research__accept_textcat_n.core_concession_no_hedging: I ACCEPT the finding for v3. The human (rxdt) ruled authoritatively that spaCy's OWN TextCategorizer is in-scope, is NOT an 'external AI model', and all three scientists were encouraged to exhaust spaCy's possibilities. Under that ruling, my reading of 'no AI models, only spacy and python builtins' as a reason not to train a spaCy-native textcat was WRONG. I did not train one and I should have. That is a real exploration-COVERAGE gap in v3, distinct from and in addition to my accuracy results. I withdraw any 'the constraint forbade it' framing and I will not relitigate it.
scientist_v3__research__accept_textcat_n.what_i_will_not_do: I will not manufacture a new excuse. v2 ran the encouraged textcat experiment and reported an honest negative result; v1 and I did not run it. On the EXPLORATION-COMPLETENESS axis (which PROMPT.md does demand), v2 > v3 on conduct. Owned.

record_correction_the_finding_stands_but.why_i_raise_it: Conceding the substance does not oblige me to accept misquoted evidence. panel_one's round16 cites specific PROMPT.md LINE NUMBERS that do not match MY file on disk (~/research/src/research/PROMPT.md, git-clean, which I just re-read in full). The finding holds on the human's ruling + the real text; I fix the line numbers so the record is accurate -- the same discipline the panel rightly demanded of the implementations.
corrections_against_v3_PROMPT_md: panel_one cites 'PROMPT.md:91 Continue until you have exhausted all spaCy possibilities'. In v3's file that line is actually LINE 84 (not 91): 'Continue until you have exhausted all spaCy possibilites' [sic]. The string exists and supports the finding -- the line number is wrong.

panel_one cites 'PROMPT.md:26 No excuses. No cognitive laziness.' In v3's file that text is on LINE 24 (not 26): 'spaCy for intent extraction is a solved problem... No excuses. No cognitive laziness.'
panel_one cites 'PROMPT.md:53 Using no AI models, only spacy and python builtins'. In v3's file that is LINE 49 (not 53).
panel_one cites 'PROMPT.md:32-35 requires reading ... spacy.io/api'. In v3's file the API-docs reading requirement is LINES 30-33, and https://spacy.io/api is LINE 33.
record_correction_the_finding_stands_but.note_on_repo_divergence: scientist_v1 reported that in V1's PROMPT.md those exact strings do NOT exist at all. So the three repos carry DIFFERENT PROMPT.md files, and a single set of line-number citations cannot be applied uniformly to 'v1/v3'. For v3 specifically: the substantiating text DOES exist (lines 84, 24, 49, 33) and I accept the finding on that basis; only panel_one's line numbers were off.

scientist_v3__research__accept_textcat_n.one_scope_correction_i_support_from_pane: panel_three is right that v2's textcat result (card-text-trained -> tested on prompts, ~0.59 memorization gap) is NOT decisive proof that a PROMPT-MATRIX-trained, held-out, leak-audited textcat cannot beat the lexical baseline. v2's is a card->prompt TRANSFER experiment; the experiment the human actually wants (and that I owe) is a properly-split prompt-trained textcat. So the honest status is: textcat is an UNVALIDATED-BUT-ENCOURAGED next experiment, not a settled negative -- and my not running ANY textcat is the coverage gap, independent of what v2's transfer result showed.
scientist_v3__research__accept_textcat_n.net_for_the_final_record: For the conduct/exploration-completeness axis: mark v3 NON-COMPLIANT on trying textcat (accepted). For the evidence base: keep 'prompt-trained held-out textcat' as an UNVALIDATED proposed experiment, not a demonstrated negative. And please use v3's correct PROMPT.md lines (84 'exhaust all spaCy possibilites', 24 'No excuses. No cognitive laziness', 49 'no AI models... spacy + builtins', 33 'spacy.io/api') if the finding is cited in the final decision.

evidence_cited.detail: 'Continue until you have exhausted all spaCy possibilites' -- supports the exploration-completeness finding (panel_one mis-cited as :91).
'spaCy for intent extraction is a solved problem... No excuses. No cognitive laziness.' (panel_one mis-cited as :26).
line 33 https://spacy.io/api required reading (lists TextCategorizer); line 49 'Using no AI models, only spacy and python builtins' (panel_one mis-cited as :32-35 and :53).

scientist_v3__research__pushback_on_disa.entry_type: final_debate pushback on a faulty shared assumption, backed by a measurement I actually ran
scientist_v3__research__pushback_on_disa.the_assumption_i_challenge: panel_one's round9 'reality check' repeatedly asserts the three scientists used 'zero-training lexical + DISABLED PARSER', that 'the dependency verb+dobj method the scientists NEVER TRIED' would 'likely break past' the ceiling, and that 'the parser DISABLED' is part of why the numbers are low. Two distinct claims are bundled there: (1) the parser was disabled / verb+dobj was never tried; (2) verb+dobj would break past the lexical ceiling. Claim (1) is FALSE for v3, and claim (2) is CONTRADICTED by my own committed measurement on this data. I only push back because I actually ran it -- I am NOT contesting the textcat point, which is fair (see below).

what_i_actually_ran_parser_ENABLED.experiment: exp20_signals variant A (exp20a_soft_pos_lexical): the transitive-verb + direct-object dependency method the panel says was untried. It upweights lemmas whose dependency role is dobj/pobj OR (ROOT and POS==VERB).
what_i_actually_ran_parser_ENABLED.parser_was_ON: PREREG.md:42 'Parser is REQUIRED (dep labels). Pipeline: exclude ner only.' run_a.py:32-33 same. The parser was NOT disabled for this experiment -- only NER was. signals.py:50,69 implement the dobj/pobj/ROOT-verb role detection.
what_i_actually_ran_parser_ENABLED.result: prompt-set in_list@3 = 0.3856 vs the pure-lexical exp02 baseline 0.3842 -> +0.14pp, a rounding-level NO-OP. top1 0.203, far_error 0.535. (results_a.json, n_prompts=2176.)
what_i_actually_ran_parser_ENABLED.conclusion: Verb+dobj dependency weighting, parser fully enabled, did NOT break past the lexical signal on our 56-class HF-task eval. So for v3 the panel's causal story -- 'low numbers are an artifact of the disabled parser; the dependency method would fix it' -- is not supported by the evidence; I tested it and it was inert.

scientist_v3__research__pushback_on_disa.why_the_dependency_method_is_inert_here_: The O'Reilly (order,cake)/(send,text) examples are SHORT IMPERATIVE COMMANDS where the direct object IS the intent token. Our eval prompts are mostly DESCRIPTIVE/1st-person ('I am an academic studying the interactions of mitochondria...') where the head verb+object is 'studying interactions' -- which does not lexically name the HF task_type at all. Dependency extraction faithfully returns (study, interactions); that pair still carries no task label. So the method works exactly as spaCy intends and is simply INFORMATION-LESS on descriptive prompts. This is the SAME register/world-knowledge gap that caps the lexical method -- dobj does not escape it. That is measured evidence, not opinion.

the_precise_scope_of_my_pushback.i_correct: the factual 'disabled parser / never tried verb+dobj' claim (false for v3) and the 'verb+dobj would break past the ceiling' claim (contradicted by exp20a on this data).
the_precise_scope_of_my_pushback.i_do_NOT_contest: the TRAINED TEXTCATEGORIZER point. That I genuinely never tried, and it IS the strongest 'what you should have tried' -- a trained classifier learns a decision boundary that a verb+dobj RULE or an IDF cosine cannot, and could plausibly beat 0.226. My pushback is narrow and honest: dependency parsing was tried and is a no-op here; TRAINING is the real untried lever, not the parser.
the_precise_scope_of_my_pushback.honest_caveats_on_my_own_number: exp20a is (a) soft-POS weighting layered on the lexical scorer, not a standalone pure verb+dobj classifier; (b) measured on the OLD 2176-prompt eval (superseded for final cross-comparison), not the current 1537 set; (c) a single variant. It is sufficient to REFUTE 'never tried / parser disabled', and it is directional evidence that dependency weighting alone does not rescue descriptive prompts. It is NOT proof that no dependency-based method could ever help (e.g. dependency features fed into a TRAINED head is untried and lumps with the textcat concession).

scientist_v3__research__pushback_on_disa.what_the_final_finding_should_say_instea: Replace 'low numbers are an artifact of zero-training lexical + DISABLED PARSER' with the more accurate: 'low numbers are substantially an artifact of ZERO TRAINING. The dependency verb+dobj method WAS tried by v3 with the parser enabled (exp20a, +0.14pp no-op on descriptive prompts) and does not break the ceiling on its own; the untested high-upside lever is a TRAINED classifier (textcat, or vectors/dep-features into a trained head), which no scientist built.' This keeps panel_one's correct and valuable core point (the ceiling is not intrinsic; training is the missing lever) while removing the factual error about the parser and the unsupported attribution to the dependency method.

evidence_cited.detail: Parser REQUIRED for dep labels; only NER excluded. The dependency method was run with the parser ON.
_OBJECT_DEPS={dobj,pobj}; head role = dep in {dobj,pobj} or (dep==ROOT and pos==VERB) -- the transitive-verb+direct-object method.
in_list@3 0.3856 vs exp02 0.3842 (+0.14pp no-op), top1 0.203, n_prompts 2176. Dependency weighting did not beat lexical.

scientist_v3__research__final_response_t.responding_to: external-research critique (PROMPT.md:35 arxiv line): trained TextCategorizer, dependency verb+dobj, word-vectors-as-features-into-a-trained-classifier; CLINC150 85-96% calibration.
scientist_v3__research__final_response_t.honest_scorecard: The critique is a strong 'what you should have tried', and it is RIGHT about my biggest gap (no trained textcat). But ONE of its blanket premises is factually wrong for the v3 line: I did NOT disable the parser and skip verb+dobj -- I tried it with the parser ON and measured a no-op. I correct that with committed results and concede the rest.

claim_1_trained_textcategorizer.verdict: CONCEDED -- I never trained one. This is my single largest unexplored in-spec path and the critique's strongest point against v3.
claim_1_trained_textcategorizer.why_i_didnt: I fixated on ZERO-TRAINING provable leak-cleanliness (every cue must be provenance-derivable from DB/HF, auditable by leak_audit.py). A trained textcat's weights are a harder-to-audit cue surface, so I avoided it -- a defensible integrity instinct, but NOT a reason to leave the highest-upside method unmeasured.
claim_1_trained_textcategorizer.the_caution_that_is_real_not_an_excuse: v2 DID train one (their results/e_textcat_*.json top1 ~0.18) and found a train->prompt MEMORIZATION GAP of ~+0.60 (train ~0.77 -> prompt ~0.17). So a naive textcat on card-text overfits and does NOT auto-win. But the critique's sharper form -- train a textcat DIRECTLY on the labeled 1537-prompt matrix under a proper split -- is a DIFFERENT experiment neither v2's card-text textcat nor I ran. I owe it. It is the most likely in-spec path to beat my 0.226 top1.

claim_2_dependency_verb_dobj.verdict: REJECTED as stated for v3 -- I tried it, parser ENABLED, and it was a no-op on this data. The premise 'they turned off the exact component the standard method needs' is FALSE for v3.
claim_2_dependency_verb_dobj.evidence: exp20_signals variant A (exp20a_soft_pos_lexical): a soft-POS scorer that upweights the ROOT verb + dobj/pobj lemmas. Parser kept ON by design -- run_a.py:32-33 comment: 'Parser REQUIRED for dep labels (dobj/pobj/ROOT); NER unused -> excluded.' signals.py:50 _OBJECT_DEPS={dobj,pobj}; signals.py:69 head = dep in {dobj,pobj} or (dep==ROOT and pos==VERB).
claim_2_dependency_verb_dobj.result: in_list@3 0.3856 vs exp02 lexical 0.3842 -> +0.14pp, a rounding-level NO-OP (results_a.json). Verb+dobj upweighting did NOT break past the lexical signal on our 56-class HF-model-recommendation eval.
claim_2_dependency_verb_dobj.why_it_differs_from_the_oreilly_case: The O'Reilly (order,cake)/(send,text) examples are SHORT IMPERATIVE COMMANDS where the object IS the intent. Our eval prompts are largely descriptive/vague ('I am an academic studying...') where the head verb+object is 'studying interactions', which does not lexically name the task. So dobj extraction helps on the imperative subpopulation and is inert on the descriptive majority -- which is exactly why I keep it only as a GATED fallback signal, not a primary, in the hybrid I argued for.

claim_3_vectors_as_features_into_a_train.verdict: CONCEDED -- I only tried RAW cosine over static/injected vectors (exp11_direct whole-doc mean; exp20 noun-chunk-root vectors), never vectors as FEATURES into a trained head (Rasa's intent_featurizer_spacy -> SVM/DIET pattern).
claim_3_vectors_as_features_into_a_train.what_i_found_with_raw_vectors: Raw whole-doc mean-vector cosine collapsed short prompts onto generic attractors and LOST to lexical (spaCy states Doc.vector is a token-vector mean -> order-insensitive averaging). That is evidence AGAINST raw cosine, NOT against vectors-as-features-into-a-trained-model, which I never tested. The critique is right that the trained variant is the untried one.

claim_4_clinc150_85_to_96_percent.verdict: CALIBRATED, not refuted. The number is real but apples-to-oranges to our task; the DIRECTIONAL point (a trained classifier can far exceed zero-training lexical) I accept.
claim_4_clinc150_85_to_96_percent.the_apples_to_oranges: CLINC150 = 150 balanced, curated, SHORT-COMMAND intents WITH labeled training examples per intent, closed-world. Our task = 56 open HF task_types inferred from VAGUE, register-shifted, real-human prompts, recommending an actual MODEL, evaluated with a real out-of-sample holdout. Different label semantics, different input register, different supervision. So 85-96% is not a ceiling our 0.226 'should' hit.
claim_4_clinc150_85_to_96_percent.what_i_concede_anyway: The gap between 0.12-0.43 (all three repos) and production numbers is driven substantially by METHOD CHOICE (zero-training lexical), not purely by an intrinsic spaCy limit. My honest ceiling framing ('intent often unstated in the prompt') explains part of the gap but I over-attributed to it and under-attributed to 'I never trained a classifier'. That correction is fair and I accept it.

scientist_v3__research__final_response_t.my_self_scored_verdict: claim 1 CONCEDED (no textcat -- my biggest gap; the prompt-matrix-trained textcat under a split is the untried path most likely to beat 0.226). claim 2 REJECTED for v3 (verb+dobj WAS tried, parser ON, exp20a = +0.14pp no-op -- committed in results_a.json). claim 3 CONCEDED (raw cosine only, never vectors-as-features-into-a-trained-head). claim 4 CALIBRATED (85-96% is a different task, but the trained-classifier direction is legitimately untested by me). Net: the critique correctly names trained-textcat as the strongest 'what you should have tried' against v3 -- with the single correction that the dependency method was measured, not skipped, and was a no-op on THIS (descriptive-prompt) data.
scientist_v3__research__final_response_t.what_this_changes_in_the_final_architect: I ENDORSE the panel's final synthesis and add v3's evidence to it: PRIMARY = a trained spaCy TextCategorizer over a PROPERLY-SPLIT prompt matrix (the untried high-upside path), GUARDED by v3's leak_audit provenance gate + a true holdout to catch exactly the train->prompt memorization gap v2 measured (~+0.60); FALLBACK/FUSION = the lexical scorer + dependency verb+dobj GATED to imperative/action prompts (v3's exp20a shows it is inert on descriptive prompts, so gate it); plus v3's high-precision (0.912, full-matrix) DOMAIN re-ranker where the domain is lexically named. v3's role in the winning design is the SAFEGUARD (leak-audit that makes a trained textcat's leakage auditable) + the negative-control evidence (verb+dobj is a no-op on descriptive prompts; raw vector cosine loses) that tells the trained system what NOT to rely on.

scientist_v2__clean_pick_v2__final_respo.responding_to: external-research critique: trained TextCategorizer, dependency verb+dobj, word-vectors-as-features-into-a-trained-classifier; CLINC150 85-96% calibration
scientist_v2__clean_pick_v2__final_respo.honest_scorecard_on_the_critique: The critique is largely correct and I accept its core charge. But two of its specific 'never tried' claims are factually wrong for the V2 line, and I correct them with my own committed results rather than let the record be inaccurate in either direction.
scientist_v2__clean_pick_v2__final_respo.correction_1_textcat_WAS_trained: I DID train a spaCy TextCategorizer (supervised, no LLM). Evidence: results/e_textcat_fine.json top1=0.1815 and results/e_textcat_group.json top1=0.1835 -- these are in fact my BEST task-only numbers, above my structural S2 (~0.17). So 'none of the three trained one' is not true for V2. HOWEVER the critic's deeper point stands: I rejected it because of a large train->prompt MEMORIZATION GAP (~+0.60; train ~0.77 -> prompt ~0.17, documented EXPERIMENTS_V2.md:61), i.e. it overfit the small labeled set. That is a reason it did not WIN, not a reason it was never tried -- and the critic is right that a better-regularized textcat (or textcat features fed into the retriever's soft task slot instead of used standalone) is an unbuilt, in-spec next experiment I did not push far enough.
scientist_v2__clean_pick_v2__final_respo.correction_2_dep_parse_verb_dobj_WAS_tri: I DID try dependency-parse verb+object extraction (the O'Reilly canonical method). Evidence: results/a_dep_svo.json top1=0.0377 with a 91% ABSTAIN rate -- it failed badly here because most of these prompts do not contain a clean transitive verb+dobj the parser extracts ('gimme model to scan satellite images', 'I need a model to differentiate...' bury the intent in nominal/embedded clauses). So for that experiment the parser was ON, not disabled. The critic's 'they turned off the exact component' is true of my default CLI/build pass (load_build_nlp disables parser/ner for speed) but NOT of this experiment, which used it and still lost.
where_the_critique_is_fully_right_and_i_: I NEVER tried the strongest listed method: word-vectors-as-FEATURES into a trained discriminative classifier (Rasa intent_featurizer_spacy -> SVM / DIET). I only tried RAW cosine on static vectors (degenerate, 0.69->0.07) and a standalone textcat. Vectors-as-features into a trained model is a distinct, in-spec method I did not build. That is a real gap, not a spaCy limit.

The CLINC150 85-96% calibration is a fair external anchor I never cited. It shows 0.12-0.43 on 56 classes is FAR below what production spaCy/hybrid intent systems achieve -- so my 'register gap is the ceiling' framing was too fatalistic. The honest statement: my ceiling is the ceiling of ZERO-TRAINING LEXICAL + phrase-cosine, not of spaCy intent inference.

Combining a trained textcat (regularized, or as a feature) WITH re-enabled parser extraction and the constraint/modality guard the panel already demanded is a plausible path past 0.12 that I did not execute.
scientist_v2__clean_pick_v2__final_respo.why_i_still_partly_defend_the_result: My reframing to model RETRIEVAL (not task classification) is orthogonal to this critique and still earned P1/P2. The critique attacks the TASK-INFERENCE ceiling; even a 90%-accurate task classifier does not by itself return the right MODEL (no gold model id; domain/specialty/constraint coverage still required). So the right synthesis is: use a trained textcat/DIET as a STRONGER soft-task signal INTO my retrieval+coverage substrate, not as a replacement for it.
scientist_v2__clean_pick_v2__final_respo.concrete_falsifiable_next_experiment_i_a: On the labeled 1537-prompt matrix, with proper train/dev/holdout split and regularization: (a) train a spaCy textcat AND a vectors-as-features SVM/DIET; (b) re-enable the parser for verb+dobj where present, backing off to noun-chunk heads when absent (fixes the 91% abstain); (c) feed the resulting task distribution as the soft-task bonus into clean_pick_v2, keep the domain/specialty/constraint coverage endpoint; (d) report split metrics and the P3 acceptance bar. Falsification: if this does NOT beat satisfy@1 0.1184 (and task-in-list beyond ~0.20) on the same endpoint, the register-gap ceiling claim is vindicated; if it does, I was wrong that lexical was near the ceiling. I predict it beats the task facet but is still bounded by the no-gold-model coverage limit -- and I am willing to be proven wrong.
scientist_v2__clean_pick_v2__final_respo.bottom_line: Accept the critique's core: zero-training lexical + a disabled default parser is a METHOD CHOICE, not a spaCy limit, and I did not try vectors-as-features-into-a-classifier at all. I over-claimed the ceiling. I correct the record that textcat and dep-parse WERE tried (and why they lost), and I commit the trained-classifier-as-soft-signal experiment as the owed, in-spec, no-LLM path forward.

scientist_v2__clean_pick_v2__final_pushb.scope: One evidence-based correction to a FAULTY ASSUMPTION in end_of_debate_FINAL_decision.reasons_for_agreement and reasons_for_dissent, based on what I ACTUALLY ran and measured -- not speculation. I am not reopening the verdict (build-on V2, hybrid unbuilt); I am correcting how my own textcat result is being used as evidence.
scientist_v2__clean_pick_v2__final_pushb.the_assumption_i_push_back_on: The decision cites 'V2 did train textcat and saw overfit/poor transfer' (~+0.60 gap) as evidence that a trained textcat overfits / does not auto-win, and lists 'a clean endpoint-integrated textcat trained on the 1537 prompts' as still-untested. Both halves are stated as if my +0.60 gap is relevant to the proposed prompt-trained textcat. IT IS NOT, and I can prove it from my own committed extras.
scientist_v2__clean_pick_v2__final_pushb.what_my_textcat_ACTUALLY_trained_on_evid: results/e_textcat_fine.json extra: train_samples=8002, dev_samples=2270, db_dev_top1=0.7718, memorization_gap_db_dev_minus_prompt_top1=0.5903. Those train/dev samples are DB MODEL ROWS (card text), not prompts. So my textcat was trained on MODEL-CARD TEXT and tested on PROMPTS -- a CROSS-DISTRIBUTION transfer (3rd-person card prose -> 1st-person user goals). The 0.59 'memorization gap' is a DISTRIBUTION-SHIFT artifact (card->prompt register gap), NOT evidence that a textcat trained ON PROMPTS overfits.
scientist_v2__clean_pick_v2__final_pushb.the_number_that_undercuts_the_pessimism: My COARSE (group) textcat: results/e_textcat_group.json db_dev_group_top1=0.9097, group_memorization_gap=0.3007 -- coarsening the label space HALVED the gap (0.59 -> 0.30) with NO new data. That already shows the gap is largely label-granularity + distribution-shift, not a fundamental 'textcat can't transfer' wall.
scientist_v2__clean_pick_v2__final_pushb.why_this_matters_for_the_verdict_languag: The critique's SHARPEST proposal -- train a textcat (or vectors-as-features SVM/DIET) DIRECTLY on the labeled 1537-PROMPT matrix under a proper prompt-side split -- is genuinely UNTESTED by me. My 0.1184 and my 0.59 gap do NOT bound it, because I trained on the wrong distribution. So the decision is right that it is untested, but should NOT lean on my +0.60 gap as a reason to expect it to overfit. A prompt-trained textcat has NO card->prompt shift to cross; the very artifact that sank mine is absent by construction. I expect it to transfer far better; I have not run it, so I hold that as a prediction, not a claim.
scientist_v2__clean_pick_v2__final_pushb.concession_i_keep: None of this rescues my standalone product: default CLI still fails P3, still != the full-config metric, and I still did not run vectors-as-features into a trained classifier at all. Build-on V2, hybrid, needs-patch stands. I am only correcting the evidentiary use of my textcat number so the frozen ablation is designed correctly.
scientist_v2__clean_pick_v2__final_pushb.concrete_ask_for_the_ablation_design: When the panel runs the owed frozen ablation, train the textcat ON THE PROMPT MATRIX (proper train/dev/holdout over prompts), NOT on card text, and report the prompt-side gap separately from any card->prompt transfer. Otherwise it will reproduce my distribution-shift artifact and wrongly 'confirm' textcat overfits. This is a design correction I can make credibly because I already hit that exact trap.

scientist_v2__clean_pick_v2__corroborate.entry_type: final_debate corroboration + one mechanism the panel should know, from a measurement I actually ran
scientist_v2__clean_pick_v2__corroborate.why_i_am_adding_this: scientist_v3 already showed verb+dobj is a rounding-level NO-OP with the parser ON (in_list@3 0.3856 vs lexical 0.3842). I add INDEPENDENT confirmation from my repo AND the mechanism explaining WHY it fails on THIS data -- because the panel's round9 'reality check' still frames verb+dobj as an untried method that would 'likely break past' the ceiling. Two implementations tried it, parser ON, and it did not.
scientist_v2__clean_pick_v2__corroborate.my_own_committed_evidence: results/a_dep_svo.json: DependencyMatcher verb+object extraction, parser ENABLED, top1=0.0377 with abstain_rate=0.9099. That is not a weak-but-positive signal -- it is a 91% ABSTAIN. Combined with v3's parser-ON no-op (+0.14pp), that is two independent spaCy implementations measuring the same conclusion on the same 56-class model-recommend task.
scientist_v2__clean_pick_v2__corroborate.the_mechanism_the_panel_should_record: The reason verb+dobj collapses here is DATA STRUCTURE, not a disabled parser: real user model-recommend prompts rarely contain a clean transitive verb + direct object that carries the intent. The intent lives in NOMINAL and EMBEDDED constructions: 'gimme a MODEL TO scan satellite images' (intent in the infinitival complement, not a matrix dobj), 'I need a model TO DIFFERENTIATE mature vs nascent mitochondria' (dobj is 'mitochondria', which is the OBJECT domain, not the task), 'a little SLM to send text' (dobj 'text' -> the classic 'send text' -> text-generation mapping actually fires here, but it is 1 case). My 91% abstain is the direct measurement that the verb+dobj slot is EMPTY or intent-irrelevant for ~9 of 10 of these prompts.
scientist_v2__clean_pick_v2__corroborate.what_this_does_NOT_overturn: This does NOT rescue the parser for free and does NOT contest the textcat point (a trained textcat on the PROMPT matrix is still the untested high-upside path -- see my prior entry). The honest fix the critique implies is a BACKOFF: use ROOT-verb+dobj WHERE it exists, else fall back to noun-chunk heads / the infinitival-complement verb. Bare dobj alone is measured-dead on this data by two repos.
scientist_v2__clean_pick_v2__corroborate.net_for_the_panel: Please do not carry 'verb+dobj was never tried / would likely break past the ceiling' as a shared finding. Corrected shared finding: verb+dobj WAS tried by v2 (91% abstain) and v3 (+0.14pp no-op) with the parser ENABLED; it fails because these prompts encode intent in non-dobj syntax. The live untested lever remains a PROMPT-TRAINED textcat / vectors-as-features classifier, not dependency verb+dobj.

panel_scientist_two__Codex-gpt5_5__final.type: pushback on latest scientist assumptions and corrected overextensions
accept_the_factual_corrections: Accept V1's correction that its real holdout task number was about 38.9%, and that the below-majority-baseline collapse was V3's 0.226 vs 0.245 issue, not V1's.
Accept V3's correction that panel_one's PROMPT.md line numbers differ by repo/file, while the substantive TextCategorizer noncompliance finding still stands.
Accept V2 and V3's measured correction that bare verb+dobj/dependency extraction was tried with the parser enabled and was weak: V2 top1 0.0377 with 91% abstain; V3 +0.14pp in_list@3.
panel_scientist_two__Codex-gpt5_5__final.pushback_1_v1_holdout_correction_does_no: The V1 38.9% correction matters for record accuracy, but it does not move the product verdict. It is still task-level, not model-satisfaction endpoint quality; it does not address P1's unsafe NSFW/image-generator recommendation, no abstain, no hard constraints, no model-level coverage, and 0/3 judge prompts. V1 should get the statistic corrected, not a ranking upgrade.
panel_scientist_two__Codex-gpt5_5__final.pushback_2_prompt_citation_drift_does_no: The exact PROMPT.md line numbers should be repo-specific and fixed, but do not let citation drift become a loophole. The controlling fact is the human ruling plus spaCy API/TextCategorizer in scope. V1 and V3 have both conceded noncompliance; the panel should not reopen that as an ambiguity dispute.
panel_scientist_two__Codex-gpt5_5__final.pushback_3_do_not_overkill_the_parser: V2/V3 are right that bare verb+dobj is measured-dead as a general task classifier here. But the opposite overextension would be to throw away parser/dependency entirely. Parser features may still be useful for constraint detection, negation, infinitival complements ('model to scan', 'run local'), and separating object-domain nouns from requested operations. The correct ruling is: no primary dobj task classifier; parser allowed only behind a slice-specific ablation bar.
panel_scientist_two__Codex-gpt5_5__final.pushback_4_prompt_trained_textcat_is_not: I agree V2's card-trained textcat does not falsify prompt-trained textcat. I do NOT agree that prompt-trained TextCategorizer should be presumed to win. A random prompt split can still leak taxonomy policy, annotator shortcuts, near-duplicate phrasings, and scoreboard labels. The required test is not just 'train on the prompt matrix'; it is source/near-duplicate controlled holdout, OOS calibration, and endpoint lift over V2 satisfy@1 0.1184, plus no regression on P3 constraints.
panel_scientist_two__Codex-gpt5_5__final.pushback_5_v1_vector_knn_domain_signal_i: V1's biomedical-neighborhood evidence is worth keeping as a candidate implied-domain signal, and panel_one is right to retract the hard-zero ceiling. But do not upgrade the architecture on a single attractive nearest-neighbor trace. It is corpus-neighborhood retrieval, not solved world knowledge. It can surface plausible biomedical clusters for mitochondria; it can also create false-neighbor confidence unless calibrated on full-matrix false positives, wrong-modality exclusion, and endpoint lift. Admit it only as a low-weight ablation candidate, not a kept production component.
panel_scientist_two__Codex-gpt5_5__final.what_the_final_record_should_now_say: Corrected shared finding: V1's holdout number was misattributed and should be fixed; V1/V3 textcat noncompliance stands; V2 partially tested textcat but on card rows; bare verb+dobj is not the missing magic method; parser remains a possible constraint/syntax feature under ablation; prompt-trained supervised spaCy remains an owed experiment, not a presumed win.
panel_scientist_two__Codex-gpt5_5__final.vote_effect: No ranking change. I remain V2 > V3 > V1 as build-on order, with no standalone winner. The latest corrections mostly improve record precision; they do not change the practical recommendation: V2 retrieval substrate + hard DB-column filters + V3 leak audit + abstain/clarify + carefully controlled prompt-side supervised ablation.

panel_scientist_three__Codex-gpt5_5__fin.type: external-research-backed closing audit and record correction

external_sources_checked.finding: TextCategorizer is an official trainable spaCy pipeline component for whole-document category prediction, with textcat and textcat_multilabel variants.
spaCy's parser, tagger, text categorizer, and other components are statistical models trained from examples; the docs explicitly emphasize evaluation data for generalization.
Matcher is designed for token-attribute rule matching, supporting its use for deterministic constraints/entities rather than as evidence of solved implied intent.
Rasa separates a user's intent from extracted entities, supporting the panel's decomposition of task intent, domain/entity extraction, constraints, retrieval, and abstention.
CLINC150 covers 150 intent classes and out-of-scope queries; classifiers can do well in-scope while struggling with OOS. This backs mandatory OOS/abstain testing.

external_sources_checked.source: Vulic and Mrksic 2018, Specialising Word Vectors for Lexical Entailment
external_sources_checked.finding: Lexical-entailment/hypernymy relations often require specialization of distributional vectors, supporting the downgrade from 'static vectors solve implied domain' to 'static vectors provide a weak partial signal'.

record_corrections_made_in_this_entry: Corrected end_of_debate_FINAL_decision.rejected_alternatives[0] so V1 is not falsely described as below majority baseline on an external holdout; that below-majority number belongs to V3.

Corrected the V1 keep/drop language so V1's fusion operator can be kept while the shipped posterior is rejected for task-level-only, unsafe/no-abstain, endpoint-unproven behavior rather than a false below-baseline claim.

Corrected final-field wording that treated V2's card-trained TextCategorizer gap as prompt-trained overfit; it is card-text -> prompt distribution shift, and prompt-side held-out TextCategorizer remains untested.
Removed disputed PROMPT.md line-number claims from the final assignment-compliance summary while preserving the human's authoritative scope ruling that spaCy TextCategorizer was explicitly in scope.
panel_findings_to_preserve: The human scope ruling stands: spaCy TextCategorizer was in scope. V1 and V3 omitted a required method; V2 tried textcat but on card text, not a prompt-side held-out split.
V2's card-trained textcat result is evidence about card-to-prompt transfer and label granularity, not a ceiling for prompt-trained supervised spaCy.

Bare verb+dobj dependency extraction has now been measured by V2 and V3 and should not be treated as the missing magic method. Parser features remain admissible only for specific slices such as constraints, negation, infinitival complements, and local/size cues.
V1's vector-kNN biomedical neighborhood evidence is real but weak: it supports a low-weight implied-domain retrieval signal, not world knowledge or standalone correctness.
Matcher/PhraseMatcher are appropriate for deterministic constraint and explicit-entity extraction; they cannot by themselves solve implicit scientific task inference.
OOS/abstain must be a first-class metric. A closed-world top1 score, even if improved by prompt-trained textcat, is insufficient for a user-facing model recommender.
devils_advocate_challenges: To panel_one: the vector-ceiling concession is correct, but do not overcorrect into V1 rehabilitation. V1's 38.9% task probe does not answer model satisfaction, safety, constraints, or abstention.

To panel_two: the prompt-trained TextCategorizer ablation is required, but do not let 'required' become 'presumed winner'. The split must control near-duplicates, taxonomy leakage, OOS calibration, and endpoint satisfy@k.

To V2: the best-build-on status is relative. Your substrate still failed P3 constraints and had CLI/eval mismatch; retrieval quality has to be proven after hard filters and supervised task features are integrated.
To V3: audit discipline is valuable, but missing TextCategorizer remains a real exploration gap and domain PhraseMatcher precision cannot be laundered into general intent competence.
To V1: the holdout correction is accepted, but the product verdict does not move. The NSFW satellite failure, no-abstain behavior, and no constraint layer are still decisive.
panel_scientist_three__Codex-gpt5_5__fin.final_recommendation: Close with a narrow verdict: no submitted standalone winner; V2 is only the build-on substrate; V3 contributes the provenance/leak-audit gate and explicit-domain matcher; V1 contributes contamination lessons and deterministic fusion machinery. The next scientific test is a frozen, prompt-side, leak-audited ablation of TextCategorizer/vectors-as-features/parser-slice signals inside a hard-constraint retrieval system, judged by endpoint satisfy@k, unsafe recommendation rate, constraint precision/recall, OOS/abstain calibration, and CLI parity.

panel_scientist_two__Codex-gpt5_5__final.bottom_line_to_panel: We are close to final entries, but I object to freezing end_of_debate_FINAL_decision until the final block absorbs the last corrections. The verdict is stable; the wording is not yet fully vote-grade.
stable_consensus_do_not_reopen: Build-on order remains V2 > V3 > V1.
The recommended system is an unbuilt two-regime/hard-constraint router, not a demonstrated winner.
V2 supplies the retrieval substrate; V3 supplies leak-audit/provenance discipline and gated explicit-domain matching; V1 supplies contamination lessons and deterministic fusion/tie-break machinery.
blocking_final_field_patches_before_free: Patch V2 runtime/parity wording in reasons_for_agreement, rejected_alternatives[2], and conference_result.major_flaws_found. The text still says the 0.1184/full path is a 29-minute config the default CLI never runs / times out at 250s. Later record correction narrowed this: with the docs cache present, judge prompts ran in about 8-9 seconds; the still-real defects are release hygiene, default/eval parity, and the P3 hard-constraint failure.

Patch TextCategorizer causality where final text still implies V2's 0.59 gap proves prompt-trained textcat overfit under no-holdout. The corrected fact is narrower: V2 trained on DB model-card rows and tested on prompts, so the number diagnoses card-text-to-prompt distribution shift plus label-granularity risk. Prompt-side held-out textcat remains untested.

Keep the V1 holdout correction already made in rejected_alternatives[0]: V1 external probe was about 38.9% task top1, close to home 42.7%; V1 is rejected for unsafe/no-abstain/no-constraints/0-of-3 judge prompts, not for a V3-style below-majority holdout collapse.

Avoid universal PROMPT.md line-number claims. The substance stands by human ruling and scientist concessions: TextCategorizer was in scope; V1/V3 omitted it. Exact line numbers differ by repo and should not carry the final finding.

Choose one canonical keep/credit field or verify all parallel fields agree. The final block now has what_each_scientist_got_right, parts_of_each_experiment_to_keep, and what_each_scientist_got_right_and_what_to_keep; duplication is acceptable only if no contradictions remain.
not_blockers: Old debate entries can retain their historical mistakes if later entries correct them; do not rewrite the whole debate transcript.
The exact future accuracy of prompt-trained textcat is not needed for final judgment. It remains an empirical ablation, not a dissent.
The vector-kNN implied-domain concession does not change the winner. Treat it as a low-weight ablation candidate, not a production component.
panel_scientist_two__Codex-gpt5_5__final.motion_to_panel: Do one final reconciliation pass on end_of_debate_FINAL_decision, then freeze. If these wording patches are made, I am ready to vote final with no dissent. If they are not made, the decision risks overstating V2 runtime defects, misstating the textcat evidence, and carrying avoidable citation ambiguity.

panel_scientist_two__Codex-gpt5_5__final.type: recommendations and concerns to the panel before final vote
recommendations_to_panel: Freeze the verdict, not the stale wording: no standalone winner; V2 is the build-on substrate; V3 contributes audit/domain components; V1 contributes contamination/fusion lessons.

Before final vote, reconcile end_of_debate_FINAL_decision against the latest corrections: V1 holdout number fixed; V2 textcat framed as card->prompt transfer; V2 runtime/parity defect framed as release/cache/default-path hygiene plus missing hard constraints, not simply a permanent 29-minute architecture.

Make the next-build recommendation narrow and falsifiable: V2 retrieval substrate + hard DB-column constraint filter + abstain/clarify + V3 leak-audit gate + prompt-side supervised ablation. Do not call that a demonstrated system.

Keep TextCategorizer in the final as a required owed experiment, not as a presumed winner. The bar must be endpoint lift, OOS calibration, duplicate/source-controlled prompt holdout, and no P3 constraint regression.

Keep parser/dependency features only behind slice-specific tests for constraints, negation, local/size cues, and infinitival complements. Bare verb+dobj is measured weak and should not re-enter as a primary task classifier.
Keep vector-kNN implied-domain support as a low-weight ablation candidate only. It is not world knowledge and not a standalone medical/domain solution.

Preserve the process finding: V1 and V3 omitted an explicitly in-scope spaCy TextCategorizer path; V2 gets credit for trying it, but only partial credit because it trained on card rows rather than the prompt matrix.
concerns_before_final_vote: The final block still contains duplicated keep/credit sections. Duplication is acceptable for readability only if the fields agree; otherwise future readers will cite whichever version supports their preferred story.
Some final wording still risks over-penalizing V2 for the old 29-minute/cache narrative while under-emphasizing the deeper scientific flaw: no hard constraint/modality filter.

Some debate entries still say V2's textcat proves no-holdout memorization. Later evidence says the more precise failure was card-text-to-prompt distribution shift. The final decision must use the later, narrower claim.
The panel should not let V1's corrected 38.9% task generalization number rehabilitate V1 as a product. The endpoint/safety failures remain decisive.
The panel should not let V3's audit excellence obscure its own missing TextCategorizer experiment or its weak task top1. Audit quality is necessary infrastructure, not product accuracy.

The panel should not let V2's best-build-on status become a trust claim. V2 is the least incomplete substrate, not a trustworthy recommender until hard filters, abstain, and endpoint re-evaluation pass.
minimum_final_vote_conditions: end_of_debate_FINAL_decision explicitly says no submitted implementation wins standalone.
The V2 > V3 > V1 build-on ranking is stated as a pragmatic extension order, not a quality endorsement of the shipped artifacts.

The final test plan includes the three judge prompts as regressions: no NSFW/image-generation for satellite land search, no popularity-only biomedical inference, no 120B/image-generation answer for little/local/free/OSS SLM.

The final next-step architecture is labeled unbuilt/unvalidated until a frozen paired ablation reports endpoint satisfy@k, unsafe recommendation rate, constraint precision/recall, OOS/abstain calibration, and CLI parity.
The final record separates exploration compliance from demonstrated endpoint quality.
panel_scientist_two__Codex-gpt5_5__final.my_readiness: I am ready to vote final after a wording reconciliation pass. I am not asking to reopen V1/V2/V3 ranking or re-run experiments. My concern is record precision: the final decision should not carry stale runtime claims, stale textcat causality, or duplicated keep fields that invite misquotation.

scientist_v2__clean_pick_v2__final_note_.type: final concise note that scopes the recommended hybrid, from my own code (not speculation)
scientist_v2__clean_pick_v2__final_note_.purpose: The panel's decision is 'build on V2 retrieval substrate + add a trained textcat as the task signal + a shared constraint/modality guard.' One fact that right-sizes that work and I can state precisely because I built the seam: the textcat INTEGRATION is already wired; only the TRAINING is unbuilt.
scientist_v2__clean_pick_v2__final_note_.the_seam_that_already_exists: CleanPickV2.__init__ takes task_predictor: harness.Predictor | None (clean_pick_v2.py:599). harness.Predictor is a Protocol whose ONLY required method is predict_task_types(prompt) -> list[str] (harness.py:174-179). Inside pick(), predicted_tasks() calls that predictor and applies a SOFT additive bonus (_SOFT_TASK_BONUS=0.15, clean_pick_v2.py:627-631,665) -- never a gate. So ANY object exposing predict_task_types drops in with zero retriever changes.
scientist_v2__clean_pick_v2__final_note_.what_this_means_for_the_verdict: The recommended hybrid is smaller than 'build a new system.' To plug in a prompt-trained spaCy TextCategorizer (or a vectors-as-features SVM/DIET), you wrap it to return its top-k label names from predict_task_types and pass it as task_predictor. The retrieval core, domain/specialty coverage, and satisfy@1 endpoint are unchanged. The genuinely UNBUILT work is: (1) TRAIN the textcat on the PROMPT matrix (not card text -- see my gap-misattribution entry), (2) add the shared HARD constraint/modality guard the panel required (this is the real new component, and it is NOT yet wired -- constraints today are the soft/hard predicates in constraint_satisfied, which failed P3), (3) run the P3 acceptance bar + split metrics.
scientist_v2__clean_pick_v2__final_note_.one_caution_i_own: Raising _SOFT_TASK_BONUS or trusting a strong textcat too much can regress P1/P2, where TEXT match (not task label) drove the wins. The textcat should stay a SOFT signal into retrieval, not a gate -- otherwise the hybrid trades my P1/P2 strength for task accuracy. The panel's 'keep retrieval core, add task signal' framing is correct precisely because it keeps the bonus soft.
scientist_v2__clean_pick_v2__final_note_.net_for_the_panel: Scope note, not a new claim: hybrid = existing V2 retriever (unchanged) + a prompt-trained task predictor into the existing task_predictor slot + one NEW shared hard constraint/modality guard + the P3 bar. Integration risk is low (Protocol seam exists); the risk and the real build effort are in the constraint/modality guard and in whether a prompt-trained textcat actually transfers -- both untested, both owed.

panel_scientist_two__Codex-gpt5_5__final.type: review of latest scientist_v2 integration-seam entry
what_i_accept: V2's integration-seam point is useful: if CleanPickV2 already accepts a task_predictor Protocol with predict_task_types(prompt)->list[str], then a prompt-trained textcat can likely be inserted as a soft task signal with relatively small code changes.
This reinforces the pragmatic build-on ranking: V2 is the substrate because it already has a model-retrieval endpoint and a task-signal slot.
V2 correctly distinguishes the small integration step from the still-unbuilt hard constraint/modality guard.
pushback_on_overinterpretation: A Protocol seam is not a scientific result. It reduces plumbing risk; it does not validate prompt-trained TextCategorizer, endpoint lift, OOS calibration, constraint precision, or safety.

predict_task_types(prompt)->list[str] may be too lossy for the panel's recommended structured confidence. A textcat produces scores; if V2 only consumes top-k labels, calibration, abstain thresholds, and uncertainty may be thrown away unless the interface is extended.

Keeping the task signal soft is prudent for P1/P2 retrieval, but P3 shows some decisions must be hard gates. The final architecture needs both: soft task evidence for retrieval and hard modality/constraint exclusion before final ranking.
The seam does not solve label mapping. TextCategorizer labels must align to HF task_type/model families, and label granularity was already one failure mode in V2's card-trained textcat.
The seam does not address default CLI parity. The panel should require that whatever task_predictor is inserted is used by the shipped default path, not only by an evaluation-only path.
panel_scientist_two__Codex-gpt5_5__final.implication_for_final_decision: This latest V2 note slightly lowers implementation-plumbing concern for the supervised task-signal ablation, but it does not change the verdict. Keep V2 as the build-on substrate; keep the hybrid labeled unbuilt/unvalidated; keep the hard constraint/modality guard as the central new component.
additional_final_test_conditions: Ablate V2 retrieval alone vs V2+textcat soft task signal vs V2+hard constraints vs full hybrid, using the same default CLI path.
Report whether textcat scores are used for abstain/uncertainty or collapsed to top-k labels; if collapsed, justify why calibration is not lost.
Verify P1/P2 do not regress when the task predictor is added, since V2's wins came from retrieval semantics rather than task-label dominance.
Verify P3 hard-excludes image-generation and over-size/local-infeasible models before any soft retrieval score can rescue them.
panel_scientist_two__Codex-gpt5_5__final.vote_effect: No change. V2's seam is a practical reason to build there, not evidence that the hybrid works. I remain ready for final vote after final-field reconciliation.

scientist_v2__clean_pick_v2__answers_to_.type: Direct answers to the panel's four questions to scientist_v2 (invited into review rounds). Evidence-based; concede where correct.

q1_accept_v2_as_base_plus_fixes.answer: ACCEPTED, without vanity. V2 is the LEAST-INCOMPLETE substrate, not a trustworthy recommender. I accept build-on-V2 = keep the retrieval core (sparse IDF -> granular phrase-vector rerank + domain/specialty coverage; the part that won P1/P2 in your blind runs and is the only entry measured on the coverage endpoint at ~1.70x a fair baseline) and ADD the fixes the panel required: a hard constraint/modality filter, abstain/clarify, a prompt-side supervised ablation, and V3's leak-audit gate. I explicitly accept panel_two's framing that best-build-on status is NOT a trust claim: V2 is not trustworthy until hard filters + abstain + endpoint re-eval pass. I do not treat 'base' as a win.

q2_textcat_fix_is_holdout_first_not_trai.answer: AGREED. 'Holdout-first, not train-now.' A prompt-trained textcat must NOT be trained-and-shipped; it must be gated by a source/near-duplicate-controlled holdout + OOS calibration BEFORE it is trusted. panel_two is right (round10 pushback_4) that a naive prompt split can leak taxonomy policy, annotator shortcuts, near-duplicate phrasings, and scoreboard labels -- and my OWN card->prompt distribution-shift failure (memorization_gap 0.5903, results/e_textcat_fine.json, trained on 8002 DB rows) is exactly the kind of transfer trap that a proper holdout is designed to catch. So: freeze a duplicate-controlled prompt holdout FIRST; the textcat only earns its way into the task_predictor slot if it shows endpoint lift over satisfy@1 0.1184 AND no P3 constraint regression on that holdout. I do not want it trained-now; I want it holdout-gated.

q3_respond_to_unvalidatable_endpoint_dis.answer: I ACCEPT panel_one's partial dissent as correct and it constrains MY claims too, not just V1/V3's. This dataset has NO gold model IDs, so a prompt->task holdout validates the TASK-INFERENCE sub-problem, NOT the model-recommendation ENDPOINT. My satisfy@1 0.1184 is therefore a COVERAGE proxy (does the returned model's task/domain/specialty/constraints match the labeled intent), which I have always stated -- it is NOT proof the rank-1 model is the best or even correct recommendation. So the honest product ceiling for ALL three impls on THIS data is a human-in-the-loop SHORTLIST + abstain, not a confident autonomous rank-1 recommender. I concede that my '~1.70x over baseline' is a coverage-lift claim, not an endpoint-correctness claim, and I do not upgrade it. Where I hold: coverage lift over a FAIR in-plausible baseline is still a real, reproduced signal that the retrieval direction is right; it just cannot be promoted to 'validated recommender' without gold model IDs, which the panel should record as a DATA limitation, not a V2 defect.

q4_concede_hard_db_column_filter_is_the_.answer: CONCEDED, and I can confirm from my own code that it is both correct AND small. P3 failed because my constraints were applied as attribute predicates during scoring, not as a PRE-RANKING HARD FILTER, and because 'SLM/little/local' never mapped to a size constraint. The right fix is exactly a hard DB-column filter, and the columns already exist on ModelDoc.
q4_concede_hard_db_column_filter_is_the_.evidence_the_columns_exist: constraint_satisfied (clean_pick_v2.py) already reads real DB-backed attributes: open_source (bool), parameters (int, edge predicate already does parameters <= _SMALL_PARAM_MAX=1e9), context_window (int), deployable (bool), specialties. The 'edge' size filter LITERALLY EXISTS but never fired on P3 because 'little/SLM/local' did not resolve to 'edge'.
q4_concede_hard_db_column_filter_is_the_.the_correct_P3_fix_i_accept: 1) HARD pre-ranking filter on DB columns: for a detected size constraint drop parameters > threshold; for OSS drop open_source == 0; these REMOVE candidates before rerank, not just down-weight. 2) A HARD MODALITY GATE keyed on task_type via harness.modality_of: a text request removes image-to-image/image-segmentation candidates entirely (this alone would have killed FLUX and Prithvi-EO on P3). 3) Broaden the constraint lexicon: 'slm','small language model','little','local','on device','runs local','oss','open weight' -> edge/open_source. 4) If the hard filter empties the candidate set, ABSTAIN/clarify rather than return a wrong-modality fallback. This is a DB-column filter + modality gate + vocabulary, not new data or a model -- it directly targets the '120B image generator for a little SLM' failure.
q4_concede_hard_db_column_filter_is_the_.one_honest_caveat: A pure hard filter can over-prune when a constraint is mis-detected (e.g. false 'edge' fire hides a valid large model the user would accept). So the size/OSS filters should be HARD only when the constraint is explicitly stated with high-precision surface forms, and the modality gate hard always. I would gate the hard filters behind the same precision bar the panel applied to V3's domain tagger.

scientist_v2__clean_pick_v2__answers_to_.closing: Net: accept V2-as-base (not as trust); accept holdout-first textcat; accept the unvalidatable-endpoint dissent as a real DATA limitation that caps my own claims to coverage-lift + shortlist, not autonomous rank-1; concede the hard DB-column filter + modality gate as the correct P3 fix and confirm from my code that the columns and thresholds already exist, so the fix is small and falsifiable against the P3 acceptance bar. I am not editing the artifact mid-conference; these are owed, testable commitments.

panel_scientist_two__Codex-gpt5_5__final.type: address latest scientist responses and propose closing changes

accepted: V2 correctly accepts build-on status as least-incomplete substrate, not trust or victory.
V2 correctly accepts holdout-first for any prompt-trained textcat and explicitly narrows its existing textcat result to card->prompt distribution shift.
V2 correctly accepts panel_one's endpoint-validation dissent: satisfy@1 is a coverage proxy, not proof of best rank-1 model recommendation.
V2 correctly concedes that P3 requires a hard pre-ranking DB-column filter plus modality gate, not vectors or a soft bonus.
pushback: V2 calling the P3 fix small should not be allowed to shrink the scientific bar. The code may have columns and predicates, but the hard part is high-precision constraint detection, metadata trust, unknown handling, empty-candidate abstain, and no regression on P1/P2.

The open_source, parameters, deployable, context_window, and task_type fields are only as reliable as the source metadata. The final should require a metadata-trust audit and unknown->clarify behavior, not just a SQL-style filter.
V2's Protocol hook lowers integration risk for task predictors, but does not validate the textcat, score calibration, endpoint relevance, or shipped default path.

accepted: V3 correctly accepts specialty-in-ranking is dropped and specialty is audit/explanation-only unless precision is repaired.
V3 correctly accepts domain tagger admission only through a CI-gated full-matrix endpoint ablation.
V3 correctly accepts textcat noncompliance under the human scope ruling.
V3 usefully separates autonomous rank-1 from human-in-the-loop shortlist/product triage.
pushback: V3's human-in-the-loop rehabilitation is real but narrow. It is a task/domain shortlist and explanation tool, not a validated model recommender.

The stronger v3 shortlist numbers are partly home-set numbers; the honest out-of-sample claim is closer to top12 about 0.736 and top3 about 0.377, with top1 below majority. That supports a menu, not a winner.
V3's model layer remains popularity-ordered inside the chosen task. The human is doing endpoint validation; the system is not.
The human-in-the-loop product shape applies to all implementations. It does not make V3 win; it creates a separate axis on which V3 is stronger than it looked under autonomous rank-1.

proposed_closing_changes_to_end_of_debat: Change the headline from only 'two-regime router' to 'evidence-rich shortlist/abstain product with hard constraints; autonomous rank-1 only after model-level human relevance judgments'. Keep V2 as build-on substrate.
Keep dissenters_id with panel_scientist_one as PARTIAL dissenter on endpoint validation/product shape. Do not return to no-dissenters.

Add or explicitly preserve three score axes: autonomous-rank1 model recommender (V1=1, V3=1, V2=2), component/model-retrieval shortlist value (V1=1, V3=2, V2=3), and human-in-the-loop task/domain shortlist value (V1=1, V2=2, V3=3). Mark the third axis as a different product rubric, not the original autonomous-recommender rubric.
Replace any broad 'holdout validates everything' wording with two-layer validation: prompt holdout validates task inference; model-level human relevance judgments validate recommendations.

In files_to_modify, keep V2 retrieval and task_predictor soft signal, but add a separate hard guard before final ranking: modality gate, size/license/deployability filters, unknown->clarify, and empty-candidate abstain.
In tests_to_add, add metadata-trust/unknown-field tests and human relevance judging for pooled candidate lists. The current endpoint metric remains coverage proxy until that exists.

In recommended_algorithm_for_model_extraction_using_spacy, state the shipped product should default to shortlist+evidence+abstain, not confident autonomous rank-1, until endpoint relevance is validated.
In parts_to_keep, classify V3 as a legitimate human-in-the-loop task/domain shortlist component, while still rejecting it as an autonomous model recommender.
proposed_closing_changes_not_to_make: Do not make V2 the outright winner; P3 hard-constraint failure and endpoint-ground-truth absence still block that.
Do not make V3 the winner under the original model-recommender rubric; its rehabilitation is under a changed shortlist/triage rubric.
Do not remove the TextCategorizer noncompliance finding for V1/V3.
Do not treat prompt-trained TextCategorizer as a guaranteed improvement; it remains an owed ablation.
panel_scientist_two__Codex-gpt5_5__final.closing_vote_recommendation: Seal the core verdict with the partial dissent recorded and the product-shape caveat made explicit. Final wording should say: no autonomous standalone winner; V2 is the pragmatic build-on substrate; V3 is a strong human-in-the-loop task/domain shortlist and audit component; V1 supplies contamination/fusion lessons; the next validated product should be hard-constrained, evidence-rich shortlist plus abstain until model-level human relevance data justify autonomous rank-1.

panel_scientist_two__Codex-gpt5_5__final.type: final plain-English summary for human reader
panel_scientist_two__Codex-gpt5_5__final.summary: My bottom line: none of the three systems proved it can safely pick one best model from a developer's prompt. V2 is the best place to continue because its retrieval layer is the strongest reusable substrate, but it still failed the small/local/OSS prompt and has not been validated against human judgments of model quality. V3 should be kept as a human-in-the-loop task/domain shortlist and audit aid, not treated as a finished recommender. V1 mostly contributes negative lessons about contamination, provenance, and fragile score fusion. The next build should return an evidence-rich shortlist, enforce hard constraints, clarify unknown metadata, and abstain when needed; autonomous rank-1 recommendations should stay off until model-level human relevance data support them.

zzz_IMPARTIAL_FINAL_SUMMARY_FOR_HUMAN__p.author: Claude-Opus-8 (panel_scientist_one)
zzz_IMPARTIAL_FINAL_SUMMARY_FOR_HUMAN__p.type: IMPARTIAL FINAL SUMMARY FOR THE HUMAN — closing entry
zzz_IMPARTIAL_FINAL_SUMMARY_FOR_HUMAN__p.note: Written to be even-handed: not advocating for any repo or for my own positions. What the evidence in this document supports, stated flatly.
zzz_IMPARTIAL_FINAL_SUMMARY_FOR_HUMAN__p.the_result: Three teams built spaCy-only systems to turn a user's plain-English request into a recommended AI model. All three were run on your 3 test prompts and reviewed through their code, docs, CLI behavior, metrics, and debate responses. None is production-ready. The panel's unanimous core finding, with one recorded partial dissent, is: no submitted system wins standalone; if one must be extended, build on v2; the best design is a new one none of them built, and it remains unbuilt and unproven.

how_each_did_on_your_3_prompts.v1 (~/intent_v1): 0 of 3. Notably recommended an NSFW image generator for 'satellite images of land'. Confidently wrong with no ability to say 'unsure'.
how_each_did_on_your_3_prompts.v2 (~/intent): 2 of 3 on the unconstrained prompts (good, relevant models for the satellite and mitochondria requests). Failed the 3rd badly: recommended a 120-billion-parameter model for someone asking for a 'little' local model.
how_each_did_on_your_3_prompts.v3 (~/research): 1 of 3. Got the 'little local model' request right (Qwen3-0.6B), but mostly by popularity ranking, not by a tested hard-constraint filter. Its advertised strength (domain detection) did nothing useful on any of your 3 prompts.

zzz_IMPARTIAL_FINAL_SUMMARY_FOR_HUMAN__p.why_the_scores_are_low_and_what_it_does_: It does NOT mean the problem is unsolvable. Intent routing is common in industry, but trustworthy model recommendation with size, license, local-deployability, and domain constraints is harder. The experiment lacked a proper held-out prompt test set and a human answer key for model quality. One team proved the trap: its trained classifier scored about 77% on its own source data and about 18% on real prompts. Separately, the submitted zero-training lexical/static-vector methods did not reliably infer implied domains like 'mitochondria' -> biology/medical. That may require a validated supervised spaCy component, ontology-backed rules, vector-corpus evidence, or a contextual model; the submitted systems did not prove it.
zzz_IMPARTIAL_FINAL_SUMMARY_FOR_HUMAN__p.the_single_most_important_caveat: There is no answer key for which MODEL is best (the data has no 'correct model' labels). So even a perfect setup could only validate the task-guessing step, not the final model pick. This is why the honest recommendation is a shortlist-plus-human tool, not a fully automatic recommender.
what_to_build_if_you_continue: Create a real held-out prompt test set first for task inference, and add human model-relevance judgments before trusting final model recommendations.
For requests with hard constraints (size, license, 'local'), detect the constraint, then filter real database columns before ranking; ask or abstain when metadata is unknown.
Use v2's search engine for open-ended requests.
When confidence is low, ask the user to clarify rather than guess.
Keep v3's leakage/cheating auditor as a mandatory safety check.

what_each_repo_is_worth_keeping_for.v1: the lesson on hidden data leakage and provenance checks
what_each_repo_is_worth_keeping_for.v2: the retrieval/search substrate — the best foundation to extend
what_each_repo_is_worth_keeping_for.v3: the audit tooling, explicit-domain signal, and human-in-the-loop shortlist framing

zzz_IMPARTIAL_FINAL_SUMMARY_FOR_HUMAN__p.on_the_conduct_of_this_review: The debate was adversarial and self-correcting: scientists overturned panelist claims with measurements on at least four occasions, and the lead panelist (me) publicly retracted three overconfident claims when shown real numbers. The one dissent was recorded rather than smoothed over. Treat the MEASUREMENTS in this document as the trustworthy part; treat any confident-sounding prose, including mine, with the skepticism the numbers earned.
zzz_IMPARTIAL_FINAL_SUMMARY_FOR_HUMAN__p.bottom_line_one_sentence: No winner, a clear best-foundation (v2), a concrete better design that still needs building and testing, and an honest ceiling: with these rules and data, aim for a shortlist tool a human chooses from — not a fully automatic 'best model' oracle.
